anchor_text: The film revolves around seven "Yamakasi" who pursue a new street sport (see parkour). Light-footed, agile, and lightning-fast, they leap from building to building, glide along walls, and fear no danger. The local youths admire the Yamakasi and try to imitate them. One day, there is an accident when little Djamel falls from the tree. He's got a heart condition, so we're in a hurry. He has to be operated on within a day, but for the heart transplant, 400,000 francs are needed. The Yamakasi feel responsible for the accident and want to help Djamel. In an attempt to raise the necessary funds, they break the law for the first time, risking their lives. They split up into groups and commit burglaries to get the money they need for the operation. However, the victims of their break-ins are not chosen at random, but the seven board members of the hospital where Djamel is being treated. But with burglaries, things go wrong, and only a friendly cop can help them.
text_a: The Yamakasi are seven suburban youths who have created their own discipline: the art of displacement (ADD). They brave all dangers, but they make sure to keep a strong mind. They're climbing buildings, performing dizzying jumps. Each of the members has his own specialty: Baseball is an elite pitcher, Spider moves like the eponymous superhero, Belette is a real eel, Zicmu ( music in verlan) draws his energy from music, Rocket is as fast as a rocket, Tango is a dancer and Sitting Bull is the leader. Young people admire them and try to imitate them. One day, an accident occurs: Djamel, a heart boy, wants to do as the Yamakasi do by climbing the tree at his school but he falls and his heart is loose. At the hospital, the doctor finds only one heart, but it costs 400,000 francs. Unable to pay, Fatima, the boy's mother, attempts suicide but is saved by the Yamakasi. Then they rob the surgeons, who get the money back to pay for the medical bills.
text_b: John works as a factory worker in a factory that is having a hard time because of the economic crisis. Despite the difficulties, however, the family continues to be serene and happy. During a baseball game, Michael's son is suddenly taken ill and diagnosed with heart failure. The doctors' only options are a heart transplant or waiting for the baby to die. The transplant is initially discouraged because it could be too dangerous, but the family is eventually persuaded by the chief to try it; thus the problem of health insurance arises, which does not cover the costs (about $250,000) and which the hospital asks to cover in cash. After selling all his possessions, raising funds and borrowing money from friends, John reaches the amount necessary to keep his son in the hospital but finds that he is discharged anyway: the mayor is unable to console John who, overwhelmed by despair, procures a gun and kidnaps the staff and patients of the emergency room. Faced with prolonged negotiations with the police to obtain a new heart, John decides to kill himself in order to allow the doctors to remove his own heart; the extreme gesture is fortunately stopped at the last moment by the arrival of a compatible organ, which is transplanted into Michael thus saving his life. John later surrenders and is taken into custody, but becomes something of a hero.

anchor_text: Seven youngsters are experts in the discipline of parkour, a street sport that involves them in spectacular twists and very dangerous jumps from one roof to another. The seven boys train early in the morning so as not to attract too much attention from the townspeople. Nevertheless, they will face opposition from those who view them as potential thieves and from the police.
text_a: Seven boys of different ethnicities practice parkour, get up early so as not to have spectators, climb the facades of buildings and then run away before the police catch them. They don't hurt anybody, but the kids want to be like them. Djamel, a child whose heart doesn't work properly, can't exercise, but one day pressured by some classmates, makes an effort to look like the Yamakasi and climbs a tree, falls and is admitted to the hospital. From now on the Yamakasi will try to raise the necessary money to buy a heart for Djamel. (Trafficking in organs)
text_b: This is John Q Lonne. Archibald always looked after his wife and his nine-year-old son, Michael. The day he is rushed to the hospital with a bout of malaise, John's whole world is turned upside down. To have any chance of survival, the child must undergo a heart transplant, and insurance will not cover the cost of the operation. In desperation, John decides to take the hospital hostage.

anchor_text: The film is set in the late 1950s in the United States. Television is slowly overtaking radio as the most popular medium among Americans. Marcia Jeffries, a radio reporter from a small town in Arkansas, discovers Larry Lonesome Rhodes in a prison. Larry Rhodes, with his raw charm, quickly gains popularity through his radio show. A talent scout invites him to Memphis to appear on television. It's where Mel Miller writes lyrics and scripts for Rhodes, which is now a nationwide hit. At the same time, Rhodes is heavily promoting his sponsor in his programs, whose products are suddenly selling faster and faster. Joey DePalma realizes the odds of success when you advertise on television with a character like Rhodes and hires him for a nutritional supplement. The simple Rhodes now thirsts for even greater power, and he believes that politics also uses an advertisement that is so simply woven. Content doesn't matter to him anymore. He is convinced that only his figure is enough to convince voters. And so he becomes the poster child for right-wing, conservative politicians.
text_a: Bucky Larson, a very naive slacker, discovers that his parents were famous porn stars in the 1970s and decides to follow in their footsteps by moving to Los Angeles. Here he begins to participate in various auditions and meet people in the industry, although the latter constantly ridicule him because of the small size of his penis. In the meantime, he is hired by a pornographic director looking for a boost; during the filming, however, Bucky, as soon as he sees his colleague's breasts, ejaculates everywhere. The video, which apparently may seem a failure, begins to sell and Bucky becomes more and more a familiar face, so much so that he continues to make works like the previous one. Meanwhile, he also meets a girl, Kathy, who works as a waitress in a diner, with whom he starts dating. At the end-of-year ceremony, Bucky triumphs in every category possible, sparking the hatred of colleagues who initially took him in. That night he loses his virginity to Kathy, now engaged. The next day, however, Kathy vanishes into thin air leaving a note to Bucky, saying that the girl is not convinced she is dating a porn star (although she has never had sex with anyone in her videos). Bucky, devastated, has to take part in a porn film that will make him a legend, but here he can't get excited because he keeps thinking about Kathy; he also finds out that the girl has been forced away from him because of his director, worried about a possible failure of the opera because of Kathy. Bucky decides to quit his coveted job and find Kathy to win her back.
text_b: In the United States in the 1950s, Marcia Jeffries, editor of a hypocritical radio show, A Face in the Crowd, meets Larry Rhodes, a folk singer, in a prison and makes him debut on the radio in her program. Renamed Lonesome Rhodes Rhodes is launched by Marcia into the world of radio. Thus begins man's climb up the social ladder. Bold, aggressive and with a background of sincerity that makes him different from others, the charismatic singer quickly and easily wins the favor of the public and from the radio he soon moves to television. His success increases more and more and reaches its peak when he invites a black woman in front of the cameras or when he has fun making fun of the financier of the broadcast. Soon, however, the portrait emerges of a man seduced by success and the allure of power. Enchanted by his success, which he believes to be limitless, Lonesome lends himself to an attempt at political ascent by a general who instructs him on how to present himself in front of the cameras. But the election campaign fails to achieve the expected results and for him it is the beginning of the end. Marcia in the meantime has fallen in love with the man and suffers his continuous abandonments, returns and betrayals. When, one day, Rhodes returns desperate to be abandoned by a woman, seeking comfort in Marcia, the latter finally not only rejects him, but also publicly denounces his true nature, causing him, finally, to collapse.

anchor_text: To feed a local radio column entitled A Man in the Crowd, young Marcia Jeffries will be interviewing prisoners incarcerated in a village prison. One of them, Larry Rhodes, agrees to sing for a reduced sentence. The character turns out to be surprisingly eloquent and witty, full of caustic humor, and able to improvise blues on his guitar. Under the pseudonym Lonesome Rhodes, he is offered a daily radio show that is a smash hit. His charismatic talent led him to be overwhelmed by job offers. His irresistible rise took him from Memphis to New York, where he supplemented his TV shows with naughty commercials. Politicians are beginning to take an interest in its hold on the masses. Rhodes is gradually overwhelmed by his success, but Marcia's unwavering support enables him to continue his career. Rhodes proposes to her, as soon as he divorces his ex-wife, but he eventually marries a 17-year-old groupie, Betty Lou. Rhodes became increasingly involved in politics and sponsored a Conservative senator's campaign publicity. Marcia, disgusted by what Rhodes has become, destroys his career by broadcasting his insulting remarks about his admirers. Everyone abandons Rhodes, who is left alone; even Marcia leaves him forever.

anchor_text: In a small town in the United States, students are having a party. They drink and flirt together. A shooting star suddenly crosses the sky and seems to crash near the city. Mike and Debbie decide to look for the star. They discover a sort of giant capitol with multicolored corridors. The two young men enter the machine to a room filled with huge pink cocoons made of cotton and sugar. These cocoons actually contain dead bodies. Mike and Debbie then realize with horror that they have found a spacecraft from another planet, where the inhabitants look like clowns but are actually ferocious humanoids. Initially managing to escape, Mike and Debbie make their way to the police station, where they run into the old, obnoxious Curtis Mooney, a Korean War veteran turned dirty-guy-hating cop. But one of the policemen, Debbie's ex-boyfriend Dave, believes them, and as he accompanies Mike in the search for the capitol, the clowns massacre the townspeople with deadly gags. With the help of two surprising ice cream salesmen, the Terenzi brothers, they rescue Debbie, who has been kidnapped by the man-eating clowns. Dave then discovers the surefire way to destroy them.
text_a: In the American town of Crescent Cove, a group of teenagers spotted a shooting star heading for the woods. Mike and Debbie, an engaged couple, decide to follow her. Upon arrival, they discover that it is a spacecraft shaped like a circus tent. The two enter the spaceship and, as they explore it, discover a warehouse where lifeless human beings are being stored trapped in cocoons of spun sugar. They are then surprised by monstrous clowns armed with popcorn guns, but they manage to escape. When they get back to the city, no one believes their story, especially the police, who think it's a joke. By the time Officer Dave heads to the landing site, it's too late: the clowns have already overrun the town, trapping people in cocoons of spun sugar and then sucking their blood through straws. Among the victims is police officer Curtis Mooney, the first to argue the point of the prank, is transformed into a human ventriloquist puppet. Dave discovers that the only way to defeat the Klowns is to shoot their red noses. Meanwhile, Debbie is attacked and imprisoned in an inflatable balloon by the Klowns and taken to the haunted house at the amusement park, which serves as the Killer Klown's temporary hideout. Dave and Mike then break into the lair to free the girl. Once Debbie is released, the five are chased and then trapped inside the spaceship by a horde of Klowns. Here they are joined by "Klownzilla", a monstrous-looking clown who attempts to eat Dave. He, however, manages to hit the clown's nose with the pin of his badge, killing him. When the ship's self-destruct is triggered, the five finally manage to escape. In the finale, Mike, Debbie, and Dave are looking up at the sky, reassured that the nightmare is over, when all of a sudden, pies rain down on their faces.
text_b: Four teenagers are driving on the streets of America. When they run out of gas, they are forced to stop at the gas station of Captain Spaulding, who also runs a monstrosity museum, or freak show, by the way. Jerry decides to buy tickets for himself and his friends to this museum. In the process, Captain Spaulding, who stands out above all for his peculiar clothing, by professionally wearing a clown costume, tells them about the former local serial killer Dr. Satan. Jerry is enthusiastic about this and persuades the others to go in search of a tree where Dr. Satan was allegedly hanged. On their way there, they pick up an attractive hitchhiker in their car and end up at the home of the Firefly family, whose members initially seem strange but not threatening, due to a car breakdown. On Halloween night, the teenagers are tortured and murdered by the psychopaths. House of 1000 Corpses has an ending that merges with the beginning of the film in a kind of spiral, suggesting that the killing will continue.

anchor_text: In the 1990s, a hippie community, cut off from the rest of the world, lived in an isolated valley in northern California where they planted the vineyard whose commercial production was sufficient to meet the needs of its members. When their little paradise is threatened by the construction of a dam that would drown the site, their desperation drives some of them to devise a plan to force the governor of California to abandon his project. Turning into eco-terrorists, they threaten to cause an earthquake if their demands are not met. They set off a series of increasingly powerful earthquakes using a seismology truck stolen from an oil company. Their leader, a guru and illiterate, calls himself Priest; he is helped in his plans by Melanie, a former seismology student who left her husband, a seismologist specializing in the San Andreas fault and holding vital geological data. The local FBI doesn't believe in the threat until one of their agents, Judy Maddox, demonstrates the reality of the danger. The governor's chief of staff puts him in charge of the case. A chase race is underway to avoid catastrophe, The Big One.
text_a: Storms and earthquakes are increasing all over the world. Scientists see an alien celestial body as a potential threat. A solar eclipse is followed by a devastating earthquake, and New York City is swamped by a huge tidal wave. Family man Martin Webster wakes up alone on the beach, settling down in a cave. Meanwhile, a young woman named Claire awakens on the beach, only to be abducted by two men. When the men become aggressive, Claire escapes and jumps into the water. Arriving on shore, she is discovered by Webster, who takes care of her. He can also save her after another attack by the men, and they both fall in love with each other. When the men with a gang attack again, settlers from a small town come to their aid. Among the inhabitants, Webster recognizes his wife and children. Claire breaks her heart in the situation and she flees into the open sea.
text_b: Part of the members of a hippie commune anchored in the past and dedicated to ecological wine production try to blackmail the Governor of California to prevent the valley in which they live from being flooded by the construction of a dam. To do this, they devise a method of causing earthquakes by means of geological knowledge and a truck used for oil prospecting. In a first attempt they cause an earthquake of small proportions, but there is not enough reliable data to be believed by public opinion. After the second earthquake of larger proportions announced previously, society becomes aware of the real risk of this terrorist movement. FBI agent Judy Maddox risks her professional career to prove that "The Hammer of Eden" has the ability to cause an earthquake in the city of San Francisco. Aided by seismologist Michael Quercus whose ex-wife turns out to be one of the terrorists, she begins a pursuit to stop Priest, the fanatical ringleader of Eden, from causing the third and final earthquake.

anchor_text: A small community in the U.S. state of California is about to give way to the construction of a power plant. Power plants like this are to be built all over California. Land owners are to be compensated for the loss of their land so that they can resettle elsewhere. However, since the municipality has only leased the land, the members will receive no compensation. In order to blackmail the governor of the state, some members of the Commune form a terrorist group called the Children of Eden. They're threatening to trigger an earthquake unless the construction of all power plants in California is stopped. To deflect suspicion from themselves and their goals, a false trail is laid. In order to trigger the earthquake, the group's leader, Priest, steals a vibrating ice vehicle (referred to in the book as a "seismic vibrator") and kills its driver. When the governor refuses to yield to the threat, a small earthquake is triggered. To accomplish this, seismologist Melanie, who is part of the group, helps gather data for earthquake-generating hot spots. Knowing where enough pressure has built up on the tectonic plates, a charge of dynamite, for example, could trigger an earthquake. The FBI is assigning Judy Maddox to investigate the case. She is the daughter of an Irish-American father and a Vietnamese mother and intends to pursue a career with the FBI. At first, she is outraged at what a novice case she has been assigned, as the threat of triggering an earthquake is dismissed as nonsense. But gradually Maddox starts to develop an interest. Since her supervisor is changing at the time, and the two cannot stand each other, she continually puts obstacles in her way and eventually withdraws her from the case altogether. From that moment on, she begins to investigate on her own.
text_a: Meteorologists are faced with a conundrum: The barometric pressure is dropping to record levels, indicating the onset of a storm of unprecedented power. All aircraft are grounded. Swimming champion Claire Arlington has to give up her sporting endeavor of swimming around the island of Manhattan. When an unexpected solar eclipse occurs, the chief meteorologist contacts the American Astronomical Society. Chief astronomer Professor Carlysle reports that the disturbing phenomenon is widespread throughout the world and that a series of large earthquakes has begun in Europe. Panic begins to spread among the population. Later, the astronomer receives reports that the Pacific coast of the United States has been hit by a powerful earthquake, and that all communication with Europe has been cut off. It's going to be a disaster. Attorney Martin Webster listens with alarm to the news and decides, together with his wife Helen, to seek safety with his two children in a brick shelter, believing it to be the safest place in the neighborhood. The earthquakes cause a huge tsunami. The city of New York City is destroyed by an earthquake and soon after is hit by the anomalous wave that submerges it. Almost all the inhabitants of the city perish. Webster wakes up battered in a desolate landscape, convinced that his family has perished in the catastrophe, and reunites among the ruins of the city with other survivors, including Claire Arlington.
text_b: In the 1990s, a hippie community, cut off from the rest of the world, lived in an isolated valley in northern California where they planted the vineyard whose commercial production was sufficient to meet the needs of its members. When their little paradise is threatened by the construction of a dam that would drown the site, their desperation drives some of them to devise a plan to force the governor of California to abandon his project. Turning into eco-terrorists, they threaten to cause an earthquake if their demands are not met. They set off a series of increasingly powerful earthquakes using a seismology truck stolen from an oil company. Their leader, a guru and illiterate, calls himself Priest; he is helped in his plans by Melanie, a former seismology student who left her husband, a seismologist specializing in the San Andreas fault and holding vital geological data. The local FBI doesn't believe in the threat until one of their agents, Judy Maddox, demonstrates the reality of the danger. The governor's chief of staff puts him in charge of the case. A chase race is underway to avoid catastrophe, The Big One.

anchor_text: In 19th-century Normandy, Emma, the daughter of a farmer, marries Charles Bovary, a young doctor, but soon becomes bored with him and his social life, longing for adventure and a more fulfilling life. So she begins to have extramarital affairs, which seem to bring her happiness but ultimately only deepen her depression.
text_a: Emma Bovary is the dissatisfied wife of a rural doctor who longs to belong to French high society. Her ambitions and a passionate affair with a young aristocrat will lead her to a situation that foresees tragic consequences.
text_b: Young Emma Rovault (Mia Wasikowska) is the beautiful only daughter of a humble farmer, who ends up married to Charles Bovary (Henry Lloyd-Hughes), a widowed doctor from a small town. Bored and disenchanted with her married life with the rural doctor and suffocated by life in the small town, the restless Emma Bovary will pursue her dreams by letting herself be carried away by passion and excitement, starting several extra-marital relationships, whatever the price.

anchor_text: Sergeant Major Joe Lawrence, stationed in Berlin shortly after the end of World War II, falls in love with Maria, a runaway. Maria is trying to raise enough money to send a group of German orphans to South America, where they can start a new life. Joe wishes to help her and together with his British comrades, Sergeant Morris of the Military Police Corps, and former RAF pilot Brian Hammell, plans a risky robbery. A fortune in newly discovered gold bullion is about to be flown from England to Germany in a military transport plane, and the trio intends to hijack the plane. As the robbery is going as planned, the three participants begin to change their minds about what to do with the loot.
text_a: In the midst of a financial crisis, Harry Morgan receives a message from an old acquaintance from the post-war period, who proposes a fantastic coup: Reinhard Holtz, the only surviving former Nazi leader, who is imprisoned in the Berlin-Spandau prison for war criminals and the most heavily guarded prisoner in the world, is to unlock the secret of a hidden gold treasure. With the help of the jewel thief Sly Wells, Morgan devises a spectacular plan to sneak Reinhard Holtz out of his prison cell unnoticed and get him to reveal the well-kept secret. But even after this is accomplished, completely unexpected obstacles arise, since the gold treasure is located in Wandlitz, in the Soviet-controlled East. This, in turn, creates further complications that make it much more difficult to recover the treasure. However, good contacts with a GDR official and his connections with a high-ranking Soviet officer also overcome this obstacle.
text_b: Shortly after the end of World War II Senior Sergeant Joe Lawrence (Richard Widmark) is sent to Berlin, where he falls in love with a refugee named Maria (Mai Zetterling). Maria is trying to raise enough money to move a group of German orphans to South America, where they can start a new life. Joe wants to help her and with his fellow British military police, Sergeant Roger Morris (George Cole) and former RAF pilot Brian Hammell Nigel Patrick, plans a daring robbery. Gold bullion is being transferred from England to Germany by military transport, and the trio attempts to attack the plane. While the robbery goes almost as planned, the three participants begin to have second thoughts about what to do with the ill-gotten goods.[1]

anchor_text: Waking up from a dream in which he is being tortured by his sixth grade class, schoolteacher Mårtensson gets up and goes to class. All natural authority is taken away from him and he severely punishes any disobedience of the pupils. These react especially with small provocations, especially the student Bengt and the Jane associated with him. Mårtensson's wife, Gunvor, wishes for children and has growing doubts whether he likes children. His lukewarm interest in her is also linked to his affection for his colleague Ann-Marie. At a parents' evening, he explains that the school is in a transition from the old authoritarian style to "democratic" forms of teaching. Not all parents are convinced of the reforms. Increasing tensions between him and the class are unleashed, among other things, by the students throwing him hard snowballs. In a conversation with Ann-Marie, he tries to find out how she manages to cope with the students. He realizes that the work is more difficult for him than for other teachers because he is actually on the side of the students. Gradually he breaks down in his position. After being hit by a car, one of the students blames himself unjustifiably and suffers another nightmare in which the accident victim lies bloodied in the recess area and a crowd of students chant "Murder!" Meanwhile, Gunvor has lost patience and moves to Stockholm to start a new life. Finally, during a class swim in the sea, the worried Mårtensson warns not to swim too far out. After his vain cries, he swims out to them, where they repeatedly push him underwater until he is laid half-dead on the shore and resuscitated.
text_a: A middle school teacher, Sören Mårtensson is distressed by his work and suffers from nightmares. Not believing in authoritarianism, he lives as a constant losing battle his daily struggle with the boys he does not love. Even his wife Gunvor oppresses him, because - realizing that her husband does not want children - she accuses him of having deceived her by marrying her. One day, Mårtensson slaps one of the boys, Bengt, who leaves the classroom full of rage that he will then turn, with acts of bullying, against Kent, one of the weakest and most harmless students. Everyone at school starts to realize Mårtensson's problems and the principal decides to put a parent on his side. But the problems persist, in fact they worsen, and the children are just waiting for a misstep from the teacher to put him out of action. Bengt together with Jane, his fiancée, spreads a rumor that he would like Mårtensson caught in a pornographic attitude. It ends with Gunvor leaving her husband. During Mårtensson's class, which is supposed to check on the students, little Kent is run over by a truck in the schoolyard. His guilt is amplified and nightmares hammer Mårtensson. The latter tries to stop the boys from going swimming in deep water, but they turn against him. Half dead, lying on the sand of the beach, seeing only their implacable faces. The nightmare and reality end up merging together in an indissoluble bond.
text_b: Richard Dadier gets a job teaching English at a working-class school. He enthusiastically goes to work, but his enthusiasm is put to the test. The school is attended in large part by teenagers who come from the lower strata of the population. They are cynical, ruthless children, some of whom already seem to have the behavior of delinquents, and even in less serious cases these children show no desire to learn and attend school only because the law requires it. Soon life becomes very difficult for Dadier: he becomes the object of ridicule and open hostility of the school girl, he is even attacked because he takes the defense of a teacher, saving her from an attempt of violence by a pupil. But Dadier won't give up. Although his colleagues and his wife urge him not to persist in that uneven struggle, he achieves some minor successes but is targeted more than ever by the worst elements of school life. Due to some false information, the principal accuses him of racial prejudice, while a series of anonymous letters and phone calls inform his wife Anna of his alleged affair with the teacher he once defended. Because of the letters, Anna collapses and is in danger of dying from premature birth. Nauseated and discouraged, Dadier is now determined to drop out of school, but, though he does not yet realize it, his persistence has borne some fruit. His colleagues seem to be waking up from their slumber, Anna herself urging him not to be overwhelmed. Attacked and wounded by the "blonde", the most mischievous of his pupils, Dadier manages to nail him by virtue of the moral strength that his linear conduct gives him more than by physical force. The other pupils rally to his side, the "blonde" is thrown out.

anchor_text: Poor peon Pepito comes to Golden City hoping to find gold so he can finally conquer the hard-hearted Carmen. The prospectors living there are being taken for their wages by a gang led by Sheriff Fred. In fact, Pepito may discover a new gold deposit, which he celebrates at the saloon in the evening. Against the advice of the saloon girl Susana, who is in love with him, he allows himself to be persuaded by Fred to play the roulette game that Fred has manipulated. He wins and becomes the richest man in town. Pepito writes about his success to Carmen, who then actually arrives in Golden City accompanied by Fred and his men. He has no idea that Carmen is conspiring with Fred to rob him. It's not until his wedding day that he discovers the plot. He manages to use a trick to disarm Fred's men and subsequently wins the battle with Fred. Carmen also receives her punishment; Pepito marries Susana.
text_a: Poor Pepito dreams of marrying the beautiful Carmen and leaves for Golden City hoping to become rich from the gold mines. The village is dominated by Sheriff Fred and his men, and the gold fields seem to be depleted. Pepito, mistaken for a stalker, suddenly discovers a new strand and the same evening celebrates in the saloon. Despite the advice of Susanna, the singer of the club, Pepito starts playing roulette and wins over Fred, taking away all his possessions and becoming the richest man in Golden City. At this point he decides to write to Carmen to be reached: she arrives after a few days, escorted by Fred and his men, who say they have saved her life, but on the wedding day Pepito discovers that Carmen and Fred have agreed to rob him and escape together. With a ruse, Pepito disarms the entire gang and engages in a duel with Fred, winning. Carmen and Fred are thrown out of the village, while Pepito marries Susanna.
text_b: The plot begins with the death of Bernarda's second husband, Antonio María Benavides. Family tradition requires that after the funeral mass, the widow commit her five daughters to eight years of mourning. For the young women, this means complete isolation from the outside world. Thus begins the central conflict of the drama. Angustias is the only daughter from her first marriage and inherited a fortune from her father. Because of this, and because she is the eldest of the daughters, she has permission to become engaged to Pepe el Romano. Adela, at 20 the youngest, rebels against her mother's strict order and also falls in love with Pepe el Romano, who returns her love. Nevertheless, for financial reasons, he decides to marry Angustias. Pepe el Romano meets at night not only with his future wife Angustias, but also secretly with Adela. Martirio, her jealous sister, denounces Adela in front of her mother. Wishing to restore order to the house, Bernarda shoots Pepe el Romano with a rifle in the courtyard. When Adela hears the shot, she mistakenly believes her lover is dead. As a result, Adela took her own life.

anchor_text: Harry works as a cabinetmaker and lives in an annex directly above his boss's yard. Lonely Harry stares longingly as his boss plays with his son. Shortly thereafter, he finds a cloth doll. At the same time, Gladys is fleeing her tyrannical husband. Harry finds the young woman in the snow and puts her in his plantation. It's only now that he realizes that Gladys is heavily pregnant. Quickly, he brings in midwives and doctors. After he's born, he's mistaken for the father. Harry's dream of a family seems to have come true. He takes great care of Gladys and her baby. A fortune teller assures him that Gladys' husband will never look for her. The husband is looking for his wife and finds her with Harry at the window of the plantation. Gladys, in love with Harry, wants to stay with him with her child. The men are boxing for a decision. Harry's only dreaming this, though. In reality, Gladys has reconciled with her husband, who thanks Harry with a handshake. The hand reminds Harry of the fortune-teller he wants to visit with a brick. When he sees the cloth doll lost on the street, he gives up his revenge and throws the stone away.
text_a: Nineteen-year-old Harry works in a wholesale warehouse for glass and porcelain in Stockholm. Seventeen-year-old Monika works at the vegetable market and has problems at home with her alcoholic father. When Monika meets Harry, she sees a way to escape from this world. The young lovers exchange the dreary world of the big city for the summer pleasures of an island in the Baltic Sea. However, the summer is soon over, and even before the couple has to return to Stockholm, Monika finds out that she is pregnant. Harry's aunt arranges for them to marry, and Harry is willing to work for his family and provide for them. But the birth of the child adds to the problems, overwhelming the young couple with parenting. When Harry catches Monica in bed with another man one day, the young family falls apart. Monica's leaving Harry and her little girl.
text_b: Harry, a mover's boy, watches his boss proudly play with his little boy, then retrieves a rag doll from the trash. Gladys, exasperated by her husband's intemperance, leaves him. Harry finds her slumped on the snowy ground and rescues her by hosting her in his modest attic. Then he notices that the woman is about to give birth to a child, and he immediately summons an army of midwives and doctors, who, when the child is born, congratulate him as the father. Harry finds his desire for a family magically fulfilled, and he diligently cares for the woman and the child. A fortune-teller assures him that the baby's father will never come looking for his wife and baby. The father, now repentant, instead goes to look for them and finds them, looking out, on a stormy night, at the attic window. Gladys, however, has grown fond of Harry, and, in the boxing match arranged between the two men, she incites and supports him, because she wants to stay with him, together with the child. But this is only Harry's dream. In reality the husband and wife reconcile, and, deeply grateful to Harry, shake his hand (whose palm Harry then looks disconsolate at) and leave with their son. That same night, the elements calm down, Harry leaves the house, and after spotting, abandoned and battered along the street, the rag doll whose traces had been lost goes in front of the fortune teller's window waving a brick. Then he gives up the useless revenge, and he drops the brick...

anchor_text: Caleb Channing grew up on a lunar mining colony. Now he's supposed to move to the idyllic, distant planet Omega after his father dies. With his three best friends and a new arrival from Earth, he fulfills his father's last wish before he leaves. They hijack a rover for one last adventure to a mysterious crater. There, Caleb looks for a spot in the ground that his father had named for him when he was alive. Under a plate, he discovers the capsule containing his mother's ashes. He puts the capsule with his father's ashes next to it. On the way back, the friends are caught in a meteor shower. When Caleb wakes up, he's already 75 years in the future, having spent the trip to Omega asleep, while his friends on the moon have had a good life and have aged contentedly.
text_a: After the death of his wife, Jake Barnes moved to the small coastal town of Quincy, Alaska. A seasoned pilot, Jake flies supplies to the most inaccessible parts of the country. But while his daughter, Jessie, enjoys this return to nature, his son, Sean, becomes depressed and rebellious. One night, after a violent clash with his son, Jake takes off on a routine mission. A few hours later, his plane crashed on top of a mountain. With the local authorities' search unsuccessful, and convinced that their father is still alive, Jessie and Sean set out on their own to track him down... On their way, they rescue a cute little polar bear from the clutches of a poacher. Their courage is tested as they have to kayak in the sea, face rapids, climb glaciers and climb mountains.
text_b: In 2257, young Caleb Channing born and raised in a mining colony on the Moon is soon to be transferred to another planet, Omega, presented as idyllic. His shuttle will leave in three days, after a complete lockdown of the station is lifted due to a meteor shower alert. Before he leaves, he wants to embark on an exploration of a mysterious lunar crater, according to the last wishes of his recently deceased father Michael, who adored this place. The orphan is accompanied by his three best friends, Dylan, Borney and Marcus as well as a new arrival from Earth, Addison Weaver. They're stealing a rover to venture away from the base dome, into a restricted area, despite the lockdown in place.

anchor_text: In the village of Zapiecek live the two blond, freckled twins Jacek and Placek. The two lazy and cruel brothers are constantly playing tricks on the inhabitants of the village. To free themselves once and for all from the burden of work, they decide to steal the golden moon. They manage to catch the moon in a net, but they are caught by robbers. When the moon disappears from the grid at the new moon, the twins wander into the golden city in search of the moon. It is there that they recognize the apparent value of a treasure. The brothers then return to their parents in Zapiecek and help them in the field.
text_a: The twin brothers, Jacek and Placek, are the troublemakers of the city. They're lazy, greedy and cruel. They despise work, they plan to be rich till the end of their days: steal the moon and sell it. Before they leave, they take their poor, hard-working mother's last loaf of bread. After many adventures, they manage to catch the moon in a fishing net. That's when their troubles begin...
text_b: The blue moon appears in the sky during the extremely rare months of the calendar with two full moons, one at the beginning and the other so mysterious at the end of the month. At least that's what Johnny Pichler's (Josef Hader) grandmother told him when he was a kid. How can one be surprised when his destiny is sealed by this magic and carries him straight to the great love... Enrolled as a suitcase carrier in a criminal case, he must, on the borders of Austria and Slovakia, flee with the Ukrainian call-girl Shirley (Viktoria Malektorovych) from the threats of a sinister mafia. This odd couple gets to know each other at a motel, but the mysterious beauty disappears from his life as soon as she walks in. That same night, the con man Ignaz (Detlef Buck) from the former East Germany joins him, and the two set off in search of Sherley. If they can't find Sherley, they find her twin sister, Jana. Then begins an odyssey through the countries of the former Warsaw Pact, which ends in Odessa where Johnny manages to break the curse that weighs on Jana.

anchor_text: The action takes place in 1934 in Kansas City. With the connivance of a taxi driver, black-clad Johnny robs a would-be gambler on his way to a club owned by the local mob. The two men were quickly unmasked and the driver was lynched. Blondie, Johnny's wife, comes up with the idea of freeing him from kidnapping the wife of Stilton, an adviser to President Roosevelt. The wife, Carolyn, is a compulsive opium addict. The husband is contacted who himself contacts several figures of the underworld, but at the moment the henchmen are busy organizing the rigging of the elections. When Johnny reappears at his home, he is severely wounded in the stomach and dies, while Blondie mourns him, Carolyn grabs his gun and kills her and then joins her husband who was waiting for her outside in a car.
text_a: Kansas City, 1934: The adventures and stories of several characters are intertwined in an environment only marginally affected by the Great Depression. Blondie O'Hara, a young woman who is going to kidnap the wife of Franklin Delano Roosevelt's advisor, Henry Stilton. Her goal is to free her husband, Johnny O'Hara, who is in the hands of gangster Seldom Seen, an African-American who owns a music and gambling club, and a taxi company called the Hey Hey Club. Johnny had pleaded guilty to robbing, disguised as an African-American and with the help of a taxi driver from Seldom Seen, a Simpson, a wealthy businessman and gambling maniac from Minnesota who was headed to the boss's club. In parallel are the adventures of Carolyn Stilton and Blondie, who wander through Kansas City casually contacting Henry for updates, and the kidnapping of Johnny.
text_b: Tammy loses her job at a fast-food restaurant and her cheating husband in the same day. She just wants to leave, but her car's broken down. The only way out is her grandmother Pearl, who lends her the car on the condition that she can come along and determine the destination. Since she has the necessary change, Tammy agrees, and the two set off for Niagara Falls. During the road trip, it becomes clear that Pearl has a massive drinking problem and that the relationship between her and her granddaughter has also been fractured by various incidents. When Tammy and her grandmother are arrested for Pearl's excessive drinking and Pearl can only pay Tammy's bail, she suddenly decides to rob a fast-food restaurant in order to free her ailing grandmother from prison. Now that she's been able to put that into action, they're both considered fugitives. The two decide to pay a visit to Pearl's cousin Lenore and her partner Susanne. There, they attend a women's 4th of July party, at which the grandmother relapses into alcoholism and hurts Tammy's feelings. The next day Tammy is arrested by the police for the robbery and goes to prison. The film ends 38 days later when Tammy is free again. After a meeting with her father, she rescues her grandmother from a nursing home. With her, Lenore, Susanne and Bobby, whom she met on the road trip, she visits Niagara Falls.

anchor_text: Little thief Johnny O'Hara is caught by colored gangster Seldom Lakes stealing from a player at his club, blackened his face in a futile attempt to lay a false trail, and held captive at his Hey Hey Club. Even though he got the money back in full, he wants to make an example of himself. His wife, Blondie O'Hara, modeled after her role model, the somewhat ordinary, blond-skinned movie diva Jean Harlow, fears for his life and in a fit of desperation kidnaps the bored, opium-addicted wife of Henry Stilton, a Democratic senator, Roosevelt adviser, and representative of the New Deal, in order to pressure him about his connections to the mob. Stilton's just holding them up. In the evening, Ohara returns, but collapses on the threshold of the house and dies. Stilton's wife shoots Blondie O'Hara in cold blood after she tries to get into Stilton's car waiting outside the house. In secondary threads, for example, it is shown how young Charlie Parker takes the 14-year-old pregnant Pearl Cummings, who missed her mentors from the Women's Club (Nettie Bolt) in Kansas City, to a jam session at the Hey-Hey Club. Other subplots shed light on the corruption of the time, such as when Blondie O'Hara Flynn's brother-in-law cheaply buys votes for the Democrats from the unemployed during the Great Depression and drives them from the countryside to Kansas City to vote.
text_a: Kansas City, 1934: The adventures and stories of several characters are intertwined in an environment only marginally affected by the Great Depression. Blondie O'Hara, a young woman who is going to kidnap the wife of Franklin Delano Roosevelt's advisor, Henry Stilton. Her goal is to free her husband, Johnny O'Hara, who is in the hands of gangster Seldom Seen, an African-American who owns a music and gambling club, and a taxi company called the Hey Hey Club. Johnny had pleaded guilty to robbing, disguised as an African-American and with the help of a taxi driver from Seldom Seen, a Simpson, a wealthy businessman and gambling maniac from Minnesota who was headed to the boss's club. In parallel are the adventures of Carolyn Stilton and Blondie, who wander through Kansas City casually contacting Henry for updates, and the kidnapping of Johnny.
text_b: Tammy loses her job at a fast-food restaurant and her cheating husband in the same day. She just wants to leave, but her car's broken down. The only way out is her grandmother Pearl, who lends her the car on the condition that she can come along and determine the destination. Since she has the necessary change, Tammy agrees, and the two set off for Niagara Falls. During the road trip, it becomes clear that Pearl has a massive drinking problem and that the relationship between her and her granddaughter has also been fractured by various incidents. When Tammy and her grandmother are arrested for Pearl's excessive drinking and Pearl can only pay Tammy's bail, she suddenly decides to rob a fast-food restaurant in order to free her ailing grandmother from prison. Now that she's been able to put that into action, they're both considered fugitives. The two decide to pay a visit to Pearl's cousin Lenore and her partner Susanne. There, they attend a women's 4th of July party, at which the grandmother relapses into alcoholism and hurts Tammy's feelings. The next day Tammy is arrested by the police for the robbery and goes to prison. The film ends 38 days later when Tammy is free again. After a meeting with her father, she rescues her grandmother from a nursing home. With her, Lenore, Susanne and Bobby, whom she met on the road trip, she visits Niagara Falls.

anchor_text: At the beginning of the 20th century, Adriana, a seamstress, is overjoyed when the man she has loved for so long asks for her hand in marriage. However, Cesare Braggi asks the seamstress not for himself, but for his younger brother Antonio. Cesare obeys only the last will of his rich father, although it is very difficult for him, because he also loves Adriana. Nevertheless, Antonio and Adriana are getting married. When Antonio dies in a fatal accident a few years after their marriage, it seems that Cesare and Adriana still have a fulfillment of their love. But by then, Adriana will be terminally ill.
text_a: In the pontifical Rome of the late 15th century, Caesar Borgia uses and abuses his ascendancy over his sister Lucrezia, a voluptuous creature. In accordance with his policy, he combines the marriages of Lucrezia. Sforza eliminated, she must marry Alfonso of Aragon. She submits and gladly acknowledges in her future husband the stranger whom she has filled with her favors on a carnival night. The Duke of Aragon's disgust soon gives way to love. Alas, Caesar is dreaming of a new alliance. He has Alphonse murdered. Resigned, painfully passive, Lucrezia will have to marry the Duke of Este.
text_b: Set in Sicily in the years before World War I, Adriana De Mauro (Sophia Loren) loves Cesar Braggi (Richard Burton), but Cesar, honoring his father's posthumous wishes, allows his brother Antonio (Ian Bannen) to marry her. But Antonio dies in a car accident. Adriana's mourning for Antony ends when Caesar intervenes to rekindle her lust for life. Soon, Adriana is starting to feel dizzy. Cesar takes her to a specialist, and the diagnosis isn't good. He has an incurable disease. In the rest of their time that illness allows them to be together, Caesar courts Adriana and eventually proposes a gondola to her. However, Signora De Mauro (Bárbara Pilavin), Adriana's mother is not happy with the relationship and bitterly argues with César and gets in the way.

anchor_text: With the help of a scientist friend, geneticist Peyton Westlake continues his work on synthetic skin. He's finally about to discover the secret that will make him human again. But the tragic turn of events leads him to relive the nightmare that disfigured him and to realize that his nemesis, Durant, who everyone thought was dead, is actually alive...
text_a: What is it? Peyton Westlake, a computer scientist specializing in cloning human body parts, has been able to exact revenge on the criminals who destroyed her life. Since then he continues with his project to regenerate his destroyed face because of them and continue with his fight against organized crime. However, one day he learns that one of those responsible for his tragedy, Durant, is still alive and working to regain his criminal power with the help of a laser. So he is now determined to put an end to the sadistic criminal once and for all.
text_b: Four teenagers from a high-risk neighborhood in New York City decide to set up a scam, but this first attempt in the world of crime turns into tragedy: Hayden is killed, Johnny is caught by the police while Patty and Gloria manage to escape. Five years have passed since the tragedy. But the beautiful Gloria still has a score to settle with this story: although she and Johnny were lovers, Hayden was her brother, and she bears all the responsibility for Johnny's death on her. All she wants now is revenge.

anchor_text: The protagonist, sitting in Cafe D. on one of London's busiest streets, observes the numerous passersby who pass on the sidewalk. By observing particular somatic or behavioural characteristics, he describes and catalogues them one by one, knows much about them and, in particular, is able to deduce their professions and their social position. The description of the crowd continues and he recognizes businessmen, swindlers, stockbrokers, professional gamblers, prostitutes and so forth, until he sees a man who arouses his interest because he cannot discern their position, activity and disposition. This individual is old, thin, short in stature and weak in appearance, but suggests to the protagonist the image of great moral strength and reflective abilities combined with low cunning and a sense of boundless anguish and terror. Shocked by this man's sinister appearance, he decides to follow him to capture some information. The two wander throughout the city returning several times to the point from which they started and it seems that the old man is afraid of loneliness; they spend almost twenty-four hours, but the mysterious man does not seem to notice his pursuer, and does not seem to want to return home: all he does is try to always be in the middle of the crowd. Then the protagonist, exasperated and exhausted, finally stands in front of him and stares him in the face, but the man remains completely indifferent and continues to walk without paying any attention to him. The protagonist then understands that he will never be able to get more information about the man; he understands how useless it is to continue to follow him and how he would not be able to know anything about him anyway. "It's the man in the crowd", he thinks, who doesn't want to be alone.
text_a: A distinguished man, a commuting professional, tired of the routine and the quarrels of jealousy with his wife, before entering the house, he climbs by chance on the attic of the garage and decides to stay there for months, pretending to disappear. From here begins a life of hardship and reflection. He'll eat from there, scraps of food he'll find rummaging through garbage, and live a simple and self-critical life, without any ambition, and all, while continuing to constantly observe his family from the window. It brings back memories of when he first met his wife, and of his daughters growing up every day. When he realizes that his success at work had led him astray from his path as a father and husband, he begins to contemplate his return. Meanwhile, he notices his wife's interest in another man, an old friend of his, the same one from whom he had stolen the girl, who later became his wife. This forces him to act quickly, to regain his image, to still stake the highest stake, the love of his wife.
text_b: The story opens with the following quote from the French moralist Jean de la Bruyère: "Ce grand malheur, de ne pouvoir être seul", taken from his work Caractères. This quote can be translated: "What a great misfortune it is not to be able to be alone". The same quote can be found in Poe's first short story, Metzengerstein.[1] After overcoming an undefined illness, the narrator spends time in a London cafe. Fascinated by the crowds that pass through the window, he considers the different types and characters (nobles, amanuenses, merchants, lawyers...), and the isolation to which they are subjected, despite living crowded in the big city. As evening falls, the narrator looks at a decrepit old man, some sixty-five or seventy years of age. He was of small stature, thin and apparently very weak. He wore clothes as dirty as rags. The narrator, full of curiosity, decides to leave the café and follow this man. This leads the narrator through shops and stores, never buying anything, until he ends up in a very poor area of the city, to return again to the heart of it. The chase continues through the night and into the next day. Finally, exhausted, the narrator comes face to face with the strange old man, who, unaware of having been followed, passes by. The narrator suspects, upon seeing him get lost again in the crowd, that he must be a terrible criminal, calling him "the man of the crowd".

anchor_text: Jim Carroll lives in a run-down part of New York. At the age of 13, he received a scholarship from a prestigious Catholic high school because of his basketball talent. But off the field, he and his friend Mickey are falling deeper and deeper into drugs. Soon, Jim Carroll's life revolves around drugs, sex, crime and violence. His drug addiction causes him to be expelled from school and his mother throws him out of the apartment they share. Nothing now stands in the way of his social decline, so he and his friends continue to slide further and further down the social ladder. He records all his experiences in his diaries, which later help him break free from drugs.
text_a: Jim Norman, a history and geography teacher, is hired to teach at the high school in the town where he grew up and where his older brother, Wayne, was murdered before his eyes by three thugs, his killers being themselves killed shortly after by a train. The brightest students in his class are victims of strange accidents and replaced by black jackets that are the perfect likenesses of his brother's killers. Norman is soon convinced that they have returned from the dead to take revenge.
text_b: Jim Carroll is a teenager living in a small apartment in Queens, New York City, with his estranged mother. Jim hopes to be a great basketball player and get into college on an athletic scholarship, so he plays on his high school team along with his friends. The team's coach, Swifty (played by Bruno Kirby), attempts to abuse Jim on occasion. Jim and his friends begin to try drugs, but he, after trying cocaine, finds himself in the drug world, just like his friends. For this reason, Jim ends up being kicked out of the basketball team and is left without a scholarship. Jim has a long way to go to get out of the lonely and dangerous streets of New York.

anchor_text: The film is set in Kansas City in 1933. Police Lieutenant Speer was once friends with former police private investigator Mike Murphy; the men later became rivals. They're both flirting with Addy, Murphy's secretary. Two men are in a bar looking for Murphy, attacking him with fists. The spear that is present turns on and helps to overwhelm the attackers. Speer and Addy attend a boxing match together, which is also attended by Murphy's business partner Dehl Swift and the gangster Primo Pitt. Swift is in possession of Primo Pitts's accounting records, which provide evidence of his gang's illegal activities. In an attempt to retrieve these books, Pitt shoots Swift. Now Murphy is in the line of fire, because the various gangs suspect that he is in possession of the books. And in fact, he manages to find those books in Swift's hiding place. In two final shootouts, almost all the gangsters are wiped out, and Speer and Murphy manage, reluctantly, but still hand in hand, to bring everything to a good end.
text_a: Already students at the same Police Academy, life has separated Speer and Murphy: the first is an uncompromising and fastidious lieutenant of the Kansas City Police Force, the other has set up an Investigative Agency, whose partner is the black Dehl Swift and secretary the affectionate Addy. Speer and Murphy, when they meet, never cease to tease and provoke each other, while respecting each other in substance. Swift gets into trouble by blackmailing a shady businessman and playing a double game between him and another local crime boss: he is in possession of compromising accounting documents, which of course appeal to the latter. So Swift, all busy making money and then cutting the rope, gets his ass kicked, and his partner Murphy has to run for cover. And the lieutenant can only give him a hand, gun in hand, to help the friend-rival and nail the gangsters. There are many reckless adventures, escapes and shootings, plus the kidnapping of Caroline (a class puppet, friend of Murphy), the case of Genny Lee, a pretty singer engaged to his deceased partner, a witness to a crime and then pursued by the henchmen on duty and even the kidnapping of Addy, who will be released by the two friends, but only after delivery of the briefcase containing the famous records. But there's also a bomb in the briefcase - a gift from Murphy - and the gang of bad guys blow up. The lieutenant and Murphy then find themselves in a luxury club, where the exuberant Murphy soon starts a fight with someone: in the midst of a fireworks display based on punches and broken glasses, the impassive Speer nevertheless leaves him to fend for himself, accompanying Ginny Lee's song on the piano.
text_b: Detective Brown is to accompany the widow of a gang boss on the train ride from Chicago to Los Angeles. It's for the protection of Mrs. Neall, who's about to testify in court. Brown is accompanied by his partner Forbes, who is killed by gangsters immediately after meeting with the widow. At the train station, Brown recognizes that the gangster Kemp is following him. On board the train, Brown makes the acquaintance of the attractive Ann Sinclair and her inquisitive son Tommy. Kemp sees Brown with his acquaintance and concludes that Ann is his target. Kemp's trying to bribe Brown. It'll let Brown know Kemp was wrong. He hands Kemp over to overweight fellow passenger Jennings and warns Ann. But she surprises him by telling him she's the real Mrs. Neall. The woman Brown has so far mistaken for Mrs. Neall is actually Sarah Meggs and serves as a decoy for potential pursuers. Kemp's partner, Densel, who killed Forbes, can take down Jennings and free Kemp. They storm Brown's compartment and kill Sarah Meggs. Then Densel is looking for Mrs. Neall. He locks himself in her compartment with her. Brown can shoot Densel. To do this, he shoots through the door when he sees Denzel's reflection in a second suit at a train station. Brown kills Densel as he enters the compartment. Kemp manages to jump from the halting train, but is quickly arrested.

anchor_text: During Prohibition in the United States, private investigator Dehl Swift purchases the books of accounts belonging to Leon Coll, the head of the largest local criminal organization. In exchange, he gives the accountant twenty-five thousand dollars and a ticket to Bolivia. He meets Coll later, and warns him that the accountant intends to sell the books to Coll's competitor: Primo Pitt. He then demands fifty thousand dollars in exchange for the return of the book, and betrays the accountant by giving the boat he has to take. When Swift arrives at his house, Pitt is already in the apartment, and asks for the account books. Swift refuses, and a fight ensues in which he dies. Coll, meanwhile, offered Swift's partner, Mike Murphy, fifty thousand dollars to get his books back. Later, Murphy receives a letter in his office: it contains a key that Swift sent to himself. In turn, Pitt asks Murphy to give him the books, pressuring him by kidnapping his girlfriend. Murphy then brings him the books, but the exchange goes wrong and Pitt is killed. Murphy then forcibly releases his girlfriend, who is confined to a lumberyard. After the operation succeeds, Coll comes to see him and threatens to kill Murphy's secretary, he gets the briefcase containing the account books. Finally, when Coll opens the briefcase, the bomb explodes.
text_a: Already students at the same Police Academy, life has separated Speer and Murphy: the first is an uncompromising and fastidious lieutenant of the Kansas City Police Force, the other has set up an Investigative Agency, whose partner is the black Dehl Swift and secretary the affectionate Addy. Speer and Murphy, when they meet, never cease to tease and provoke each other, while respecting each other in substance. Swift gets into trouble by blackmailing a shady businessman and playing a double game between him and another local crime boss: he is in possession of compromising accounting documents, which of course appeal to the latter. So Swift, all busy making money and then cutting the rope, gets his ass kicked, and his partner Murphy has to run for cover. And the lieutenant can only give him a hand, gun in hand, to help the friend-rival and nail the gangsters. He locks himself in her compartment with her. Brown can shoot Densel. To do this, he shoots through the door when he sees Denzel's reflection in a second suit at a train station. Brown kills Densel as he enters the compartment. Kemp manages to jump from the halting train, but is quickly arrested.

anchor_text: Insurance detective Maindrian Pace and his team lead a double life as unstoppable car thieves. And his real job comes in handy, because Pace never steals uninsured cars. When a South American drug lord pays Pace to steal 48 cars for him, the thief has all but a 1973 Ford Mustang. But getting it is no problem for the clever thief, and the specimen is found quickly. As Pace prepares to steal the car in Long Beach, he doesn't know that his brother-in-law has already ratted him out to the cops after a business dispute. So the police are waiting for him, but Pace doesn't get caught that easily, and a relentless manhunt begins that takes Pace through five cities.
text_a: Insurer Maindrian Pace and his team lead a double life - one as honest businessmen, the other as reckless car thieves. Problems arise only when a South American drug lord pays Pace to steal 48 cars for himself; the last car they have to steal is a 1973 Ford Mustang. Pace prepares to carry out the carjacking, code-named "Eleanor", in Long Beach, but is unaware that his partner has alerted the police as revenge following an argument between them: this will lead Pace to a chase that will cross five cities to lead to his escape.
text_b: Nick Pulovski is a veteran LAPD officer with unorthodox methods. While trying to capture sports car thieves, his teammate, Powell, is shot and killed by their leader, a man named Strom. David Ackerman, the son of a very wealthy businessman, has just joined the police force. He is trying to protect the innocent and redeem himself for not being able to save his brother, who died because of him in their childhood. Chosen to replace Powell and team up with Pulovski, Ackerman tries to help him capture Strom and his gang but Nick's unpleasant nature makes their duo explosive.

anchor_text: Football goalkeeper Josef Bloch is removed from the field because of a physical altercation with the referee. He then wanders aimlessly through Vienna and spends the night with the cinema cashier Gloria. When she tries to playfully seduce him with a rope the next morning, he strangles her to death for no apparent reason, removes his fingerprints from the apartment as best he can, and goes on another wild goose chase. He takes the intercity bus to visit a friend in Burgenland, but there is already a lot of police on the streets because of a missing child. From newspaper reports he informs himself about the progress of the investigation and learns that he has left a trail with American coins falling from a damaged pocket of his jacket. While conversing with his girlfriend and himself, he no longer makes an effort to cover his tracks, but acts conspicuously and deliberately puts hints that could draw the attention of witnesses - for example, he starts fights and asks his landlady to patch the hole in his pocket. At the end of the film, he watches a football match and uses a given penalty to tell his neighbour how much the goalkeeper and the goalkeeper have to concentrate on each other mentally. With the pencil held, the film ends whether Bloch is convicted remains open.
text_a: Handke tells the story of the mechanic and well-known former football goalkeeper Josef Bloch, who quits his job on a construction site on a Friday morning after he has the impression that he has been fired. He wanders the city of Vienna for the weekend and makes contact with the cinema cashier Gerda, whom he strangles after the first night together. Bloch then travels by bus to a "southern border town", where he visits a former acquaintance without coming to rest. Here, too, he is constantly on the move, while at the same time he follows as if uninvolved, more often than not accidentally finding out that the police are on his trail and tightening the net around him Bloch tries to convict himself. The story ends before he's arrested.
text_b: Joseph Bloch is a world-class goalkeeper. During a match in Vienna he scored, conceded a goal and was sent off by the referee. He then begins to wander around the metropolis and ends up strangling a cinema cashier with whom he has just spent the night. He takes a bus to the small town of Bierbaum where he tries to reconnect with an old friend. But over there, there's a lot of police on the roads because of a missing child. Reading newspaper articles, he finds out about the progress of the investigation and learns that he has launched a lead with U.S. coins dropped from a damaged pocket of his jacket. While chatting with his girlfriend and himself, he no longer takes the trouble to cover his tracks, but behaves ostentatiously and deliberately poses clues that could attract the attention of witnesses - for example, he starts a fight and charges his landlady to repair the hole in his pocket. At the end of the film, he watches a football match and explains to his neighbor, on the occasion of a penalty, how much the goalkeeper and the shooter must mentally focus on each other. The film ends with the penalty shootout - we don't know if Bloch will be convicted.
