text_a: Hans Behnke is a trained printer and, after years of unemployment and famine for his family, works in a large publishing house. Politically disinterested, he devoted his efforts to the preservation of the workplace even after the seizure of power by the National Socialists. Nevertheless, at the request of his brother-in-law, who later dies in the concentration camp, he repairs a printing press on which anti-fascist leaflets are printed, of which he takes home some copies in pure naivety. His son Hellmuth, a fanatical Hitler boy, discovers them and denounces him. Behnke is arrested by the Gestapo, tortured and transferred to Moabit prison. There, together with the other prisoners, he escapes being shot by the SS in the last days of the war. The advancing Russian troops save him and the others from execution. Returning to the half-destroyed apartment, he finds his wife Lotte no more, she is dead. Hellmuth is in captivity. One day, he's at the door, his father forgives him.
text_b: It tells the story of an old traveling bookseller. A Jew of Russian nationality, his presence is tolerated in a Viennese café because of his bibliographic erudition. Inwardly engrossed in his world, he is unaware of the drastic changes around him brought about by World War I. Falsely accused of collaborating with the enemy countries, France and Great Britain, in 1915 he was sent to a prison camp. After two years of imprisonment he is released, returning to the Café as if nothing had happened. But his memory is no longer the same, he can no longer read, his clients have disappeared, his whole environment has changed, reflecting the suffering suffered, which contributes to his tragic end. In his usual tearful tone, Zweig masterfully poses, through a small story of a modest character, the shocking blow that the Great War meant for Viennese life and culture.

anchor_text: A rescue expedition is organized and sails north to try to find men lost at sea under heroic circumstances. Equipped with fur coats and dog sledges, the characters must, as the title of the story suggests, spend the winter imprisoned in the ice of Greenland.
text_a: A rescue expedition sets sail to try to rescue some men who were lost at sea under heroic circumstances. Equipped with fur coats and dog-drawn sleighs, the characters must, as indicated in the title of the novel, spend the winter trapped in the ice of Greenland[3]
text_b: In February 1958, a team of Japanese scientists in Antarctica had to leave the Shōwa Antarctic Base due to extreme weather conditions, leaving behind fifteen sled dogs. Bad weather prevents the landing of the second team, which was supposed to take over. For almost a year, the dogs manage on their own to feed, keep warm...

anchor_text: In 1909, farmers and farm workers demanded from Mexican President Diaz the return of their lands, but were forcibly evicted from the fields. Zapata flees to the mountains with his brother Eufemio. There he receives a message that Madero, together with Zapata and Pancho Villa, wants to overthrow Diaz. After a short time, Zapata is captured, but farmers, Indians, and farm laborers are able to free him. Eventually Diaz resigns and Zapata marries Josefa. Diaz's successor, Madero, is weak and depends on General Huerta's help, who wants to evict the peasants again. Resistance flares up again, with Zapata even shooting his friend Pablo for contacting President Madero to mediate. His companion Fernando's influence is growing. After Madero's fall, Zapata moves to Mexico City. When a delegation of peasants complain about his brother's corruption in his homeland, he treats them as he was once treated by Diaz. Zapata is horrified by himself and returns to his roots. He stops his brother from turning his back on him. At home, he teaches the peasants that it is more important to organize themselves than to hope for a savior. He was assassinated in 1919 and became a legend.
text_a: Long live the Zapata! is a film about the life of Emiliano Zapata (Marlon Brando), a revolutionary leader of the Mexican Revolution, who is part of a delegation that meets with President Porfirio Díaz (Fay Roope) to protest his government's unfair treatment of peasants, favoring the landowners who had taken away their land. President Diaz was a president whose mandate was seen as a dictatorship, which lasted 34 years, and he treats them with rudeness and dismisses them. Faced with this attitude, Emiliano decides to start an open rebellion in southern Mexico. At the same time, Pancho Villa (Alan Reed) does the same with the reformist leader, Francisco I. It's called Wood (Harold Gordon). The rebellion succeeds and Porfirio Díaz is overthrown. He is replaced by Francisco Madero, and the new government soon follows the same corrupt and selfish path of the previous one. Emiliano's older brother, Eufemio, (Anthony Quinn), is shown as a petty dictator who takes for himself what he wants, without any respect for the law. President Madero naively hands his share of power to General Victoriano Huerta (Frank Silvera), who soon overthrows him and orders his execution. In turn, Huerta is soon overthrown and exiled. A new leader, Venustiano Carranza, assumes the presidency and defeats Pancho Villa. Zapata would follow in his footsteps until he was killed in an ambush.[7]
text_b: Diego de Zama, an American official of the Spanish Crown, is waiting for a letter from the King to take him away from the border post where he is stuck. Your situation is delicate. You must be careful that nothing clouds that possibility. He is forced to accept with submission any task ordered by the successive governors while he remains. Some years go by and the letter never comes. Realizing that in waiting he has lost everything, Zama decides to join a party of soldiers and leave for distant lands in search of a dangerous bandit. Free of his hopes of transfer and promotion, knowing he is in danger, he discovers that all he wants is to live. Maybe I can make it.[7]

anchor_text: Takezō and Matahachi fought in the Battle of Sekigahara. They're both on the losing side. Instead of the victory and glory Takezō expected, he is now a fugitive. The two friends seek refuge with a widow and her daughter. Both women try to seduce Takezō, but he refuses them. The widow tells Matahachi that Takezō attacked her. He should now accompany her and her daughter to Kyoto. Matahachi agrees, especially since he wants to see his fiancée Otsu again. Takezō returns to his village and tells Matahachi's family that he is alive. His mother doesn't believe him, and Takezo is to be arrested for treason. The whole village is looking for him, using Takezō's relatives as bait, but Takezō is not caught. Only the Buddhist monk Takuan Sōhō is able to seize Takezō. Takuan makes the governor promise to appease Takezo by certain methods. However, with Otsu's help, Takezō is able to escape. The monk finds out that the two are going to the Himeji-jō of a castle in the mountains, and can seize Takezō again there. He'll be locked in a room for three years. After his imprisonment, Takezō was given the samurai name Miyamoto Musashi. He sets out to gain enlightenment and leaves two messages for Otsu: "I will be back soon" and "Forgive me".
text_a: Raizo is one of the deadliest killers in the world. Seized from the streets as a child, he was turned after many years of training into a trained assassin by the Ozunu Clan, a secret society whose existence is considered a myth. However, upon seeing the atrocity committed by the Clan by killing his friend, Raizo abandons them once he has had all the training and disappears, hoping to exact revenge. In Berlin, Europol agent Mika Coretti has stumbled upon a lead that leads her to uncover the murder of politicians by a clandestine network of impossible-to-find killers from the Far East, for exactly the present or past value of 100 pounds of gold. Defying orders from her superior, Ryan Maslow, Mika digs deep into the agency's secret files to find out what happened after the murders. Her investigation makes her the target, and the Ozuno Clan sends a squad of assassins, led by the deadly Takeshi to stop her from talking and to continue covering her tracks, as the price of 100 pounds of gold is the price they always demand in exchange for killing someone. Raizo saves Mika from the attackers, but he knows the Clan will not rest until both are eliminated. Then, embroiled in a fateful game of cat and mouse through the streets of Europe, Raizo and Mika must rely on each other if they hope to survive and finally take down the secretive Ozunu Clan.
text_b: Before becoming the legendary samurai Musashi Miyamoto, the film focuses on the young man still known as Takezo, impulsive, violent, uneducated but also strong, skilled and eager to distinguish himself in combat. In the Battle of Sekigahara, Takezo and his friend Matahachi find themselves on the losing side. Forced to flee for their lives, after several adventures, the two friends are separated. Matahachi, although engaged to Otsu, a young woman from his native village, decides to escort women he meets on the way to Kyoto. Takezo joins Matahachi's family alone and announces that Matahachi is still alive. Incredulous, Matahachi's mother suspects Takezo of abandoning her son and tries to have him arrested. Eventually captured by the Buddhist monk Takuan Sōhō, Takezo spent three years in seclusion and study, despite the intervention of Otsu, who was in love with him. He will come out as Musashi Miyamoto, an apprentice samurai and will travel to complete his training, abandoning Otsu, who waited for him during his captivity.

anchor_text: The story takes place in high society among the lovers of classical concert music. In the center of the action is the famous and celebrated stardirigent René von Geldern. The man is a widower and has a little daughter with Susi. Since he is a busy artist, he hires a young and beautiful educator named Inge Salinger, who turns out to be a good-hearted person. René's ex-girlfriend Vilma Cortini, on the other hand, presents herself as an attention-seeking diva and extremely jealous superior, who sees Inge as a tough competitor to regain the conductor's favor. That's why she's asking for Miss Salinger's release. Eventually, Vilma's pressure builds up over money, and he fires the teacher. This, however, does not fit Little Susie at all, she has become very fond of Inge and wishes nothing more than that the young woman becomes her new mother. To achieve this goal, the girl tears herself open. He now has all hands full of money in the aftermath: he must get rid of the annoyingly intriguing Vilma Cortini, at the same time prepare for the upcoming wish concert and, by the way, also find his daughter again. In the end, everything adds up at the title-giving great wish concert, and von Geldern has not only orchestrated an artistic success, but has also found himself a new wife for life and a new mother for the super-lucky Susi.
text_a: Renowned conductor René von Geldern is a widower with a young daughter, Susi. As he is a busy artist, von Geldern hires a young and pretty housekeeper named Inge Salinger, who turns out to be a big-hearted person. René's ex-girlfriend, Vilma Cortini, on the other hand, behaves like a diva demanding a lot of attention and an extremely jealous bitch, who sees Inge as a tough competitor to regain the conductor's favor. So she's calling for Ms. Salinger to be fired. Eventually, von Geldern caved in to Vilma's pressure and fired the housekeeper. But this does not suit little Susi at all, since she has grown fond of Inge and wants nothing more than for the young woman to become her new mother. In order to accomplish this, the young woman flees without delay. Von Geldern must get rid of the annoying schemer Vilma Cortini, at the same time prepare for the next concert and, incidentally, also find her daughter. In the end, everything goes well at the charity concert, and von Geldern not only achieved artistic success, but also found a new wife for life and a new mother with the delighted Susi.
text_b: Inge Duhan lives with her husband, lawyer Erich Duhan, and their young daughter Susi in Berlin. Inge is a very attractive woman. She loves her husband and has always been faithful to him. The longer the marriage lasts, the more Inge feels neglected by her husband, who is absorbed in his profession. This leads him to believe that he is indispensable, and sends Inge on a vacation to the French Riviera alone. Although the young woman does not want it at all, on this occasion she succumbs to the charm of the painter Francard and begins a brief affair with him. Back in her home, Inge is soon blackmailed by an unknown woman who threatens to unleash a scandal and tell her husband everything. Inge is confused and struggles with herself: does she tell her husband the truth or does she end her life and what then becomes of the couple's common daughter? Eventually, Inge's husband Erich confesses to her that it was he who had set the unknown woman on her, since he had learned of their relationship. He thinks they're even now, and so the couple reconciles.

anchor_text: Dan Burns lives in the suburbs of New Jersey. By the time he was in his 40s, he was a well-known local newspaper columnist on morality. In addition, his column is in the running to be featured in a national daily newspaper. He is also the father of three daughters: Jane, the eldest, who wants to drive the car, Cara, who is in love with a boy and whom Dan prevents from experiencing her love, and Lily, the youngest. But he's been a widower for four years and he's convinced he'll never find love again. With his daughters, Dan traveled to Rhode Island to visit his parents, where he also met other family members, including his brother Mitch. One day, Dan goes to a bookstore when he meets Mary, who he falls in love with, but the latter is not at the end of his surprises: indeed, he finds Mary in the family home, because the young woman is Mitch's new girlfriend...
text_a: Dan Foreman is 51 years old, sales manager for Sports America magazine, highly regarded by his collaborators, married, with his wife expecting their third child, and his eldest child Alex has just been accepted into a prestigious New York university. Everything would be fine, except that the company he works for is taken over by a multinational corporation, Dan is demoted to make way for Carter, a 26-year-old held in high regard by the new management. His young age and his ways of doing (staff cuts) do not make him likable in the eyes of the other employees, everything gets worse when the young man after only seven months of marriage is dumped by his wife, and falls in love with Alex, Dan's young daughter. Carter is very much in love with the girl but when her father discovers the affair and punches him, the girl also out of respect for her father decides to leave the young boss.
text_b: Dan Burns (Steve Carell) is a widowed father who must take care of his three daughters. He works writing a newspaper column in which he advises on aspects he considers real life: family relationships, friends and love. One day, on his way to a family gathering at the home of his parents and with the rest of his brothers and nephews, he meets Marie (Juliette Binoche) in a bookstore and falls in love with her at first sight. What she doesn't know is that this is her new sister-in-law and she's going to be introduced to her family at the same family gathering she's attending...

anchor_text: A ruse by the corrupt Sheriff Douglas puts Ted Barnett behind bars for a crime he didn't commit. He is said to have murdered his father. After three years, Barnett escapes from a penal colony. Barnett begins a bloody campaign of revenge against all those involved in the conspiracy against him. With the help of the quack Pajarito and his daughter, he enters the city, where he manages to extract a confession from the witness Gomez before killing him. His ex-girlfriend Dolly has married Sheriff Douglas and pretends to still love Ted, but her intention is his extradition, for which she must also die. Then Ted, who now has a bounty on his head, takes on the gang of landowner Cobb, who works with Douglas. His attempt to do this with the help of the men around General Porfirio fails they have economic interests in a connection with Cobb's people and beat him up. He is imprisoned again, although Dulcie, Pajarito's daughter, had found him. Meanwhile, his innocence is established; Judge Kincaid delivers the official letter that prevents his execution at the last second. Cobb doesn't accept this and starts a firefight, which ends with him being defeated in a duel with Ted. Dulcie will take care of Ted in the future.
text_a: Ted and his gang commit a bank robbery and hide the loot. When he is mortally wounded by his accomplices, he gives Joe, a poor prairie hunter, a map to the hiding place. The remaining gang, now led by Jack, capture Joe and torture him to find out what he knows. He's blinded by torture with a torch. His love, the saloon owner Rosy, is also used against him; when the bandits allegedly lead Joe and Rosy to their hideout, he is ambushed and the map is stolen. When Joe regains his sight, he takes up the fight against the bandits, which Rosy uses to escape with the money now in the possession of the group. She has an accident while crossing a river. The money's floating away, too. Joe will be left alone and poor.
text_b: Ted Barnett was wrongfully imprisoned and his father was murdered. Escaping his cell, Ted and his cellmate head to Kartown, where Ted wants to shed light on why he was put in jail and who killed his father. His escape alarms Cobb, a local gentleman, Sheriff Douglas, bribed by Cobb and Ted's girlfriend Dolly, induced to marry the same sheriff. To reach the town unnoticed, Ted lets himself be escorted by a drifter and his niece Dulcy. Upon arriving in the small town of Kartown, Ted discovers that the plot has been plotted against his father by Mexican revolutionaries who, aided by Cobb and Sheriff Douglas, had to dispose of his father's railroad station to carry out the plan. The young man's revenge is well planned, but in the end the duel seems to have a happy ending only for Cobb. Ted - an escapee - is sentenced to hang but, thanks to Dolly's help revealing the truth to the judge, Ted manages to free himself, turning the situation in his favor.

anchor_text: Around 1995 Vietnam wakes up after a long slumber and to survive in Ho Chi Minh City, a young cyclist works hard to provide for his family. The family knew and knew the meaning of the word "sacrifice", it was etched in their flesh. The elder sister studies very early in the morning to work quietly in the only room of the house, and thus improve the daytime routine of the family in a cantina in the city. The grandfather, he makes himself useful for a few coins, by the roadside. The young cyclo is robbed of his work buggy, and is thus led by his employers into the world of irregularity and abuse.
text_a: In a small village like thousands in Vietnam, young Le Ly discovers the anger of a people, her people. In a Buddhist family that sends two of its sons to war, almost forcibly recruited by the northern troops, the Việt Cộng are far from the defenders of the fatherland that they claim to be. Nor are the Americans and the South Vietnamese government forces. And there's a perfect critique of both sides of this war. Suffering the worst physical and moral humiliation, the girl flees to Saigon, where fate seems to be cruel against her, until the day she meets Steve Butler, an American soldier who falls madly in love with her. He wants to marry her and bring her back to the States.
text_b: Ho Chi Minh: Young Cyclo earns his living by driving the rickshaw he rents. When his ransom is stolen, he is forced to pay it back by committing criminal acts on behalf of a gang of thugs. The leader of the gang, nicknamed the Poet, is the same one who will force Cyclos' sister into prostitution. The world around the characters continues to spin indifferently. The horrors of some scenes take place in close proximity to everyday life, with no one intervening to stop them.

anchor_text: Sam Houston, raised by Cherokee Indians, joins the U.S. Army. Under General Andrew Jackson, he participated in the Battle of Horseshoe Bend and was wounded. Houston's bravery in battle earns him Jackson's friendship. When Jackson is elected President of the United States, Houston becomes Governor of Tennessee. On the eve of his re-election, he marries Eliza Allen. But soon it turns out that the hesitant Eliza can not cope with the impetuous and wild character of her husband. She leaves him, which causes a scandal. Houston is forced to resign as governor. He and his friend Lannie Upchurch move in with the Cherokees. He's traveling to Washington as a negotiator for the Cherokees. There he meets Margaret Lee at a presidential ball. After President Jackson agrees to the demands of the Indians, Houston travels with Margaret to Texas. During the journey in the carriage, the two fall in love. Houston, however, turns first to political tasks. He wants to support the state of Texas to secede from Mexico. He meets Stephen F. Austin, who seeks a peaceful solution. Soon news arrives that General Santa Ana is sending his troops across the country to murder and plunder. Now even Austin realizes that war is inevitable. Jackson assigns Houston to fight the Mexicans and makes him commander-in-chief of the Texan troops. With his army, Houston moves to the Alamo garrison near San Antonio. The garrison, however, has already fallen. Houston is retreating from the approaching Mexicans. It comes to the Battle of San Jacinto, in which Houston defeats Santa Ana's troops. Victory clears the way for Texas independence. When Texas later joins the Union, the dying president thanks his old friend for his fight for Jackson's foundations of democracy.
text_a: Sam Houston is tasked with organizing the Texas Army to attack the Mexican Army led by General (and dictator of Mexico) Antonio López de Santa Anna. Houston desperately needs time to enlist soldiers and train them; the only way to give him that time is to defend the town of San Antonio de Bexar to the end by entrenching himself inside the mission, known as the Alamo. At the head of the small group of soldiers is Colonel Travis. Supporting these are the volunteers of Jim Bowie, a famous soldier who became a wealthy landowner in the area. Adding to the ranks from Tennessee is David Crockett, a popular hunter and pioneer, who was also elected to Congress, with about thirty men. Travis knows perfectly well that there is no hope against Santa Anna, but also that the Mexicans will have to waste time to seize the Alamo; Jim Bowie hopes to succeed with guerrilla actions to slow down the march of the Mexicans without having to stay in the mission; Crockett tries to mediate between the two. With even the last hope of receiving reinforcements gone, Travis decides to resist in spite of everything, and is also followed by Bowie first and then Crockett. Two days of battle and the death of the three protagonists follow. At the end of the battle, none of the combatants survive, but Captain Dickinson's wife, daughter, and an African-American child are rescued. They are joined by Smitty, a young follower of Crockett who had been sent to warn Houston and who returns in time to see the end of the battle.
text_b: Sam Houston fights alongside his friend Andrew Jackson and is wounded. Shortly thereafter, Jackson is elected President of the United States and appoints Houston as Governor of Tennessee. Houston is married to Eliza Allen, but his lifestyle as a politician does not appeal to him. Their divorce proves somewhat scandalous for the time, and Houston decides to accept Jackson's suggestion to become an ambassador for the Cherokee tribe. On a trip to Washington D. In order to deliver his speech that Indians are being mistreated on their own land, Houston falls in love with Margaret Lea at a presidential ball. She returns with him to Texas, where Houston's next mission is to free the territory from Mexican rule, either through diplomacy or on the battlefield. This is Stephen F. Austin disagrees with Houston's methods, preferring peaceful negotiations, but as Santa Ana's army heads toward the Alamo in large numbers, Houston knows that a peaceful settlement is not possible. He arrives too late to prevent the carnage there, but then leads the Texans in their fight for freedom and statehood.

anchor_text: An injured man is found on the side of the road. The police come to investigate the college student David Klein, who in some flashbacks tells about the previous events. Klein befriends Jenny Kelley and hopes that it will become a love affair. In time, he realizes that his chances are slim. His friend Alan Hammond meets Kelley and enters into a relationship with her. It makes Klein jealous.
text_a: Carolyn Ryan and her husband, Ben Ryan, live in New England. Carolyn is a doctor, Ben is a sculptor. They have two children, Jacob and Judith. Jacob's girlfriend is found dead. Ben refuses to cooperate with the police. Jacob goes underground, pretending to be hiding in another city through the postcards sent by the travelers. He's going to be arrested. Jacob is accused of murdering his girlfriend. Ben destroys evidence against his son by wiping blood off a forklift. Attorney Panos Demeris is charged with Jacob's defense. Jacob tells his parents how his girlfriend died. There was an argument and a hand-to-hand combat, in which the girl fell on a forklift.
text_b: When David befriends a college classmate, the brilliant and vivacious Jenny, it doesn't take long for them to fall in love. However, Alan, David's old friend, unreliable and with a transgressive and erratic life, attracts Jenny's attention, and the two soon begin a troubled relationship. The situation is spiraling inexorably.

anchor_text: A writer searches for inspiration on a beach in the French Riviera. There he meets a mysterious young woman who reminds him of the heroine of his novel. He sets out to conquer this strange and possibly psychopathic young woman, protected by a powerful lawyer and his henchmen. Murders are being committed. Peggy's the number one suspect. She had earlier killed her husband with an ice pick.
text_a: In a village on the banks of the Meuse on the Franco-Belgian border, Basile Matrin, a factory storekeeper, leads a monotonous life alongside his wife Rose. The young Maryse Duval, returning from Paris leaving her dream of being an actress behind, will unwittingly change the fate of Rose and her husband. The neighbour and friend of Basile Matrin, a local correspondent for the Quotidien de la Meuse, witnesses the comic drama playing out in front of his house. He's going to enter his body defending in this drama...
text_b: Nice one. Writer François meets the beautiful Peggy on the beach, a woman with a mysterious past, and falls in love with her almost instantly. The woman, however, has a strange relationship with the wealthy lawyer Rilson, who first advises François to leave the woman alone, and then decides to switch to more aggressive methods, making him beaten by one of his men. But François has no intention of abandoning Peggy, at least not until he knows the whole truth about her past.

anchor_text: Through the recordings made between 2001 and 2003, the viewer gets an insight into the creative process as well as into the conflicts that prevailed at this point in the band's career. In particular, the alcohol problems of singer and rhythm guitarist James Hetfield, who enrolls himself in a rehab clinic, are discussed. In the foreground is the constant power struggle between drummer Lars Ulrich and James Hetfield. Without consulting the rest of the band, Hetfield leaves the album production midway to pursue his passion for hunting in Siberia, where he kills two bears. Talks are held with the specially hired psychologist Phil Towle, who finally succeeds as a mediator to keep the band together. The film also deals with former guitarist Dave Mustaine, later vocalist and lead guitarist of Megadeth, who, during a conversation with Ulrich, expresses his disappointment that he was kicked out of the band in the 1980s due to drug problems. Also briefly addressed is the lawsuit against Napster, which led to an angry reaction from some fans who gathered outside a courthouse where drummer Lars Ulrich was suing Napster for Internet piracy, to throw their Metallica CDs to the ground and trample them. The departure of bassist Jason Newsted in the spring of 2001 is also discussed, and recordings of an interview and of his new band Echobrain are shown. Metallica had to go without a bassist for a long time after Newsted's departure. The film documents the search for a new bassist and the selection process. As a new bassist, Robert Trujillo was eventually hired by Metallica, who played with Ozzy Osbourne until 2003. At the presentation of the new bassist and the new album St. Anger towards the end of the documentary, the band presents unity and confidence for future successes.
text_a: Director Martin "Marty" Di Bergi (Rob Reiner) presents the documentary (or "rockumentary") he shot on the band Spinal Tap in 1982, their first US tour in 6 years. The band consists of childhood friends David St. Hubbins (lead guitar), Nigel Tufnel (lead guitar) and Derek Smalls (bass), along with keyboardist Viv Savage and drummer Mick Shrimpton. This group has been successful since its inception in 1967, but its history is marred by strange accidents that have killed successive drummers (garden accident, spontaneous human combustion, suffocation in vomit...). Nigel has an impressive collection of guitars, as well as one-of-a-kind amplifiers: while traditional amps are rated up to 10, his are rated up to 11, which allows him, according to him, to play louder than any other guitarist... But little by little, the troubles accumulate: several dates of their tour are canceled, the cover of their new album, "Smell The Glove" is considered sexist and censored, and the presence of Jeanine, David's girlfriend, increases the tension. Following a failed concert at an air base, Nigel left the band, which continued without him. Eventually, the surprise success of their new album earned them a tour of Japan with Nigel deciding to rejoin the band...
text_b: Metallica are forced to question themselves, when James Hetfield abruptly leaves the band to enter rehab. Metallica's managers then hired a "performance improvement coach" Phil Towle, to help the band get along better, as friends, partners and human beings. Towle is often mistakenly referred to as the "therapist", since he himself revoked his degree, to try to convince clients to continue their treatment. When the members of Metallica decide that they don't need the "Therapist" anymore and they tell Phil Towle, he tries to convince them that they still need him: We still have some [trust] issues that I think we need to sort out Metallica fans especially note the perceived "power struggle" between Hetfield and drummer Lars Ulrich - best friends for nearly two decades, who are finally starting to understand each other. In one of the film's most memorable scenes, Ulrich confesses that Hetfield's need to control everything weighs heavily on him, even when Hetfield is not present (Hetfield criticized the band's work on some tracks even when he was not present). The scene ends with Hetfield starting to regret his words when his best friend yells at him nose to nose FUCK!. On April 2, 2004, a rush of the film was mounted at the Gateway Theatre in Chicago, IL, where the FUCK scene is longer. Ulrich actually shouts FUCK! several times in Hetfield's face, rather than just once in the final version, which was released in theaters in 2004. This scene was probably altered due to the nature of its content and does not appear in the "Cut Scenes" or elsewhere in the DVD content. In the film can be heard many pieces of unreleased songs. The only song that can be heard in its entirety that isn't on the St. Louis album. Anger is the song "Temptation".

anchor_text: A year has passed since Amaia Salazar solved the crimes that terrorized the Basque valley of Baztan. Pregnant and determined to leave behind what happened in Elizondo, the life of the inspector is again upset by an unexpected event: the suicide of several prisoners who leave a single word written on the wall of their cell, "Tartalo" (Cyclope in Basque mythology). The dangers Amaia thought she had left behind come back with more force than ever and the detective will have to face this new case in a dizzying investigation threatened by the presence of her own mother.
text_a: On the banks of the Baztán River, a river located in the northern area of the Foral Community of Navarre, the naked body of a young woman appears in circumstances that relate that crime to a murder that occurred a month ago. Inspector of the homicide section of the Foral Police Amaia Salazar (Marta Etura) leads an investigation that will take her back to the town of Elizondo (Valle del Baztán), where she grew up and from which she has tried to flee all her life. Faced with the complicated derivations of the case and its own ghosts, the investigation is a race against time to find a murderer, in a fertile land in superstitions and witchcraft so significant of the Basque mythology in that area.[15] [16] [17]
text_b: It's been a year since Amaia Salazar solved the crimes that terrorized the Baztán Valley. Pregnant and determined to leave behind what she lived in Elizondo, the inspector's life is again altered by an unexpected event: the suicide of several prisoners who leave a single word written on the wall of their cells, "Tarttalo". The dangers that Amaia thought she had left behind return with more force than ever and the inspector must face this new case in a dizzying investigation threatened by the presence of her own mother.[4][5]

anchor_text: In Thailand, Connor's girlfriend Amanda is kidnapped by a group of bikers. The police aren't doing anything. That's why Connor's going after his girlfriend on his own. Connor encounters other vampire clans who have been fighting each other for 800 years and now face the decisive battle on the Siamese New Year's Eve. Amanda's in the group of another vampire clan. So that Connor can save Amanda from the vampire boss Niran, he allies himself with Sang's group.
text_a: Connor and Amanda, two American tourists on vacation in Thailand, unwittingly find themselves in the middle of a struggle between two gangs of vampires: the gang of the very first vampires who chose to drink only animal blood and the gang of the new vampires, the biker vampires who thirst for human blood. These two gangs have been fighting for 800 years. Amanda is kidnapped by Niran, the biker vampire lord, and Connor will have to join the war against the forces of evil to get her back. Besides, it's the opposite of the legendary vampires, and whereas in this movie, these vampires are not afraid of crucifixes or garlic cloves, and they can reflect themselves.
text_b: A group of college students decide to relax by spending an evening in a bar with stripteases. After having some problems with bikers, also present in this bar, they find themselves faced with another concern, because all the staff of the bar seems to be vampires...

anchor_text: In Thailand, Connor's girlfriend Amanda is kidnapped by a group of bikers. The police aren't doing anything. That's why Connor's going after his girlfriend on his own. Connor encounters other vampire clans who have been fighting each other for 800 years and now face the decisive battle on the Siamese New Year's Eve. Amanda's in the group of another vampire clan. So that Connor can save Amanda from the vampire boss Niran, he allies himself with Sang's group.
text_a: For many centuries, the Phi Song Neng (vampires who have sworn never to drink human blood) have lived among the villages of the Far East. One day over 800 years ago, a young human warlord named Niran fell in love with a song of the Neng, a woman named Sang. Niran wanted Sang, so he kills her husband and son. In his grief and anger, Sang bites Niran during a Songkran (New Year) solar eclipse, turning him into a vampire.
text_b: A group of college students decide to relax by spending an evening in a bar with stripteases. After having some problems with bikers, also present in this bar, they find themselves faced with another concern, because all the staff of the bar seems to be vampires...

anchor_text: Boxer Johnnie Bradfield is the new heavyweight champion of the world. During a banquet in his apartment, his manager, Doc Ward, blames the death of reporter Magee. Ward arranges the evidence so that it all points to Johnny's guilt. Ward causes a car accident on his way home, in which he dies and is burned. Since he's wearing a Johnnie wristwatch, it's mistakenly assumed that the burned body is Johnnie. The next morning, Johnnie wakes up in a strange environment. He reads in a newspaper that he killed a reporter and then died in an accident. Johnnie runs away and gets himself a new identity. In Arizona, he rents out to Grandma Rafferty, who runs a youth rehabilitation camp with Peggy. New York detective Phelan is convinced that Johnnie is still alive and sets out to find him. The so-called Dead End Kids, a group of six youths from a New York street gang, are sent to the camp. Johnnie, who calls himself Jack Dorney, takes in teenager Tommy, Peggy's brother. He convinces him to go into business for himself with a gas station. He wants to win the money for it in prize fights. Phelan was able to follow Johnnie's trail and show up at the boxing match. First of all, Johnnie doesn't want to fight because he doesn't want to give himself away. But the obvious disappointment of Peggy and the teenagers changes his mind. He deliberately avoids using the fighting style that made him famous. This gives his opponent leverage and threatens to defeat Johnnie. Johnnie gives himself up, but loses in the fifth round. He's going to Phelan to be arrested for his faith. But it allows him to stay in Arizona.
text_a: Psychiatrist John Clancy, who can predict the future, teams up with FBI agents Joe Merriweather and Katherine Cowles to catch a serial killer, who is later revealed to be linked to John. The killer, apparently gifted with such precognition, kills terminally ill people destined for death, without causing any pain, in a kind of preventive euthanasia. In one of these investigations, John expects to find some evidence to find a likely suspect who, once found, shoots Joe and then runs away. John goes in pursuit of the suspect with Agent Merriweather, who is injured, but the man turns out to be just a mythomaniac. After this episode, John will understand that the serial killer had arranged everything to try to kill Joe, who in fact was also suffering from a stage four cancer. He also predicts the violent death of Officer Katherine Cowles, but withholds information from the woman. In the end, it will be the criminal who reveals himself to him, a man named Charles Ambrose. The man will do anything to capture him. Charles and John meet on a subway train. Charles tells him he'll kill Agent Cowles unless he kills him, since he's dying too and needs John to take his place. As Katherine steps inside the carriage to catch the serial killer, Charles shoots her and John shoots Charles, but only the latter dies. It turns out that John had also euthanized his daughter Emma, who had died of leukemia years earlier.
text_b: After a victorious fight in New York, left-hander Johnnie Bradfield spends a rainy evening with his friends, during which one of them, in the absence of witnesses, kills an overly curious journalist. Taking advantage of Johnnie's cooking, the killer flees to make him take the fall, robbing him of his money, his watch, and his girlfriend. But when the police chased the couple, they were burned to death in their car. Newspapers can run headlines about the champion assassin, identified by his watch and his death. Only Phelan doesn't believe it: he had tied the watch to Johnnie's left wrist and it was found on the dead man's right wrist. With his colleagues at work, he decides to find Johnnie. He wandered as far as Oklahoma, where he eventually settled as Jack Dorney at Peggy Rafferty's date farm, which hosts troubled teenagers, and they began living together. But the ranch's in a bad way, and a gas station project could get it back on track. It takes $2,000 to do that. And now we've got Rutchek on the show, a bully who's promising $500 for every round against him. Johnnie/Jack signs up, but Phelan, who recognized him from an award-winning photo of one of the kids shows up to watch the fight and stop him...

anchor_text: Vince Rizzo is the father of a tumultuous family living in City Island, a small town in the Bronx. He's also a prison guard. But what's his biggest secret? Is this the acting class he's taking instead of playing poker? The cigarettes he's smoking when he's supposed to be quitting? Or his son in prison, whose existence only Vince knows? A son he's going to host when he gets out of prison, and who's going to gradually uncover all these little secrets.
text_a: Vince Rizzo is a P.O. who has a passion for acting but is ashamed to admit it to his family. He takes acting classes, and to hide it from his wife, he makes up stories about playing poker with his friends. One day at work in prison he meets his son, Tony Nardella, from a young relationship with a woman he abandoned before his son was born. Vince gets permission to take Tony to his house on parole. No one in the family knows why Tony's in his house, not even Tony himself. Daughter kicked out of college and lost her scholarship because of some amazing drug found in her room, to pay for her studies finds a job in a nightclub as a stripper. Each member of the family is hiding a secret, but one night they will all tell the truth.
text_b: After being bullied, teenager Cal Chetley joins the school wrestling team in Oklahoma, also to reunite with his estranged and rebellious brother Mike. His mother, Sharon, is initially not supportive of Cal's decision, especially when she finds out that Mike is coaching him and after she receives a call from the prison where Mike is being held, arrested for a fight. Cal, like his father and brother, will also become a wrestling legend, and he will be able to reunite his brother Mike and his mother, 10 years after his father's death.

anchor_text: When Sherlock Holmes and Dr. Watson decide to go on a vacation, they receive a mysterious invitation. Holmes, aroused by curiosity, accepts the invitation. He meets with the prime minister of the fictional state of Rovinia, who asks him to accompany the heir to the throne, Prince Nicholas, whose father was murdered, home. A flight is booked, but problems mean that only the Prince and Holmes can board. The protesting Watson is to follow the passenger steamer Friesland to Algiers. During the journey, Watson reads the news that the plane carrying Holmes and the Prince has crashed in the Pyrenees. There is no hope of finding survivors. However, Holmes, who has an aversion to following plans made by others, has also boarded the ship with Nikolas. He introduces the prince to the other passengers as Watson's nephew. For the sake of simplicity, Watson suspects everyone of being potential killers. American singer Sheila Woodbury, sports enthusiast Agatha Dunham, and a secretive couple also came under Watson's suspicion. But it's not until Lisbon that the real assassins get on board: Gregor, the knife-thrower Mirko, and the mute and cumbersome Gubec. Mirko commits an assassination attempt on Holmes by throwing a knife through a bull's eye at Holmes. Also, Gregor's attempt to detonate an explosive during a celebration of the wealthy Agatha Dunham is thwarted by the detective. Arriving in Algiers, the three assassins are able to kidnap the prince. But Holmes makes it clear that the kidnappers got the wrong guy. The alleged prince was a decoy, the real Nicholas was employed as a steward on the ship the whole time.
text_a: Sherlock Holmes and his faithful companion Dr. Watson travel by train to Dartmoor to investigate the disappearance of the racehorse Silver Star and the murder of his trainer John Straker. Inspector Gregory is also investigating the case and initially suspects Fitzroy Simpson, a bookie from London, as his tie was found at the scene. The horse's owner, Colonel Ross, is asking Holmes and Watson for help. Holmes quickly finds out that Simpson had nothing to do with the horse's murder or disappearance. Instead, Holmes and Watson follow the tracks the horse left in the moor on the stormy night. You'll find the horse in the stable of rival Silas Brown, who found the horse alone in the moor. Holmes and Watson now return to London with no further investigation of the murder. On the day of the race, in which Silver Star was supposed to start as the favorite, Holmes, Watson and Ross meet again. Ross must find to his surprise that Silver Star is still lined up for the race and is winning it as well. He does not recognize his horse at first, but Holmes explains to him that his rival Brown Silverstern's characteristic white head and white legs have been dyed dark to better conceal the horse. All that remains is the murder of John Straker, which Holmes is about to solve. While attempting to immobilize the horse with a knife so that it could not win its next race, Straker was slain by Silver Star himself. Straker led a double life under the name Darbyshire, attempting to make a lot of money with what he believed to be a safe bet against Silver Star to fund his second identity.
text_b: Intending to leave London for a well-deserved holiday, Holmes and Watson receive an encrypted invitation. Intrigued by the mystery, Holmes agrees to the meeting and finds himself facing the prime minister of the fictional state of Rovinia, who pleads with Holmes to escort Prince Nikolas home, since he finds himself in the delicate position of heir to the throne after his father's death. Holmes agrees with me. The plane, booked in haste, barely made room for the prince and Holmes, who were forced to leave Watson to continue the voyage by ship to Algiers. During the journey, Watson reads that the plane crashed in the Pyrenees and that there were no survivors. Fortunately, Holmes had at last changed his plans and brought himself aboard the ship with Nikolas. He then suggests that Watson introduce Nikolas to the other passengers as his nephew. Although Watson suspects everyone from American singer Sheila Woodbury to gymnast Agatha Dunham. Everything seems to go smoothly until Lisbon, where two Russian secret agents board the ship with the task of kidnapping the prince. Eventually the criminals manage to kidnap the prince during the docking at the port of Algiers, to then allow Holmes to reveal that the kidnapped prince was a substitute and that the real prince had boarded as a steward on the ship and remained incognito the entire time.

anchor_text: Dr. Jennifer Pailey and her sister Lisa are coming to the town of Snowfield in the mountains. They find that all the villagers have disappeared or are dead, some of the bodies are mutilated. Sheriff Bryce Hammond and his deputies from headquarters are on the case. They are being assisted by scientist Timothy Flyte, who has been contacted by government agencies. Investigators have determined that the city was attacked by an ancient, petroleum-based alien entity that has made similar attacks in the past. In one of them, the Mayans were wiped out. Timothy Flyte refers to this being as The Old Enemy. The survivors attempt to kill the creature, which can take the form of the life forms it has engulfed. This is supposed to be done with the help of oil-eating bacteria. A duel begins between Flyte and the part of being he calls mother substance.
text_a: Two sisters, Lisa and Jennifer, arrive in the tourist town of Snowfield, Colorado. Jennifer, who works as a doctor there, wants to get her sister away from Los Angeles and their alcoholic mother. They discover that Snowfield has become a secluded ghost town, littered with apparently contaminated corpses or dismembered body parts. The two sisters meet the village sheriff and his deputies. Several monsters of different shapes attack them and kill the deputies. Dr. Flyte, banished from the academic community for his fair theories suitable for sensation magazines, is brought by the army to Snowfield with a group of scientists. The military and scientists are decimated but the doctor determines the chemical nature of the age-old enemy living in the bowels of the earth and with the help of the sheriff and the two sisters, arranges for the shape-shifting beast to be contaminated with a dissociating bacterium. Unfortunately the smart oil is not completely destroyed.
text_b: Sisters Megan and Abby Graves are lost on a pleasure cruise in the Arizona desert. In a restaurant, they are invited to visit an abandoned mine. In the Skull City Mine, they seem to be the only visitors. Visiting the dilapidated houses of the mining area, they witness a brutal murder. The sisters themselves are then pursued by the Hammer Killer. Megan manages to kill this one with a piece of metal. The sisters and another survivor stop a car to escape. The driver of the car is suspicious and forces everyone to return to the scene of the crime. This is where he recognizes the Hammer Killer's body as his brother. Now he himself, with a sickle, begins the pursuit of the two women. Abby, who was tied up by him alone, manages to overpower him, her hurried sister does the rest. Desperate to find a doctor, the sisters return to the village. Here they are tied together by the inhabitants and taken to a mass of a Christian sect. They are rescued by the young woman Becka, who informs them that a demon is raging in this area, feeding on the souls of the victims and the villagers serve him. In the madness of the arrival of their "savior", the villagers kill each other. The two women manage to escape and also manage to stab the Reverend in the end with a stake. When the women disappear, the Reverend stands up and promises to "come back".

anchor_text: The young Bohemian farmer Peter is a simple foolish guy, comparable to Hans Jakob Christoffel from Grimmelshausen's Simplicissimus. From the field with his ox, he is dragged off to war by the recruiters of Mansfield, whom later generations will call the Thirty-Year-Old. Peter manages to escape the Mansfeld men, only to fall into the hands of the Imperial, who are also in need of a man. "Nobody can take away our right to die a heroic death", they chant as they march into battle in good spirits. Together with Matthias, a forced comrade who was the only one to escape a battle, Peter soon gets into trouble again when he is mistaken for a high lord who is supposed to make a good match for the castle master's daughter, Princess Veronika, because of his pretty booty dresses. Matthias, Peter and the farm girl Lenka, whom the two boys met on the way and liked to take with them, find an involuntary quarters in the dungeon of a castle. However, the fortune of war is capricious, and the whims of Lady Fortune (and those of the lady of the castle) carry Peter and his companions the girl dressed as a court jester back to more comfortable rooms. It goes back and forth between the fronts a few more times, until Peter and Lenka can finally escape the merry-go-round of war and seek a quieter, more peaceful life somewhere hidden.
text_a: Peter Kleist, newly arrived in Vienna, is hosted by his uncle Karl Hummel. The young man wants to discover the history of his ancestor, Baron Otto Von Kleist, also known as "The Bloody Baron". The relative then takes him to visit the castle of the deceased and there they meet Eva Arnold, an architecture student. Later, Karl invites his nephew and Eva to dinner. During the meal, mention is made of a certain Elisabeth Hölle, a witch who cursed the baron, after the latter punished her to death. After eating, Peter proposes that Eva return to the castle and try to resurrect her ancestor. Despite some omens, the grandfather does not appear and so, the next day, the two young men try the spell again. This time, unaware of the danger, Eva and Peter awaken the baron. In the meantime, an auction is being held at the Sanguinario's residence. The final bid is for ownership of the fortress itself. The entire property is purchased by a mysterious disabled gentleman, Alfred Becker, who intends to restore it and use it as private property. Eva, meanwhile, is repeatedly stalked by the baron. No one initially believes the girl's story. Later, Karl and Peter change their minds and, to find a solution, decide to consult a medium. The latter manages to "get in touch" with Elisabeth's spirit. The hag reports that the only way to kill the baron is to resurrect a victim of the Bloodsucker. Peter warns Mr. Becker of the danger looming in the castle. The old man doesn't believe the young man's words. Soon it will be discovered that, in reality, Becker fakes everything and that he is the baron himself.
text_b: The Bohemian peasant Petr, while engaged in agricultural work with his ox, is forcibly enlisted and goes to war in the service first of the Mansfeld house, then of the opposite side, the imperial one. A defeat leaves only Petr and the mercenary Matyáš alive, who plunders a camp, not caring whether it belongs to the enemy or his own defeated army, and in it finds the portrait of a beautiful noblewoman. The two men later meet the peasant Lenka, who joins them. The countryside is traversed by soldiers from both sides, and the three are ready to change disguises suddenly to appear to be on the right side every time. Matyáš is disguised as a squire and Lenka as a crier when they encounter Captain Varga who captures them and takes them to a castle, passing Petr off as a wealthy margrave. The lords of the castle are equally ready for changes in the front depending on how the fickle wind of war blows, and alternate seclusion in the vaults for the three unfortunates with the proposal made to Petr to marry the Countess Veronika, the woman in the portrait. But Petr loves, in return, Lenka, and after several misadventures the couple manages to escape from the castle, followed by Matyáš. More proof will be needed, including a near-hanging, before Petr and Lenka can be reunited in peace.

anchor_text: British Colonel Lewis Morgan and his wife Rachael have just been reunited in post-war Germany in Hamburg. It's autumn 1945, the city is completely destroyed and the Allies are working on denazification. Lewis, who is to help rebuild the Hanseatic city, confiscates the undamaged Villa Lubert, but allows the owner and his daughter to live in the attic, even though there is still mutual hostility at first.
text_a: In the harsh winter of 1946, in post-war Germany, Rachael Morgan arrives in the ruins of Hamburg to reunite with her husband Lewis, a British colonel tasked with rebuilding the destroyed city. But as they make their way to their new home, Rachael is stunned by Lewis' unexpected decision: the two will share the big house with its previous owners, a German widower and his struggling daughter. In a crescendo of tension, enmity and pain begin to give way to passion and betrayal.
text_b: Rhoda Montaine learns that her first husband, Gregory Moxley, is still alive, which puts her in trouble as she has remarried Carl, the son of the wealthy C. Phillip Montaine. She turns to Perry Mason for help, but when he visits Moxley, all he finds is his body. Rhoda's being arrested for murder.

anchor_text: The film begins with very harsh images of hundreds of workers working in the open-pit gold mine of Serra Pelada in Brazil, carrying heavy bags on their backs in the rugged terrain, to the rhythmic, dry music. That's when the credits roll. The rest of the film will revolve around three recurring poles: In keeping with the principles of the trilogy, the film is neither directly political in the traditional sense of the term, nor moralizing. It merely presents, avoiding caricature (positive aspects of change are also shown), the world seen through the eyes of the directors, and leaves the spectator free to conduct his own reflection. Philip Glass was much more involved in Powaqqatsi than he was in the first film. It was the same for Godfrey Reggio, because of the great variety of cultures to be represented: Asian, Arab-Muslim, African, South American... The inspiration of the traditional themes is evident, with Philip Glass' final touches giving the film its time unit.
text_a: Reggio reuses familiar techniques from the previous film (slow-motion, time-lapse, overlay) to dramatize the effects of the so-called First World on the Third: displacement, pollution, alienation. The narrative plot is expressed through associations of images declined in sequences in oxymoron between the contemplation of the ancestral archetypes in extinction in modern cultures (cooperative life, sense of joy in work and religious values) and models of modernity such as trains, airliners, coal cars and loneliness. The rural landscape gives way to a crowded, suffocating, accelerating urban industrial hell from Peru to Pakistan. Reggio frames Powaqqatsi in the evocative opening sequence in the Serra Pelada gold mines, where thousands of men, with their clothes and skin soaked in the earth they are moving, carry wet bags over steep slopes in a superhuman effort to provide wealth for their employers. As Glass juxtaposes his paradoxically joyous music, which includes the voices of South American children, some of these men carry one of their exhausted companions out of the pit, head back and arms outstretched.
text_b: The film portrays the hard work along the Douro River, especially at the river shipping unloading stations in the Ribeira district, where ordinary men, women and children bring goods to shore, from coal to agricultural products to crates and barrels, with great effort and persistence. Fishing boats land their catch, livestock is loaded, goods are transhipped between trains and ships, on foot, in trucks or oxcars. People return tired to their homes in the narrow alleys or talk cheerfully to one another. Steel bridges and automobiles can be seen, airplanes fly over the river, and small boats as well as large freighters ply it incessantly. Dangerous situations arise, people clash, policemen roam the scenes. The camera shows the people and their faces and their living conditions, sometimes in quick cuts and sometimes in quiet settings, sometimes in full-length views and sometimes in part-detailed views. In the background, the river flows as the main connecting factor.

anchor_text: The theme of the film is progress and change brought about by the effects of industrialization on the peoples of Asia, Latin America and Africa. Often the camera lingers for minutes on people's day-to-day processes. The first part of the film shows countless men in Serra Pelada working in a gold mine, carrying bags of dirt at first, later a man who is hit by a falling stone. The scenes are often blurred, resulting in a variety of appearances. The scene shifts to Africa and Latin America, giving an insight into the lives of the people of a village. Here, too, the people are bearing something, but not as wage laborers, but as crops for themselves and their village. While the women take care of preparing the food, the men go out in the morning to fish and also to the jungle and to their fields. Laughing and raging children meet us here and in Asia as well. There are festive and religious scenes with a view of mosques and temples, the people who pray there or do their work in the village. A colorful tangle of people moves through the streets, and soon the picture shifts from the idyllic way of life to the hectic operation of modern cities with fast cars, trains, large buildings, television news, crowds watching a military parade, or even rushing to work. There's business and trash everywhere in the crow-infested cities. Full trains, views of a refugee camp and slums, people working in all kinds of jobs, a burnt-out car, people begging. The film ends with footage from the beginning that is blurred and the workers appear to be floating in water. Like his predecessor Koyaanisqatsi and his successor Naqoyqatsi, Powaqqatsi is completely without dialogue. Philip Glass composed the music. It is partly influenced by traditional Asian and African music as well as electronic sounds.
text_a: The film depicts the reactions of the inhabitants during the organization of the evacuation of the village of Matiora on the island of the same name, which is to be submerged by the construction of a dam on the Angara. The villagers' hostility towards the authorities as they begin to exhume the coffins from the island's cemetery to transport them to the new location. The behavior of children who joyfully say goodbye to their village to the point of forgetting the skeleton of the science room. They arrive in their newly built city and start playing ball, riding motorbikes... In front of elderly people who take refuge in prayer to express their grief while others celebrate and bathe in the river for the last time. Resignation of the inhabitants who themselves set fire to their isbas while an old lady cleans it from top to bottom and blooms it as if she were doing her mortuary toilet. Reaction of some who have come to retrieve what they can from the ashes of their burned houses. Nature's resistance to change is symbolized by a giant tree that the chainsaw can't cut, that the axe can't cut, that the cables can't uproot, and that seems to be taking revenge on an engine driver, and that in desperation people try to destroy by burning it, but it's still standing. The same is true of an isba that fails to ignite. Sacrifice of the mother of the head of the evacuation works who disappears with the island: no one finds her when they come to look for her. This upheaval is more or less managed by the imposing stature of the chairman of the rural soviet, Vorontsov, by the team leader Pavel Pinéguine who has to deal with the past embodied by his mother, Daria, and the future, a brand new city but without "soul" yet.
text_b: Reggio reuses familiar techniques from the previous film (slow-motion, time-lapse, overlay) to dramatize the effects of the so-called First World on the Third: displacement, pollution, alienation. The narrative plot is expressed through associations of images declined in sequences in oxymoron between the contemplation of the ancestral archetypes in extinction in modern cultures (cooperative life, sense of joy in work and religious values) and models of modernity such as trains, airliners, coal cars and loneliness. The rural landscape gives way to a crowded, suffocating, accelerating urban industrial hell from Peru to Pakistan. Reggio frames Powaqqatsi in the evocative opening sequence in the Serra Pelada gold mines, where thousands of men, with their clothes and skin soaked in the earth they are moving, carry wet bags over steep slopes in a superhuman effort to provide wealth for their employers. As Glass juxtaposes his paradoxically joyous music, which includes the voices of South American children, some of these men carry one of their exhausted companions out of the pit, head back and arms outstretched.

anchor_text: The film depicts the third and final voyage of the Dutch navigators Jacob van Heemskerk and Willem Barentsz in the years 1596/97: prompted by the sea blockade of the Spaniards, the expedition was to find a new route to India through the Arctic Ocean. But when the ship gets stuck in the ice, the crew is forced to survive the arctic winter in a cramped cabin on the island of Nova Zembla. The events are narrated from the point of view of a participant in the expedition, Gerrit de Veer. His diaries are the basis for much of the film's plot.
text_a: In 1959, French adventurer Pierre-Dominique Gaisseau embarked on a seven-month expedition to New Guinea. Gaisseau is accompanied by six explorers, four soldiers and 60 porters. The expedition will be done on foot and by raft and canoe. Crossing ridges, descending into deep valleys, you reach the Princess Marijke River. The expeditioners encounter pygmies, headhunters, and cannibals and participate in their rituals. The men suffer from swarms of insects and are plagued by leeches. By the time the journey was over, three of the carriers were dead. Eight men have been wounded, 22 are sick.
text_b: The story is inspired by the third voyage of Willem Barentsz and Jacob van Heemskerk (1596-1597) to the Nouvelle Zemble. While the Dutch explorer was looking for a passage to Asia by the northeast route across the Arctic Ocean, the crew, caught in the ice, had to winter on this archipelago before returning to the Netherlands the following year. The story is told from the point of view of Gerrit de Veer, a crew officer, who kept a diary throughout the voyage and published it on his return.

anchor_text: The story is inspired by the third voyage of Willem Barentsz and Jacob van Heemskerk (1596-1597) to the Nouvelle Zemble. While the Dutch explorer was looking for a passage to Asia by the northeast route across the Arctic Ocean, the crew, caught in the ice, had to winter on this archipelago before returning to the Netherlands the following year. The story is told from the point of view of Gerrit de Veer, a crew officer, who kept a diary throughout the voyage and published it on his return.
text_a: In 1959, French adventurer Pierre-Dominique Gaisseau embarked on a seven-month expedition to New Guinea. Gaisseau is accompanied by six explorers, four soldiers and 60 porters. The expedition will be done on foot and by raft and canoe. Crossing ridges, descending into deep valleys, you reach the Princess Marijke River. The expeditioners encounter pygmies, headhunters, and cannibals and participate in their rituals. The men suffer from swarms of insects and are plagued by leeches. By the time the journey was over, three of the carriers were dead. Eight men have been wounded, 22 are sick.
text_b: The film describes the last voyage of Willem Barentsz and Jacob van Heemskerk, between 1596 and 1597, when they and their crew attempted to discover the Northeast Passage to the Indies. However, due to sea ice, they are stranded on the island of Nueva Zembla and have to spend the winter there in Het Behouden Huys (The Safe Home). The story is told through the eyes of Gerrit de Veer, and is based on a diary he published in 1598 after his safe return. Gerrit is portrayed as having a relationship with Catharina Plancius, the daughter of astronomer, cartographer and Reverend Petrus Plancius, who pioneered the concept of the Northeast Passage to reach the Indies. The New Zembla effect, first described by De Veer, is shown in the film, though not historically.

anchor_text: A group of friends are going to a Woodstock-style concert for the weekend. But when they arrive at the scene, they are confronted by a psychopathic killer wearing a mask of former U.S. President Ronald Reagan, determined to finish what he started years earlier.
text_a: Claude Bukowski, a young farmer from Oklahoma, wants to visit New York before he leaves for Vietnam. In Central Park, he befriends a group of hippies. This encounter will turn his life upside down.
text_b: The film is a nostalgic homage to the films of Wes Craven and Tobe Hooper, and follows a group of hippies who escape to the Woodstock Festival to enjoy a week of debauchery, only to be stalked by a radical-thinking psychopath who dresses and talks like Ronald Reagan.

anchor_text: Victoria Jones is a very special girl with a very romantic soul. When she meets a young man named Edward, who's on his way to Baghdad, she doesn't think twice and boards her own plane to the Middle East. Once there, she finds a hotel room and sets off in search of a job and her Edward. One night a wounded man walks into her hotel room and whispers three words to her before he dies: "Lucifer...Bassora... Lefarge... " What are you talking about? The girl finds herself embroiled in a story of international espionage, and against her will only thanks to her will be able to uncover a plot of immense proportions.
text_a: Victoria Jones was a young English stenographer who had just lost her job. Suddenly she meets Edward, a young man who was going to go to Baghdad to work with Dr. Rathbone, an intellectual preserver of culture. Being involved in a typical Romeo and Juliet story, Victoria decides to travel to Baghdad, where she manages to find the young man. However, one night Henry Carmichael, a British agent, enters Victoria's room and dies, entrusting her with three key words in her ear before her expiration. The young woman, horrified, soon finds herself drawn into a world where cunning would be the most effective weapon at hand.
text_b: Edgar Brodie (John Gielgud), a British war hero is declared dead in action by R, the head of the Intelligence Service, so that he can be sent on a secret mission to Switzerland under the identity of Richard Ashenden. To expose a German spy, he is aided by General (Peter Lorre), a vulgar and unscrupulous South American, and Elsa Carrington (Madeleine Carroll), who poses as his wife. After accidentally murdering one of their fellow citizens, Edgar and Elsa consider quitting their job as secret agents but eventually learn the identity of the spy, who is none other than Robert Marvin (Robert Young), a dandy who courted Elsa. Marvin's death may allow the defeat of Germany in World War I but Elsa, traumatized by the death she already has on her conscience, refuses the execution of the spy in a train full of enemy soldiers and attacked by the British air force. Eventually, Marvin dies, and so does the General. Edgar and Elsa are resigning from their duties as undercover agents.

anchor_text: In London, Victoria Jones meets young Edward who is leaving for Baghdad and decides, on a whim, to join him. An inveterate liar, she finds a place as a traveling companion. A secret conference between world powers is to be held in Baghdad, but a shadowy group is plotting to sabotage the event. Victoria unwittingly finds herself entangled in the plot when a secret agent, Carmichael known as "the Fakir", comes to her hotel room to die. In Basra, she met Edward who got her a job in the Olive Branch Association.
text_a: Maurice Castle, an obscure British secret service official seconded to African affairs, leads a life without much glamour and very compartmentalized. Since a leak of confidential documents has occurred, he is suspected, especially since he is married to an African woman. Following an identity check, an investigation is conducted, revealing his identity as a double agent in the service of the nationalist cause in South Africa...
text_b: Victoria Jones was a young English stenographer who had just lost her job. Suddenly she meets Edward, a young man who was going to go to Baghdad to work with Dr. Rathbone, an intellectual preserver of culture.
