anchor_text: Eight intellectual friends meet in a country house. The four men cooked dinner while the women worked out at a gym. Most of them are middle-aged and in various positions in the Department of History at the University of Montreal. They discuss their amorous adventures and reflect on sex and love in humorous conversations. They're all in search of personal happiness, each with their own story. The homosexual Claude is sick. Diane has a sadomasochistic affair with Mario, who joins the group. Pierre met and fell in love with his girlfriend Danielle at an erotic massage club. The successful Dominique has written a book. She is lonely and frustrated and reveals that she has slept with the present Pierre and Rémy. Rémy and Louise, who have been married for a long time, are shocked. Their previously sacred family life is ended by the truth about the husband's busy love life, which his naive wife did not want to know. A world is falling apart for them. Meanwhile, Dominique is spending time with young Alain.
text_a: In this classic love story set against the southern Riviera sun, the two French students Bertrand Griot and Dominique Vallon are the main characters. They both experience their love in a youthful, carefree way. One day, Dominique Bertrand, a law student, meets Luc Ferrand, her charming uncle, a handsome and married gentleman whose experience and skill impress her. The girl and the much older gentleman begin a short-lived but nevertheless very intense romance, which questions and confounds the entire previous relationship network of all parties involved. For the wealthy Luc, however, this short-lived romance was little more than a summer fling, a temporary love affair. All the more disappointed is the still somewhat naive Dominique, when after a short time it's all over again. After that, it seems that there are only losers: Luc has to explain himself to his equally noble and indulgent wife Françoise, and Dominique tries to save the relationship with the rebellious Bertrand.
text_b: In the fall, in the Montreal area, four men and four women, mostly university students, are preparing to spend their weekend at the country house of the couple Louise and Rémy. While the men prepare their meals at home, the women work out in the university's huge gymnasium. In the hours leading up to their reunion, they talk about their lives, including their sexual mores. The same events, reported by men and women, sometimes differ so much that it is not clear where the truth lies. The conversations of the eight characters, which began as soon as they were reunited, continued during their meal and into the early hours of the morning, bringing their share of reflections, discoveries and revelations that would shake the lives of some of them.

anchor_text: This account is partly an autobiographical novel. Among many personal details, Turgenev suggests, for example, that his father married his mother out of self-interest, as she was a wealthy heiress. In the early summer of 1833, the then sixteen-year-old Vladimir Petrovitch was quietly preparing for his university entrance exams at his parents' estate not far from Moscow. He falls madly in love with Zinaida, a 21-year-old woman of singular beauty, whom he observes from behind the fence separating his garden from the park. She is in fact his newly moved neighbor in the adjoining house and lives there with her mother, Princess Zassékine, a penniless person reduced to a miserable existence. The girl frequently gathers in her home a number of worshipers whom she amuses by arousing jealousy or by playing tricks on them to commit a number of foolish acts. One day she meets Pyotr Vasilyevich, Vladimir's father, a handsome and authoritarian man. She succumbs to his charm and they end up a few days later making an appointment in the garden, on the occasion of a night. Vladimir, who has been watching them, goes mad when he sees the terrible truth. Only after resuming his studies will he be able to heal this wound and free himself from the snares of this devastating passion. Both Zinaida and her father suffered a tragic fate: the first, who had married in the meantime, died in childbirth and the second died of a heart attack, not without leaving her son a last letter in which he exhorted him to beware of love.
text_a: The book narrates the sentimental experience of a sixteen-year-old Muscovite boy named Vladimir Petrovich. The love conflict of the protagonist will be triggered after the move of some new neighbors, among whom is Princess Zinaida Alexandrovna, with whom the protagonist falls in love. One of the central themes of the work is the struggle between the rational and the sentimental. In fact, all characters who get carried away by feelings end badly, in contrast to idealized stories with happy endings. The last pages of the novel are still a way of capturing this topic. It is the vision of death that leads the protagonist to reflect on youth and the appreciation of this: Oh youth, youth... You think you have all the treasures of the universe, even sadness serves as a distraction... and yet your days run and disappear without counting without leaving a trace and everything disappears inside you like wax in the sun, like snow. In this way it is described how, although the first love was tragic, Vladimir keeps a "normalized" memory of it. Away from the drama, his life has moved on. This outcome is raised in several questions: "Which of all my hopes have been fulfilled? What else do I have left but the memories of that spring morning storm that so fleetingly passed?
text_b: Dmitry Gurov is a banker from Moscow, is married and has three children. He views women as inferior to men, and his marriage is not a happy one. One day, during a holiday in Yalta, he meets Anna Sergeevna in a restaurant who is having lunch in the company of her dog. Soon Dmitrij and Anna begin a relationship that leads the two protagonists to an involvement as deep as it is unexpected. Unexpected from Dmitrij, a masculinist who has had many relationships and is not prone to sentimentality; unexpected from Anna, who is a shy woman with a 'collegial' air, whose upbringing and culture should have discouraged her from living an extramarital relationship. An intense and tender love blossoms between them, and they begin to nurture feelings for each other that they have never felt before. If the situation is not surprising for Anna, who is young and beautiful and has had no romantic experiences before the "unhappy marriage"; it is surprising for Dmitrij, who finds himself facing for the first time a story that causes him to live his days in function of Anna. Anna experiences the story with great emotional involvement and with strong guilt complexes, unlike Dmitrij, but both realize that they cannot stay away, and this pushes them to look for a solution, a way out, for better or for worse to their story. But time passes and the solution does not appear. All they know is that the end of their love is still a long way off and the complicated part has just begun.

anchor_text: In 1930, at the height of the Great Depression, a new teaching couple, Rusty and Juanita Russell, arrived at the Fort Worth Masonic Home, a well-deserved institution that houses and educates the area's orphans. A World War I veteran and an orphan, he believes in the educational value of sport and its ability to channel the energies of young and, in many cases, resentful and angry boys. An American football team is organized, manages to enter the high school championship despite lacking practically everything and in fact counting on only 12 members. The team will not only be able to compete, but thanks to the innovative strategies of their coach, they will make the most of their qualities until they reach the incredible result of the state finals in Amarillo. He will emerge defeated, receiving the honors of all, and being celebrated on his return by the entire institute, within which, for a long time, many had been perplexed about the expediency of this enterprise.
text_a: In the United States of 1929 and during the Great Depression, war veteran Rusty Russell moves with his family to Fort Worth, Texas, to take up teaching positions with his wife at the Masonic Orphanage. The orphanage cares for 150 children, operates a printing press, and manages a farm. Russell organizes a men's American football team despite not having a field, equipment (only one ball) or clothing and manages to enroll it in the metropolitan championship of the area, at the same time that with his wife they manage to improve the academic performance of all the children. Thanks to the "extended offensive", a strategy invented by Russell to counteract the underweight of other teams, the team starts a winning streak and becomes known throughout the country, even by President Franklin D. Roosevelt.
text_b: A high school in the small American town of Shiloh has a problem: According to some parents, its football team is not as good as it could be. The coach Grant Taylor was no longer able to lead the team. This opinion is confirmed when one of the best players leaves and the team loses the first games of the new season. When the coach is in the office a little longer one evening because of personal problems, he learns that his dismissal is being demanded. He also has bad luck: his doctor says he won't be able to have children, his car is broken, and so on. Even as a believing Christian, he is desperate and struggles with God. But he puts his life, his problems and everything else in God's hands. He designs a new concept for his team, which says that they should play to serve God. And God helps him the team wins because they play with the right attitude in their heart, but more than that also in the players and students something changes. Many find God and start a new life. God answers the prayers of another man who prays for the students. And also the small and big problems in the private sphere of the trainer are solved. Their explanation for the happy turn is simple: because they want to serve God, God leads the team of Shilow Eagles to the final there await the undefeated Giants, but even these they can defeat in faith.

anchor_text: It's the time of the Great Depression. The Masonic Home for Orphans in Fort Worth, Texas, has a pretty lame football team. When Rusty Russell gets to high school, he's gonna be her coach and he wants to lead her to the championship. Little does anyone know that in these troubled times, he's going to make the orphanage the pride of Fort Worth.
text_a: In 1930, a teacher couple arrived at the Masonic orphanage in Fort Worth, Texas. The husband, also an orphan, creates an alternative American football team called The Mighty Mites with twelve boys from the orphanage. Turning the enormous feeling of abandonment into a strong desire to win for these youngsters and although they started from nothing, they go against all odds and thanks to the tactical ingenuity of their coach reach the final of the state championship.
text_b: A coach who coaches a team of boys who play football helps them to do teamwork, to respect each other, to believe in themselves through faith in God. Will the coach be able to solve his problems and make his team win?

anchor_text: The film is set in the port district of Porto on the banks of the Douro. The story is told from the point of view of children. Schoolboys Carlitos and Eduardo are fighting for the favor of the same girl, Teresinha. Eduardo is rude and mean, while Carlitos is reserved and good-natured. The rivalry is growing. During a harmless prank on the railroad track, Eduardo slips and falls to the side of the tracks. To the kids playing further away, it has to look like Carlitos pushed him under the tracks and killed him. After that, he's an outcast. The desperate Carlitos plans to escape at the port as a blind passenger. But the shopkeeper had seen it all and cleared up the misunderstanding. Eduardo comes out of the hospital cheerful and the children can play in the streets again, always using their beloved meaningless countdown rhyme Aniki Bóbó.
text_a: Aniki Bóbó narrates the adventures and loves of some young people from the slums of Porto. Specifically, the love of a shy pre-adolescent for a girl from his school, a crush that will make him transgress the limits set by adults (by stealing a rag doll, whose crime will torment him). It is the story of a love triangle whose vertex is Teresinha and at its base Carlitos and Eduardo.[1] The old streets of the slum where they live, in the riverside area of the city of Porto, are the scene of their adventures. It's where they're happy and they feel free. Only the authoritarian rigidity of their teacher at school and the control of the neighborhood policeman, who watches over them, undoes their longing.[2]
text_b: Millionaire Riccardo Bolzoni is threatened by strangers who are trying to kidnap him. On the advice of his friend Peppino, the gym teacher at the local high school, he hides in the south of the country disguised as a simple worker. The Catholic country school in Bolzoni's shelter, however, hosts a larger number of students who have fallen through the high school exams. They are given the opportunity to follow up their schooling with a holiday course lasting several weeks. One of those students is Ricardo's son, Carlo. Joining the teaching staff around the chaplain Don Marcello, a half-blind professor and a crippled gym teacher, is the attractive 26-year-old English teacher Monica Sebastiani, the priest's niece. Immediately, a competition for the favor of the educator begins. Carlo falls in love with his teacher, who at first coldly rejects him. At the same time, Monica is also courted by Carlos' unfaithful father, Riccardo. At the end of the film, during a school performance, there is a passionate love affair between Monica and Carlo. Riccardo is found in his hiding place by the bandits and kidnapped, while his wife runs away with Peppino.

anchor_text: The film illustrates the adventures and loves of some children, between ten and twelve years old, in the city of Porto. All students in the same class, they meet after school to swim in the harbor. A rivalry develops between the sensitive Carlitos and the more enterprising Eduardito, the leader of the gang, to win the attention of Teresinha, the pretty little model girl. Weakened physically, Carlitos resolves to steal a doll that Teresinha covets in order to win her favor. It's a journey into childhood, through the eye of the camera: the eye of memory.
text_a: To escape the kidnappings in person, a rich industrialist from the North, the commendator Riccardo Bolzoni, moves with his family to Martina Franca where, despite living disguised as a homeless man, he continues to frequent the lover and tries, at the same time, to seduce the beautiful English teacher of his son Carlo. There, the entrepreneur will call himself by the surname Marconcini, confident that this will keep him safe from any kidnapping attempt. The boy, really in love with her, will be preferred. The commander will lose his wife (who fled with the secretary) and his mistress, as well as the teacher he will choose precisely Carlo, being kidnapped despite all his precautions.
text_b: Aniki Bóbó narrates the adventures and loves of some young people from the slums of Porto. Specifically, the love of a shy pre-adolescent for a girl from his school, a crush that will make him transgress the limits set by adults (by stealing a rag doll, whose crime will torment him). It is the story of a love triangle whose vertex is Teresinha and at its base Carlitos and Eduardo.[1] The old streets of the slum where they live, in the riverside area of the city of Porto, are the scene of their adventures. It's where they're happy and they feel free. Only the authoritarian rigidity of their teacher at school and the control of the neighborhood policeman, who watches over them, undoes their longing.[2]

anchor_text: Sylvia is left by her husband Daniel for another woman after twenty years of marriage. Her friend Bernadette then founds a "Jane Austen Book Club" with which she wants to change Sylvia's mind. In this club, the six novels of the British writer Jane Austen will be discussed. Six novels, six members. Sylvia's best friend, single dog breeder Jocelyn, brings her random friend Grigg as the only man in the round. Whose only motivation for joining is to score with Jocelyn and get to know her better. Sylvia's lesbian daughter Allegra only joins the club for her mother's sake. Prudie, an unhappily married French teacher who is in danger of having an affair with one of her students, is sixth in the round. Each month, the club members meet to discuss one of Austen's works. Gradually it becomes clear that the love lives of the individual club members coincide with the storylines of the novels.
text_a: In a long introductory sequence, four women living in Paris at a crucial time in their lives are introduced in flashback. The real action begins in Laura's luxurious country house in Provence. Laura has invited her long-time friend and colleague Sarah, 16 years her junior, to join her. Enjoy the reunion, sunbathe by the pool, curl up in the hammock. Julienne and Caroline, two younger and lesser-known actresses, are coming to visit. You're sitting together at lunch, and your mind goes back to last year in Paris. Sarah has just finished a film and the affair with her director, is about to receive a film award and is about to take on a new role. Then there is the relationship with Gregoire, an oncologist, which takes place on a purely platonic level, and at the same time a romance is developing with the German writer Heinrich Grün. Laura invited the three men and their girlfriends to the award ceremony. The other two women have reached a low point in their careers. In Julienne's career she is a successful and popular actress things are tight and her current relationship with a young screenwriter is not without tension. The ambitious Caroline, perhaps because of a lack of talent, doesn't get very far. She argues with her ex-husband for custody of their son; she makes unveiled advances to the director of her new film. In a relaxed atmosphere, women talk about men, love, successes and failures in life and in the movies.
text_b: Bernadette, who is in her fifties and has been divorced six times, got the idea of starting a book club when she met Prudie, a young married French teacher who was very elegant, at a film festival about Jane Austen. The concept is that each of the six members host the group in turn once a month, to discuss among themselves the six Austen novels. The other members of the club are Sylvia, a 40-something housewife recently separated from her husband Daniel, a lawyer and racer, after more than twenty years of marriage; her 20-year-old lesbian daughter Allegra; Jocelyn, a happily single, self-control-obsessed Rhodesian dog breeder, who has been friends with Sylvia since childhood; and Grigg, a science fiction fan whom Jocelyn has invited to join them in the hope that he and Sylvia will form a matching couple.

anchor_text: Bill Rago, a brilliant advertising man, is nevertheless fired after missing out on a huge contract for his company. Bill becomes unemployed and receives an offer to teach at a military school. Its mission: to cultivate the intelligence of young recruits supposedly intellectually disadvantaged. Through a competition of circumstances, Bill began to teach his students the plays of William Shakespeare. Shakespeare's learning, frowned upon by the young soldiers' superiors, will nevertheless be used for the physical and intellectual development of these young recruits more abandoned than beasts and idiots...
text_a: Bill is a young man who spends his days watching videos that criticize excessive consumption or world overpopulation while performing strenuous physical exercises. His parents are pressuring him to get a job and become independent. Bill has abnormal and/or aggressive behavior with people, except with his friend Evan, which fuels his revulsion with human hypocrisy. One day, Bill assembles an arsenal of assault weapons, equips himself with Kevlar armor, and lays out a meticulous plan to carry out a ruthless massacre, unstoppable by the police.
text_b: Bill Rago is a recently fired, divorced advertising executive who reluctantly accepts a temporary job as a teacher at a U.S. Army training base. His class is semi-illiterate, and Bill has only six weeks to teach the basics of comprehension and the English language to his lazy students. Despite Sergeant Cass's distrust and the loss of a brilliant trainee turned fugitive ex-crack dealer, the professor will, through the teaching of Shakespeare's Hamlet, win the heart and esteem of the class, and will accept the extension of the contract.

anchor_text: A man of few words arrives at a Madrid airport and is sent on a mysterious journey through Spain. He repeatedly encounters similarly mysterious people who exchange matchboxes with him for ritualised identification flakes. These contain small slips of paper with three rows of letters and numbers on each. The man reads the notes and swallows them with espresso coffee. While his companions at the meetings make pseudo-philosophical and poetic monologues (in which the spoken word becomes almost incomprehensible because the background noises gradually predominate), while some sentences are varied again and again, the Loner says almost no word. Between meetings, he sits in cafes, where he always orders two espressi, looks at pictures in the museum, spends the nights awake and dressed in bed, and does tai chi exercises. He always rejects the attempts to seduce a naked woman who occasionally appears in the hotel room: "Never while I'm working". Eventually, the loner travels by train and, through another contact, into the Spanish wilderness to a heavily guarded estate with black limousines parked outside. A helicopter that has been seen time and time again lands. The loner enters the estate "through his imagination". He has a very brief, cryptic conversation with an American who contemptuously lets it be known that he doesn't have the slightest idea what makes the world go round. The Loner strangles the American with a guitar string as ordered, then returns to Madrid. Once there, he changes his clothes in a toilet at the Atocha station, disposes of his "service train" in the locker and leaves the place.
text_a: The work tells, in the form of a personal diary, alternating poetry and postmodern prose, the wanderings of a man who escapes from a guarded building and travels through a distorted universe that resembles the aftermath of an indefinite calamity. It becomes apparent from the first entries in the diary that the narrator/protagonist escapes from what appears to be an imprisonment in a building composed of guards and staff and from which people are inexplicably taken in order to be thrown into pits. The fugitive leaves the camp to go to the nearest train station and then begins a journey that he records in a found booklet Bible that he turns into a diary. Gradually, during the journey, a feeling of paranoia follows and the idea of being chased becomes an increasingly central concern. However, there are no pursuers to identify and the alleged hunt remains a mystery until the end. At the same time, the environment seems to hint at a decadent futuristic state of a totalitarian nature. The course is plotted indefinitely, although the oblique references create a sense of space-time void. The narrator seems to move forward while being engulfed in his own nightmarish fantasies. Z213: Exit ends with a description of a sacrifice where the protagonist and a hungry crowd celebrating, roast a lamb on a spit, cut and strip its still-bleeding body and remove its entrails as if to observe a sacred rite. The atmosphere is accentuated by the establishment of a desert land that could be (it is never explicit) the result of a war that left the landscape in ruins. However, the overall impression is reminiscent of a spiritual quest or an eschatological experience.
text_b: A lonely man, probably of African origin, accepts a mysterious contract at a Paris airport: he must go to Madrid, where his next contact will manifest itself in the possession of a violin. The man walks around the city in an elegant two-piece suit, especially in the Reina Sofía Museum, which seems to attract him magnetically. A succession of succinct messages hidden in matchboxes with the evocative name of The Boxer leads him from station to station in southern Spain: from Seville, where he retrieves a historic guitar that once belonged to Manuel El Sevillano and from which he removes a string, to Ocaña, a village in the province of Almería, where the guitar is passed on to a Mexican. There, a final contact finally points him to his target, who appears to be a wealthy American financier holed up in a secluded and overprotected house; the man then executes his contract by breaking into that house and strangling the American with the guitar string. He returned to Madrid, to the Reina Sofía Museum, then changed in the toilets of the Atocha station, from which he emerged wearing simple sports clothes flanked by the symbol of the Cameroon football team.

anchor_text: A killer originally from Cameroon follows a series of improbable clues to carry out a mysterious criminal mission. Along the way, he meets a series of envoys of different nationalities who instruct him on his next move and give him their own view of reality. The killer is sent from France to Madrid, then to Seville, to Almería, and finally to the Tabernas Desert, where he reaches his target.
text_a: Howard Wakefield is a lawyer who has everything going for him. A beautiful situation, a woman he's madly in love with and beautiful children. Until one day, depressed, he decides to drop everything and secretly settle in front of his house to spy on his loved ones.
text_b: A man of few words arrives at Madrid-Barajas airport and is sent on a strange journey through Spain. He always ends up meeting mysterious people who share matchboxes with him. These contain slips of paper with letters and numbers. Once they're read, they're swallowed. While his companions have poetic and philosophical interventions when they meet him, ranging from a few sentences to lengthy monologues, the loner says practically not a word. Between meetings he sits in cafes, looks at paintings in museums, spends nights awake and dressed in bed and does Tai Chi exercises.

anchor_text: Muhsin is a music arranger and is passionate about classical music. Professionally, he meets the self-proclaimed hit singer Ali. Different tastes in music and cultural backgrounds lead to ridiculous dialogues and arguments. With Muhsin's help, Ali moves up the career ladder. At the end of the film, the young Ali is a celebrated star, while the cultured Muhsin still works as a simple organizer.
text_a: Ali Nazik seeks the help of the reclusive record producer Muhsin Kanadikirik to become a Türkücü (folk singer). Muhsin, who is a fan of artists such as Müzeyyen Senar and Safiye Ayla, is against the arabesque-type music played by the young Nazik. Muhsin finally agrees to work with the boy with the aim of making him the new star of Turkish music.[5]
text_b: The film chronicles the story of boxing icon Cassius Clay/Muhammad Ali from 1964 to 1974, which includes the heavyweight title shot of Sonny Liston, his conversion to Islam, his opposition to the Vietnam War, his banishment from boxing, his return to fight Joe Frazier in 1971 and finally his reclaiming of the title against George Foreman in 1974 in Kinshasa, Zaire. It also includes the social incidents that triggered the assassinations of Malcolm X and Martin Luther King, Jr.

anchor_text: The film tells the love story of Annika and Pär, two teenagers aged 13-14 years. Both of whom will begin to experience adult life, with hard and strong emotions.
text_a: The film follows five homeless children in Bucharest who struggle with addiction to Aurolac, a repair material used as an inhalant drug.
text_b: A love story between Annika, a thirteen-year-old girl from a middle-class family, and Pär, a fifteen-year-old boy from the countryside.

anchor_text: The film tells the true story of William Wilberforce, a young Christian abolitionist elected to the House of Commons in the late 18th century. There he campaigned for the abolition of the slave trade in the United Kingdom. He is supported by John Newton and William Pitt. The film begins with a seriously ill Wilberforce recovering from his illness in Bath, Somerset. With him is his cousin, Henry Thornton. Here he is introduced to his future wife, Barbara Spooner. Despite his initial reluctance, she manages to elicit William's past life story. The action then jumps to 1782. From there, William recounts the events that brought him to his current situation. He is an ambitious and popular Member of Parliament when he is persuaded by his friends William Pitt, Thomas Clarkson, Hannah More and others to press ahead with the dangerous task of abolishing the slave trade. This made him unpopular in the House of Commons with the MPs of the cities of London, Bristol and Liverpool, who earned very well on the slave trade, and initially succumbed several times to his request. Exhausted and frustrated by his inability to make a difference in Parliament, William contracted colitis. After this narrative, the plot has returned to the starting point. Having given up hope of changing the slave trade, he is considering retiring from politics altogether. Barbara convinces him to keep fighting, because if he doesn't, no one else will be able to. A few days later, William and Barbara marry, and with new courage and hope of success, he resumes the fight, supported by Thornton, Clarkson, and James Stephen. After 20 years and many attempts, in February 1807, he succeeded in passing the Slave Trade Act, which prohibited the slave trade in the British Empire.
text_a: The film begins in 1797. William Wilberforce, seriously ill, went on holiday to Bath, Somerset, with his cousin, Henry Thornton Wilberforce. This is where William is introduced to his future wife, Barbara Spooner. Although he resists at first, she convinces him to tell her his story. William's account takes him back 15 years, to 1782, and allows him to take stock of the events that led him to where he is today. An ambitious and popular Member of Parliament (MP), William was persuaded by his friends William Pitt, Thomas Clarkson, Hannah More, and others to take up the dangerous issue of the British slave trade. This led him to become very unpopular in the House of Commons among MPs representing the interests of the slave trade in London, Bristol and Liverpool. Exhausted and frustrated at not being able to change anything in the government, William's health deteriorated (in the film he is portrayed as suffering from chronic colitis), bringing his story back to 1797. Having almost given up all hope, William considered quitting politics for good. Barbara convinces him to continue fighting, William being the only one in her eyes capable of completing this fight. A few days later, William and Barbara were married. William, with renewed strength and hope, resumed the fight against the slave trade where he had left it, aided by Thornton, Clarkson and James Stephen. After 20 years of campaigning and many attempts to get his text passed, he was finally responsible for a bill passed by Parliament in 1807, which abolished the slave trade in the British Empire forever.
text_b: William Guest, a militant libertarian, returns home after a heated discussion with his comrades about the prospects of the future social revolution, experiences a strange moment of suspension between dream and reality, during which he finds himself transported to 21st-century London. Here, confused and curious, he meets the boatman Dick and the old librarian Hammond, who explain to him the characteristics, extraordinary for the anarchist William, of their new society, from which capitalist exploitation and authoritarian institutions are definitively and unanimously banned. In this material and spiritual journey, through a peaceful and serene "new world", particularly touching are the pages in which an imaginary London is described, no longer the great industrial establishments and the cathedrals of capitalism, cleaned of the fumes, dirt and darkness of the profit society, immersed and inserted in a lush countryside and crossed by a Thames that has become a seraphic place of delight.

text_a: The film is set in England between 1788 and 1789. It was during this period that the long reign of George III of Hanover (1760-1820) experienced a serious internal crisis: the sovereign, in fact, went completely out of his mind. The Prime Minister, William Pitt the Younger, takes the necessary measures for the King's care and to keep Parliament in the dark as much as possible about his real state of health. The risk Pitt fears running is that Prince George of Wales, with whom he is in open conflict, may assume the regency. While his son plots with the Whig opposition leader Charles Fox to introduce a motion in the House of Commons to suspend the sovereign from his duties, George III is entrusted to the care of Dr. Willis, patient but severe physician, to the point of looking directly into the king's face and giving him orders, in his counter-current way of applying the principles of medical science.
text_b: Elected as a young MP in the British Parliament, Wilberforce is about to leave this role following a strong conversion to devote himself to the faith as an Anglican pastor; he then understands, encouraged also by his friends, that he can devote himself to his religious vocation also in the political arena. In fact, by not giving up his seat in parliament, he begins a series of political campaigns inspired by his Christian faith. He is the first politician to take an interest in the abolition of black slavery. The struggle has been going on for over 30 years, and William is fighting against everything and everyone. Laughed at by many, he also goes through difficult times, even becoming sick. It's the presence of his wife Barbara, supporting him, standing by him. Also noteworthy is the encouragement and spiritual guidance he receives from John Newton, a repentant former slave, who, now elderly and blind, gives him the needed support not to give up. Newton will write the famous hymn of praise to God in thanksgiving for his conversion, Amazing Grace, which gives the film its title. The lyrics of this song refer to the author's feelings of remorse for his past as a slave. In 1807 the slave trade was abolished and in 1833 slavery was abolished in the British colonies, three days before Wilberforce's death.

anchor_text: A group of filmmakers come to the small town of Waterford in the US state of Vermont to shoot the historical film The Old Mill. They discover on the spot that the mill intended as a backdrop has been destroyed by a fire. Director Walt Price commissioned screenwriter Joseph Turner White to write screenplay adaptations that set the story in a fire station. However, White suffers from writer's block. The lead actress, Claire Wellesley, is demanding an additional salary of $800,000. Dollars if she's to perform in a nude scene. The male lead Bob Barrenger seduces the underage Carla, while White enters into a relationship with local Annie Black. Film producer Marty Rossen is coming to inspect the shoot.
text_a: The film centers on two sharecroppers, Lennie and George, who in the 1930s, at the height of the Depression, live on the road, moving from ranch to ranch in search of work. One has the brain of a child but the strength of a potter; the other tries to protect him from life's obstacles. Difficulties that won't be long in coming, given how easily Lennie gets into trouble. Landing on one last farm, Lennie, attracted by the seductive wife of the chief's son, and unable to control his own strength, accidentally kills her. George, torn by grief, in order not to abandon his friend to the lynching, decides to end his existence.
text_b: The shooting of a movie has to be moved from New Hampshire to little Waterford, due to problems with its lead actor. Since the movie is called The Old Mill, they need one, but it turns out the local one burned down decades ago. The film's director (William H. Macy) puts her faith in her screenwriter's (Philip Seymour Hoffman) ability to alter the script, but the screenwriter is blocked. The local librarian, Annie Black (Rebecca Pidgeon), is trying to help him. While the lead actress (Sarah Jessica Parker) says she won't do a nude scene unless they pay her an extra $800,000, the perverted lead actor (Alec Baldwin), seduces Carla (Julia Stiles), a village teenager and they have a car accident when they go together. The screenwriter witnesses the accident and hesitates whether to tell the truth, which would sink the actor's career, or keep quiet as the film's producer (David Paymer) suggests.

anchor_text: The thief and petty thief Tony Fait has stolen a handful of valuable "black diamonds" from a bank as part of an assignment. The problem is, after his client's dead and Tony's wanted, there's not much he can do with them. But it seems the entire underworld is after the diamonds. On several occasions, Tony's life is put in danger, and his young daughter is kidnapped by gangsters. He enlists the help of Taiwanese intelligence officer Su, who is eager to return the stones to their rightful owner, his government. Together, the two pursue their goals up and down through all the dangers and obstacles that still stand in their way, ultimately bringing both the Stones and Tony's daughter to safety.
text_a: Tony Fait is a skilled jewel thief who stages a high-profile heist to steal black diamonds. Also interested in the loot are Su, a secret agent from Taiwan, and Ling, an arms dealer who was Su's former comrade-in-arms. The fight for the jewels is fierce, but when Ling kidnaps Fait's daughter Vanessa, Su teams up with rival Fait to rescue her. Su tells Fait that the black diamonds are nothing more than crystals of synthetic plutonium, produced by his country: a single diamond may be able to destroy the entire planet. Ling wants to sell them to the highest bidder, so he's holding an auction in a hangar. Fait and Su, discovering the location of the hangar, rush to the scene, and manage to rescue Vanessa. Su manages to retrieve the plutonium after killing Ling in a vicious martial arts fight. Fait, reflecting on all that has happened, decides to stop stealing and promises to start an honest life.
text_b: Three boys (Kevin is a clerk, Rob repairs appliances, and Sam) delight in planning perfect robberies that they do not execute. One day, they are pursued by the notorious criminal Leo Gillete who steals a plan to rob a jewelry store and forces them to work together to seize a treasure trove of bonds stored in a bank. At first the young people decide to cooperate, but the understanding will gradually become more complicated.

text_a: Diamond dealer Kyle Miller and his wife Sarah are a wealthy couple. Together with their daughter, Avery, they enjoy a seemingly happy life in their spacious mansion. But the family has problems: Avery disrespects her parents and sneaks out of the house to go to a party even though she is forbidden to do so. Sarah too is dissatisfied with her situation; she is not fulfilling her role as a housewife. A brutal gang of criminals invades the mansion in hopes of a major coup and takes the family hostage. The situation does not develop as planned by the kidnappers: Kyle does not want to cooperate, and his wife recognizes one of the masked criminals. Jonah, who she had an affair with in his eyes, had security technology installed in the house. The criminals first claim that they need money for a kidney transplant, but one of them later reveals that he is a drug dealer who needs money because he has lost a large sum of money. Kyle pretends to be penniless; the vault in which the gangsters suspect money and diamonds is empty. Jonah suffers from a mental illness; he imagines that Sarah has feelings for him as he does for her. In the course of the clashes, two thugs shoot each other, Avery incapacitates the gangster bride, the injured Kyle is able to escape with his wife from the burned-out property, in which the Jonah he shot dies amidst burning bundles of money. The family lies in the poor, police and rescue workers meet.
text_b: Tony Fait navigates the perils of business with a dedication only surpassed by the love he feels for his young daughter Vanessa. After carrying out a complex heist involving a stash of black diamonds, Fait soon finds himself pitted against Su, a Taiwanese government agent who has searched for the extraordinary stones halfway around the world. Su, who is as dangerous as he is ruthless, isn't just looking for the diamonds, he's looking for the ruthless Ling, his former partner, who kidnaps Fait's daughter with the intention of trading her for the stolen jewels. When word gets out that Fait has hidden the jewels with his expert witness Archie, the diamonds immediately end up in the hands of a powerful capo. With his back against the wall, Fait mobilizes his team, suspends his strict no-gun policy, and prepares to unleash all the forces at his disposal to save his daughter. But Fait realizes that Su might be his best hope for Vanessa to come home alive. Opponents by principle but allies by circumstance, the master thief and the enigmatic agent join forces to wage war on their common enemy: Su to take revenge on the man who once wanted to kill him and Fait to rescue Vanessa.

anchor_text: Hester Street, which gives the film its title, is a street in Manhattan where many of the Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe came to live in the late nineteenth century. This is also the home of Yankle, who, since arriving in the United States, has assimilated so quickly into American culture that he has changed his name to Jake. Although legally married, he maintains a relationship with Mamie, a dancer. His routine is disrupted when his wife, Gitl, joins him with their baby Yossele. Unlike Jake and Yossele (whom her father calls "Joey"), Gitl has difficulty fitting in: she insists on dressing and styling her hair according to Jewish custom, and for this she is constantly scolded and insulted by her husband. Also, Jake keeps seeing Mamie, which Gitl finds out later thanks to Mrs. Kavarsky, a neighbor. Jake and Gitl eventually divorce, and the woman remarries Bernstein, a fellow Talmudic scholar whom Jake had given hospitality.
text_a: It tells the story of Jewish immigrants who arrive in New York City in 1896 from Eastern Europe and who live on Hester Street in Manhattan. Young Yankle arrives in the United States for the first time, quickly assimilates into American culture and becomes Jake, an American man. Jake starts having an affair with Mamie, a dancer. His wife, Gitl, later arrives in the country with their son Yossele and has difficulty assimilating to the culture and being part of a new society. Tension arises in their marriage as Jake continually berates and abuses Gitl. Besides, Jake keeps seeing Mamie in secret. Then, Gitl discovers her husband's infidelity through Mrs. Kavarsky, a neighbor. Jake and Gitl eventually divorce, so Gitl takes all of Mamie's money and marries Bernstein, a staunch traditionalist. At the end of the film, she is assimilated on a personal and linguistic level, walking down the street with Bernstein and Yossele (now known as Joey), speaking English and showing her hair. But now she's free of Jake, who in turn married Mamie.
text_b: The film stars two freshmen from the University of New England, Walter Gibson aka Gib and Alison Bradbury. Gib doesn't study hard, and he's about to fail English. Alison, who's in the same class as him, is the classic perfect girl, all healthy food and timetables. A week before Christmas, the two of them must reach California. Gib was invited by his best friend Lance, who sent him a picture of a beautiful girl willing to spend a night with him (A Certain Thing, hence the film's original title. The English title has nothing to do with the plot. Alison, on the other hand, wants to go visit her boyfriend, Jason, who's studying to be a lawyer in Los Angeles. To save money, the two ask other kids on campus for a ride and end up staying with the same couple, Gary and Mary. During the journey, however, tired of their constant bickering, Gary dumps them in the middle of nowhere. Thus begins the boys' quest to reach California after a thousand perils.

anchor_text: In 1896, Yankel Bogovnik, a Russian Jew who had immigrated to the United States three years earlier, moved to Hester Street on New York's Lower East Side. Accustomed to American life, he learned English, anglicized his first name to Jake, and even shaved off his beard. He works as a tailor for $12 a week. That salary, he's saving to bring his wife and son to America. Meanwhile, he falls in love with a dancer named Mamie Fein. Nevertheless, Bogovnik is delighted when he learns that his wife and son have found a way to make the trip from Russia to New York. His happiness, however, is greatly diminished when he finds that his wife Gitl is not at all Americanized and that she is nothing like Mamie. Can their marriage survive under these conditions?

text_b: The film stars two freshmen from the University of New England, Walter Gibson aka Gib and Alison Bradbury. Gib doesn't study hard, and he's about to fail English. Alison, who's in the same class as him, is the classic perfect girl, all healthy food and timetables. A week before Christmas, the two of them must reach California. Gib was invited by his best friend Lance, who sent him a picture of a beautiful girl willing to spend a night with him (A Certain Thing, hence the film's original title. The English title has nothing to do with the plot. Alison, on the other hand, wants to go visit her boyfriend, Jason, who's studying to be a lawyer in Los Angeles. To save money, the two ask other kids on campus for a ride and end up staying with the same couple, Gary and Mary. During the journey, however, tired of their constant bickering, Gary dumps them in the middle of nowhere. Thus begins the boys' quest to reach California after a thousand perils.

anchor_text: France in the colonial era. While Madame Brisson belongs to high society in French-controlled Algiers in North Africa, she lives a double life as Musa Samarra, a notorious representative of the local nightlife who runs a shady establishment. Her daughter, Adrienne, did not want to know about all this, and so she was sent to a convent school in the south of France to be educated from her earliest childhood. Now, however, she has grown up and is eager to visit her mother at their invitation. Adrienne meets the half-silky and unscrupulous Nicola Molescu, whom she does not know is her mother's lover. Molescu has a lot on his plate, and that's why the French judiciary is interested in him. Anyway, the prosecutor, René Cadillac, attacked Adrienne because there was hope in her shadow. Arriving in Algiers, Madame Brisson is delighted to see her daughter, who has grown over the years. She realizes that her life should not continue like this and hopes to leave behind her nocturnal existence as "Musa Samarra" through Adrienne's presence. However, Molescu does not want his beloved to leave his previous life, because he has new, sinister plans. He abducts Adrienne and sends her to his tavern to work as a prostitute. Madame Brisson is desperately trying to free her daughter from his clutches without having to expose her double existence to him. And so it comes promptly to a confrontation with Molescu, which ends deadly for Adrienne's mother. However, the villain must also pay for his shameful act: Mira's brother, a girl who had been raped, stabs him to death. At the last moment, prosecutor Cadillac appears and rescues Adrienne from the hands of the traffickers. Both have fallen in love with each other over the course of events and can now begin a future together.
text_a: The sadistic and half-mad Alain de Maletroit, descendant of an old aristocracy, has kept his brother Edmond imprisoned in a hidden dungeon of his mansion for 20 years. This is his revenge for allowing Edmond to win the love of the woman whom Alain worshipped. Edmond's daughter Blanche, who was raised by Alain after her mother died in childbirth, lives in the belief that her father also died early. To complete his revenge on Edmond, Alain lures the bully Denis de Beaulieu into his house to force him and Blanche into a marriage. He wants to make this young woman's life a living hell and torment Edmond with it. However, it turns out that beneath the young man's rough shell lies a good heart, and after initial reluctance, Blanche and Denis fall in love. Together, they try to free themselves from the clutches of Alain and his brutal servants. The only help they get is from Voltan, the jailer who has secretly made life easier for Edmond in captivity. But the lovers' escape fails. Alain decides to murder Edmond and the two of them, and for this purpose locks them together in the dungeon, whose walls can be moved by an ingenious mechanism and threaten to crush the inmates. With his last strength, Voltan, who was badly wounded in the battle with Alain's servants, manages to overcome Alain and free the three.
text_b: In Algiers lives Mrs. Brisson who leads a double life, during the day she is an elegant lady, but at night she becomes Musa Samarra manager of a notorious club. The woman, in order to preserve her daughter Adrienne, had her study in a boarding school in the south of France, but now the ragza is returning to Algeria to find her mother. During the crossing, the girl accidentally meets Molescu, a shady individual, who collaborates with her mother. Prosecutor René Cadillac is also on board, investigating the affairs of Mrs. Brisson and Molescu. The meeting between mother and daughter is full of emotions and Mrs. Brisson decides to abandon her double life in order to take full advantage of her daughter. Molescu, worried about business, wants to stop him by any means and decides to kidnap Adrienne to blackmail her mother. The woman manages to save her daughter but Molescu stabs her to death during a fight. Meanwhile, the man is stabbed in turn by the brother of Mira, a girl he raped. The film ends with the prosecutor René Cadillac, who brings to safety Adrienne, with whom he has fallen in love.

anchor_text: In the 1960s, Mrs. O'Brien (Jessica Chastain) receives a telegram informing her of the death of one of her 19-year-old sons. Later, an architect in a large American city, Jack O'Brien (Sean Penn), his eldest son, recalls his childhood with analepsia and wonders about the world, which leads to a long digression where he talks about the origin of the world, dinosaurs, volcanic eruptions, birth, limbo, and the end of the universe. As a teenager in 1950s Texas, Jack encounters the authoritarian upbringing of an ambitious and individualistic father (Brad Pitt) who struggles to take an interest in his family. This engineer dreamed of being a great pianist. The mother, a loving and sensitive housewife, tries by her love to give her three children faith in life, but her submission to her tyrannical husband is considered a betrayal by Jack. Desperate to regain his father's respect, the son gradually discovers a violent, dark side to his father.
text_a: Jack and Bette Tremont (Jack Lemmon and Olympia Dukakis) are a quiet retired couple where the housewife handles the couple's daily life with an iron fist. Until the day Bette was hospitalized for a heart attack. Overnight, the quiet Jack Tremont finds himself alone at home for the first time in his life, master of his own destiny. Of course, such freedom may at first make him a little wary. Therefore, he decides to call on his children John (Ted Danson) and Annie (Kathy Baker) to give him a hand in the gestures of daily life. John is a successful businessman, always up and down in his career, and it is in these circumstances that he becomes closer (more than he has ever been) to his father: he teaches his father to become more independent and self-reliant, more in his image, and he gradually realizes in the process, that he himself is estranged from his own son, Billy (Ethan Hawke). So it's through learning about his relationship with his father that he discovers his relationship with his son...
text_b: The film is set in the 1950s and tells the story of an American family, focusing on the evolution of their eldest son, Jack (Hunter McCracken). On the journey from his childhood to his maturity, the film shows the attempt to reconcile with his father, Mr. O'Brien (Brad Pitt), who will try to teach him how to face a hostile world. On the other hand, the relationship with his mother (Jessica Chastain) is totally opposite, given the kind and humane character that the character demonstrates. As the film progresses and Jack (played by Sean Penn) becomes an adult, we see how the character feels like a lost soul in the modern world, searching for answers to the origin and meaning of life, while questioning the existence of faith. The film is a poetic and personal reflection on the meaning of existence.[5]

anchor_text: Scenes from all five joint films are shown: Aguirre, the Wrath of God, Nosferatu Phantom of the Night, Woyzeck, Fitzcarraldo (also unreleased footage with Jason Robards and Mick Jagger) and Cobra Verde. Herzog also shows several of Kinski's tantrums recorded during filming, as well as excerpts from Kinski's Jesus Christ Savior tour in the early 1970s. Among the speakers are Claudia Cardinale and Eva Mattes as Kinski's former co-stars.
text_a: The members of an internationally renowned string quartet are torn apart after the announcement of the illness of the cellist (the oldest of the group, Christopher Walken): betrayal in love, power struggle, outbursts of desire... Individually and as a group, they will have to go through a path full of obstacles to regain the momentum that will allow them to play the string quartet No. 14 in E flat minor, op. 131 by Beethoven.
text_b: The film is about Werner Herzog's stormy and fruitful relationship with actor Klaus Kinski, who starred in five of his films: Aguirre, the Wrath of God, Woyzeck,[2] Nosferatu, Ghost of the Night, Fitzcarraldo and Green Cobra. The tape is about 100 minutes long. In addition to Kinski and Herzog, the documentary features Claudia Cardinale and Andrés Vicente Gómez.

anchor_text: An American writer, Helene Hanff, who lives in New York, is looking for old editions of some books that interest her and that are available in her city at prices too high for her. After reading an advertisement in a newspaper, the woman contacted an antiquarian bookshop in London, at 84 Charing Cross Road, which specialised in out-of-print editions. Helene thus begins an epistolary relationship that will last twenty years with Frank Doel, director of the library. The two will never meet, but they will become friends sharing a love for books, for literature, for reading.
text_a: The heroine is Hélène Mouret, daughter of Ursula Macquart and the hatter Mouret. At the age of seventeen, she married a man named Grandjean with whom she had a daughter, Jeanne, who became ill and suffered from regular seizures. The family moved to Paris, where Grandjean died shortly after his arrival. Widowed by a man she never truly loved, Hélène is seized by a violent passion for Dr. Deberle, her neighbor, who rescued her during one of her daughter's seizures. But the latter has an equally violent passion for her mother: she cannot bear to see her smile at other children or other men. On the day Hélène gave herself to Deberle, Jeanne, who had foreseen everything, went to her window in the rain and contracted what was then called a galloping phthisis (tuberculosis), from which she died three weeks later. Later, Hélène married a man named Rambaud, with whom she went to live in Marseille. She will return anyway, at the end of the novel, to Paris, where she will stay only a few hours.
text_b: An American writer, Helene Hanff (played by Anne Bancroft), who lives in New York, is looking for some books. He contacts a specialist second-hand bookshop in London, number 84 Charing Cross. Thus begins a twenty-year correspondence with Frank Doel (Anthony Hopkins), an employee of the bookshop; and that will even lead her to send food to the employees of the bookshop, when she learns that London is still living in a post-war economy. The two will never meet, but they will end up being friends, sharing a love of books, of literature, of reading.

anchor_text: In 1949 in New York, Helene Hanff searches for rare classics and British literary titles that she can't find. In the Saturday Review of Literature, she noticed an advertisement placed by Marks & Co, a second-hand bookseller in London. She contacts the store and Manager Frank Doel answers. A long-distance friendship developed over time between the two, then between Helene and other Marks & Co. staff.
text_a: Alice Hughes is a Pulitzer Prize-winning and best-selling author. She's working on a new manuscript that her editor hopes will be a sequel to her best-selling book, You Always/You Never. Alice is due to receive a literary award in the UK but cannot fly there due to health problems. His agent Karen suggests that he take a cruise there on the Queen Mary 2. Alice invites her nephew Tyler and friends she hasn't seen in years, Roberta and Susan. Karen is also invited on board, to learn the details of the new manuscript. For her part, Alice is eager to have a good time and get over her past.
text_b: An American writer, Helene Hanff (played by Anne Bancroft), who lives in New York, is looking for some books. He contacts a specialist second-hand bookshop in London, number 84 Charing Cross. Thus begins a twenty-year correspondence with Frank Doel (Anthony Hopkins), an employee of the bookshop; and that will even lead her to send food to the employees of the bookshop, when she learns that London is still living in a post-war economy. The two will never meet, but they will end up being friends, sharing a love of books, of literature, of reading.

anchor_text: The film tells the story of a group of four teenage orphans all born in the month of December. Their dream of finding a family seems to come true during their first beach vacation away from the orphanage. In fact, children learn that a couple is determined to adopt one of their own. This sets off competition and rivalry in the group, but by the end of the summer each of the boys will have learned something more about himself.
text_a: In a small town in the Australian Outback, the Parker family is trying to start a new life. But no one's really happy about this move. While Matthew spends most of his time at work, Catherine has to manage their two rebellious teenagers, Lily and Tom, on her own. During a sandstorm one night, the two children disappeared without a trace. Family life is disrupted. The parents are assisted by police officer David and some neighbors. But their attempts to search under the blazing sun are transforming the atmosphere. Soon, rumors began to circulate about the Parker family. And the hope of finding the two children alive is diminishing day by day.
text_b: They share the same birth month, which is why the orphanage calls them the December boys - Map, Spit, Spark and Misty - they have a lot in common. With no hope of being adopted, they form their family obligations. Suddenly an unexpected news that a young couple could adopt only one of them, will turn them into rivals for being chosen.

anchor_text: Jason wakes up on a bus full of troubled teens, completely amnesiac. He's surrounded by Piper, who claims to be his girlfriend, and Leo, who claims to be his best friend. When a spirit of the wind, disguised as a student, attacks Jason, his two classmates and their coach Gleeson Hedge (who turns out to be a protective satyr) fight him off. Instinctively, Jason will use his hidden weapons to save his friends and use his newfound ability to fly to save Piper, who has fallen into the void. Soon after, a girl named Annabeth and her friend Butch land on the scene with the help of a cart, claiming to be looking for their friend Percy Jackson, who had been missing for three days. With no new information on the whereabouts of her boyfriend, Annabeth eventually gathers the three demigods and agrees to bring them to the Half-Blood Colony. There, they will discover their half-god identity and will be entrusted with the quest to save Hera, Queen of the Gods, to prevent the awakening of their new enemy, who is none other than Gaia, the Goddess of the Earth and the rise of Porphyrion, King of the Giants. They will have to face numerous monsters, deities, as well as witches, wizards, and werewolves before reaching the place where Hera is imprisoned and freeing her, giving the world a respite by delaying Gaia's awakening.
text_a: Pelias misinterpreted a prophecy of Zeus and seized the throne of Thessaly by killing King Aristo and numerous members of his family. In doing so, he incurs the wrath of Hermes and Heras, who will henceforth protect his nephew Jason. Jason, spurred on by Pelias, sets off in search of the Golden Fleece to secure his rule over Iolkos. Pelias hopes that Jason will not survive the journey. Jason's journey has been joined by many heroes, named after Jason's ship, Argo, the Argonauts. On an island, the Argonauts must contend with a bronze Titan (Talos). King Phineus, blinded by the gods, consults the Argonauts in Phrygia, where they also succeed in capturing two Harpies. As they cross the Strait to the Black Sea, they narrowly escape a rockslide by a lucky amulet of Phineus. In Colchis, Jason defeats the Hydra and must fight the skeletal warriors created from their teeth, managing to escape. Jason loses several companions and makes the journey home to Thessaly with Medea and the surviving Argonauts. On Olympus, Zeus informs Hera that he will soon call Jason back for new deeds.
text_b: While on their way to the palace of Aeolus, the trio encounters the Hunters of Artemis, led by Talia, who meets her brother Jason for the first time in a long time. During the ensuing struggle they manage to save Tristan McLean and kill the giant, though Jason's weapon breaks as they fight. Jason regains some of his memory and realizes that he is a hero trained in the Roman Camp, Camp Jupiter, located somewhere near San Francisco, California. Chiron and the group leaders arrange a meeting in which Jason, along with Annabeth, understand that Hera has made a leader swap between the Greek hero Percy Jackson, who is now at Camp Jupiter, with no memory of his life at Camp Half-Blood, and Jason Grace, who is now at Camp Half-Blood. Jason and Percy were traded by Era so that Greeks and Romans could ally and fight against the Giants and the other monsters.

anchor_text: Ilona works as a waitress in the restaurant "Dubrovnik", her husband Lauri as a tram driver. Both lose their jobs one after the other: Lauri because the transport companies are restructured and some of the employees are dismissed, Ilona because the restaurant "Dubrovnik" goes bankrupt. Both have difficulty getting a new job: Ilona is certified that she is too old at 38 to work in the restaurant business; Lauri's health problems lead to the loss of his driver's license, making it impossible for him to work as a tour bus driver. The former doorman of the Dubrovnik, Melartin, finally gives Ilona the idea of opening a restaurant herself. The bank won't give you a loan, but Mrs. Sjöholm, former owner of the Dubrovnik, has offered to help. Ilona and Lauri rented rooms, hired the former staff of the Dubrovnik as employees and opened the restaurant under the name 'Arbeit' (Finnish for Work). Ravintola työ) and the Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs (Ministry of Labour and Social Affairs). After a rough start, things seem to be going well.
text_a: In the times of the Slovak State, the poor and apolitical carpenter Tono Brtko is appointed in 1942 by his brother-in-law Marcus Kolkotský, who is the town leader with the Hlinka Guard, in the course of the Aryanization to the arische Trustee of a small business on the main street. His greedy wife Evelyna is blinded by the potential profit from the sales, while Tono soon finds out that the business, owned by the deaf and withdrawn old widow Rozalie Lautmann, is bankrupt and survives only by the financial support that the old woman receives from the Jewish community. Tono becomes a more or less involuntary helper of Mrs. Lautmann, helps her in the shop, repairs her furniture, while on the other hand he pretends to his own wife that he already makes money from the shop. At the same time, the indifferent Tono is also induced to behave in a semi-decent manner by regular allocations of money to the Jewish community. When the Jews are about to be deported from the city by the Fascists, Tono decides to help the widow. At first, the old lady is not at all aware of the danger she is in and only slowly realizes that Tono wants to help her. In a drunken state, fears are aroused in the carpenter that he might be exposed as her helper. In his ambivalence, he wants to extradite Mrs. Lautmann first, but then thinks of something better. When she finally understands that it is about the deportation of the Jews, she panics for fear of a pogrom. In his fear of being discovered by her behavior, he pushes the old woman by force into a storage room, where she falls to her death. When Tono discovers this, he hangs himself in the store.
text_b: Lauri loses his job as a tram driver and the restaurant where his wife Ilona works, Dubrovnik, is bought by a restaurant chain. The film then follows the journey of the couple to support themselves. Each will try to find work in his branch; but Lauri's age will prevent him from finding work in transport, and the catering crisis forces Ilona to work in a small restaurant that is shabby, underpaid and not even declared to the tax authorities. She gets fired after a tax audit. She then meets the former doorman of Dubrovnik, who has become a shoemaker, and they try to get a loan to open a new restaurant, which they are denied. She then meets the former owner of the Dubrovnik, reapplies with the owner as a guarantor, and this time the loan is granted. With the help of her husband and the entire former Dubrovnik team, they opened a new restaurant: the Travail (Työ in Finnish).

anchor_text: Chasm City is about contract killer Tanner Mirabel, who is on the hunt for the killer of his employer Cahuella and his wife. To do so, he leaves his perpetually civil war-torn homeworld of Sky's Edge, which orbits the star 61 Cygni-A, and travels to Chasm City, the capital of the planet Yellowstone, which orbits the star Epsilon Eridani. Some surprises await him when he arrives. First, he must learn that the once-thriving Yellowstone system has fallen victim to melting sickness, a kind of virus that infects and mutates nanotechnology. The once glittering band of habitats that circled the planet has now become a pathetic rust belt. But as if that wasn't enough, his memories are starting to change, too. Apparently triggered by an indoctrination virus, the criminal and ruthless founding father of his home world, Sky Haussmann, manifests in his mind. Through the adverse circumstances of hunting and being hunted, his mental state becomes increasingly unstable, and his journey becomes more and more of a confusion on a scale previously known only to Philip K. Dick, leading to a surprising climax.
text_a: After losing her family's farm, local beauty pageant winner Rene Chaplin decides to leave the small town of Berkeley. Meteorites suddenly fall from the sky, turning the inhabitants into zombies. Rene and other survivors hide in the underground shelter of Marion's house and try to survive the attacks of the numerous "undead". Finding themselves without food and water and seeing the increasing number of zombies, the group is forced to abandon the construction. Entering the garage, they flee with the van, but discover that a huge barrier surrounds the entire city. A strange rain continues to fall incessantly as the protagonists try to climb over the mysterious huge wall. The water appears to be contaminated and the group begins to suffer the symptoms of the epidemic. Rene tries to cover himself as much as possible, but during his escape he comes across some hooded figures. These will prove to be aliens, while rain will prove necessary to decontaminate the pestilence area. After treating Rene, the last survivor, the aliens leave with their spaceships. Only one man, afraid of the strange rain, managed to shelter from it by hiding. He, still infected, will spread the disease again. Rene, armed to the teeth, in Marion's house (fenced by a metal net) protects herself from a horde of zombies.
text_b: Most of the novel is told in the first person by a character who calls herself Tanner Mirabel. Originally from Sky's Edge, in the 61 Cygni A system, Tanner is a mercenary who has hired the services of the Cahuella arms dealer as a bodyguard. He lands in the town of Chasm City, on the planet Yellowstone, in pursuit of Silver Reivich, responsible for the death of his client's wife, with the intention of executing him. Upon arriving on Yellowstone, the most technologically advanced planet in human history, he is surprised to discover that the system has been hit by a virus, Melting Rot, which attacks all machines with a certain level of complexity. Chasm City, once a forest of giant, shape-shifting skyscrapers, has been transformed into a nightmare of misshapen ruins, inhabited by the miserable mobs of the city below (Mulch, the Mouise ) and the decadent aristocrats of the Dais who survive on injections of a drug that seems to be becoming rare. Similarly, the Glitter Band, once a glittering slideshow of ten thousand orbital habitats, has become the Rust Belt, where a few hundred people survive with the aid of primitive machinery. Tanner Mirabel sets out to find his prey in this world of chaos and desolation, only to discover that Reivich's motives are more complex than he thought. He is also increasingly affected by the effects of an indoctrinational virus that causes him to relive in flashback moments from the life of Sky Haussmann, the admired and hated founder of his home world. As he explores Chasm City, he begins to understand the origin of the molten rot.

anchor_text: Concert pianist Stephen Orlac loses his hands in a terrible train accident. After that, the hands of a murderer and knife-throwing expert are sewn onto it. From then on, he has the murderous thoughts and abilities of the man previously possessed by the hands. Meanwhile, the brilliant surgeon Dr. Gogol falls in love with Orlac's wife Yvonne and will stop at nothing to get her.
text_a: Famous pianist Stephen Orlac loses both hands in a train accident. After Dr. Gogol's surgery, which implanted the hands of a knife-throwing assassin, Orlac feels that his new limbs condition his mind, driving him to murder. The surgeon, morbidly in love with the pianist's wife, performed the gruesome operation in the vain hope of winning her attention, and is now willing to do anything to eliminate her husband.
text_b: Renowned pianist Stephen Orlac undergoes surgery on his hands after a plane crash. A few months later, he realizes that he is no longer able to play the way he used to and eventually convinces himself that someone else's hands have been grafted in place of his own. Desperate, he flees to the Côte d'Azur with his fiancée, Louise. Settled in a hotel in Marseille, he becomes the prey of a couple, Li-Lang, singer, and Nero, her lover.

From then on, he has the murderous thoughts and abilities of the man previously possessed by the hands.
