What he didn't expect was that his girlfriend was the daughter of a mob boss. And what's worse, involuntarily, he gets more and more involved in the affairs of the "family". Things get complicated when a mobster decides to launder his money by having Michael sell his son's hideous paintings, which ends up attracting the attention of the FBI.

anchor_text: Emma is a gallery owner, she had a relationship with Jerry, a literary agent, which she confessed to four years ago to her husband Robert, who is an editor and also Jerry's best friend. The play opens with a confession, not a confession to the husband of the past infidelity, but a confession to Jerry: Emma tells him that she told everything to her husband Robert.
text_a: A young woman alone in a room moves her furniture, writes letters and eats powdered sugar. She leaves her room and meets a truck driver with whom she wanders for a while. Later, she meets a young woman with whom she is having sex.
text_b: The narrative unfolds in reverse chronological order between 1977 and 1968. The play focuses on the double life of Emma, married to Robert, who for years has maintained a romantic relationship with Jerry, a literary agent and Robert's best friend.[1]

anchor_text: The young medical student Frank Braun falls in love with Alraune, the alleged daughter of his uncle Professor Jakob ten Brinken. When his uncle learns of the relationship, he reveals to his nephew that Alraune is not a natural person, but the product of a scientific experiment in which the genetic traits of a double murderer and a prostitute were crossed. Horrified by this revelation, Frank leaves his beloved behind without speaking to her again. He gets engaged to another woman and then travels to Paris to finish his studies. To its producer, Professor ten Brinken, Alraune brings wealth and success. On a seemingly worthless piece of land that the professor buys on Alraune's advice, a valuable mineral resource bubbles up, enabling him to open a healing pool. Alraune, hurt and uncertain by Frank's rejection, reacts aggressively and begins to use her radiation on men to play them off against each other, causing the deaths of three of her admirers. A troublesome governess is fired because Alraune accuses her of stealing a valuable necklace that she had previously hidden with the governess. Frank's fiancée attempts suicide because Alraune intercepts Frank's letters and pretends to the fiancée that Frank is only interested in her, Alraune. When Frank, who knows nothing of all this, finally decides to marry Alraune, her magic is broken by the love she feels for Frank. The mineral spring is drying up. When Alraune learns the secret of her true origin from Professor ten Brinken, who hopes not to lose her, it is already too late. Professor ten Brinken shoots Alraune to prevent the wedding, and is therefore sentenced to death.

text_b: Professor ten Brinken is attempting artificial insemination with the sperm of an executed murderer. Thus a prostitute is conceived; her child is the girl Alraune. She grows up in a convent boarding house. From there she flees with a boy whom she had previously incited to steal a larger sum of money. Alraune ends up in a circus, where she performs for a magician. Ten Brinken tracks her down and takes her in, where she can live a life of luxury. From the professor's diary entries, she learns of her origins and, in her spontaneous hatred, devises a plan to strangle him at night. However, she comes up with the more sadistic idea of making him suffer by making him fall in love with her. This calculation works out; Professor ten Brinken collapses emotionally and ruins himself for her by gambling. He also suffers from his jealousy deliberately provoked by Alraune. Alraune, finally tired of being herself, wants to become a normal sentient person without a tendency to cruelty and gives herself to redemption in love.

anchor_text: William 'Steamboat Bill' Canfield Sr. is a bearded captain sailing his old steamboat on the banks of the Mississippi. But the newly wealthy banker Mr. King is also building a new steamship and now faces stiff competition from Steamboat Bill. His son William, who grew up in Boston and has never seen his father, comes to visit. The father has little regard for his son and especially for his outward appearance. He had pictured his son as a tall, strong young man, but instead he is a frail boy with a narrow beard, a Basque cap, and fashionable clothes. He also has a ukulele. The old captain has his son shave his beard and buys him new clothes. At the barber's, he meets William Jr. Kitty, a girl he knows from Boston. He falls in love with Kitty, but it turns out that she is the daughter of Mr. King, his father's rival. This puts him in a conflict of loyalty with his father. William Jr. tries again and again to meet with Kitty, but the two fathers try to prevent the connection by all means. There is a series of entanglements and conflicts. Eventually, Steamboat Bill is thrown in jail when he gets into a fight with Mr. King over his ship's driver's license being revoked. When a hurricane tears the small town to shreds, William Jr. rushes to the rescue in a truly stormy finale, first rescuing Kitty from the floodwaters, then freeing his father from the flooded prison, and finally rescuing Mr. King from his sunken steamship. The fathers have reconciled and no longer object to William Jr. and Kitty's relationship. Finally, William Jr. throws a life raft into the water and leaps through it. Surprisingly, he returns, dragging a priest in a life jacket to the steamboat, presumably as a metaphor for marriage.
text_a: Bill, a rude but good-natured heater, on the only night he has left before his steamer leaves for a country vacation, pulls the ballroom girl Mae out of the water into which it had jumped in suicide. He steals dry clothes for them, then they go to a pub. Later that evening, he and the rescued girl discuss love and come to the conclusion that they might as well get married. "Songbook Harry", a self-proclaimed priest, still trusts her at the bar. The next morning, Bill goes to his ship. Bill's buddy Andy, the steamboat's third engineer, is already married, but in Bill's absence, he still attacks Mae. His jealous wife Lou finds out and kills him in a fight. Mae is arrested for the theft of the clothes and has to appear in court. But Bill can't forget the girl who's now his wife. He breaks up a fight with his supervisor over the fence and jumps overboard and swims ashore as the ship drives along the coast. He pleads guilty before the magistrate and goes to prison for Mae, who promises to wait for him until he has served his sentence.
text_b: Two riverboats have long been in fierce competition: Steamboat Bill's Stonewall Jackson, an old, shabby boat, and John King's modern, fitted-out boat. Steamboat Bill is warned by a telegram that his son William Jr., whom he has not seen in years as a college student, is coming to town: he imagines he is as big and powerful as he is, but he is thin and tiny. The young man is also in love with the rival's daughter, Kitty. King and Steamboat Bill Sr. come to blows when the former swallows the latter because of the unsafe boat: the father ends up in prison and William Jr. tries to get him to escape, unsuccessfully. Hit by a car, ends up in the hospital. While he is in bed, the city is hit by a terrible cyclone: people try to save themselves as much as they can, while houses collapse under the devastation and King's boat sinks. William himself is carried, with the flood, on his father's boat. So he jumps in at the helm of the boat and saves the parent who was about to drown in prison. He also saves rival King and his daughter, as well as a priest, to get himself married to Kitty.

anchor_text: The story takes place in 1793, on the western border of the state of New York. The main characters are the aged leather stocking (Natty Bumppo) and Judge Marmaduke Temple, large landowner and founder of the Templeton settlement. The story begins with a dispute between Leatherstocking and the judge over the possession of a freshly slaughtered deer. However, in the course of the action that follows, both, each in their own way, speak out against the ruthless exploitation of nature by the settlers. The plot heats up when Leatherstocking and his companion Chingachgook begin competing with Judge Temple for the loyalty of the young visitor Oliver Edwards and Leatherstocking violates the hunting laws. Edwards eventually marries Elizabeth, the judge's daughter. When Chingachgook dies, Leatherstocking leaves his hut on the shores of Otsego Lake and moves further west into the freedom of the wilderness. # People
text_a: Wild Bill Hickok (Charles Bronson) is haunted by a nightmare where a monstrous white bison chases him through a snowy landscape. At the same time, a white bison appears in the Dakotas and causes panic in an Indian village, killing the child of Sioux chief Crazy Horse (Will Sampson). Overwhelmed by grief to the point of being named Earthworm by his family, he must avenge his daughter's death by killing the white bison. Meanwhile, Hickok decides to find the monster in order to rid his mind of the fear and terror that consumes him. With the help of an old trapper friend (Jack Warden), and disguised as James Otis, Hickok sets out in pursuit of the animal in the Black Hills. On his way he encounters soldiers who want to shoot him, then crosses Earthworm, who he guesses will continue the same quest.
text_b: The story takes place on the rapidly advancing frontier of New York State. The protagonists are an elderly hunter named Natty Bumppo, nicknamed "Leatherstocking", his Indian companion Chingachgook, Judge Marmaduke Temple, and his daughter Elizabeth. Leatherstocking and Temple have a conflict related to the laws of hunting, as both of them at the beginning of the story are fighting over a killed deer. The contrast, however, is also personal: Leather Socks and Chingachgook are lonely men living in the jungle, while Temple is a landowner and founder of a settlement of settlers named Templeton, which represents the unstoppable advance of civilization. Although from different points of view, the two contenders are very interested in protecting the territory in which they live. The feud escalates when Leatherstocking and Templeton also begin competing for the loyalty of young hunter Oliver Edwards. The latter eventually marries Elizabeth Temple. Chingachgook, meanwhile, lets himself die in a fire, representing his entire people destined, according to the vision of the white settlers of that time, to extinction, because the incarnation of a life pattern linked to wild nature and incompatible with civilization. Even Leather Socks, left completely alone, finally decides to abandon his home at Otsego Lake, to retreat to the territories to the west, where still the whites have not arrived.

anchor_text: George Malley is an auto mechanic who lives in a small town in California. On the night of his 37th birthday, after his birthday party, he is dragged to the ground by a mysterious beam of light and develops powerful mental powers. He can quickly read, learn, and move objects with his mind. On various occasions he demonstrates his new abilities, but this does more than win him favor. He and newly moved single mom Lace Pennamin are getting closer. His old friends look at him with suspicion, only Doc Brunder and Nate Pope remain loyal to George. The government is interested in Malley's abilities, but he wants his powers to be used for good. At a party, George wants to ask all of his friends questions, but he's not mentally equipped to handle the onslaught. He falls, gets hit again by the beam of light, and loses consciousness. Doctors diagnose Malley with an inoperable brain tumor that's stimulating his nerve cells, but increasingly throttling his blood supply. A proposed surgery by a neurosurgeon for scientific purposes, which would be associated with his immediate death, is rejected by George. He is held in the hospital by court order, yet manages to escape to Lace, where he eventually dies after a short time. The end of the film shows George's friends at the celebration of his 38th birthday.
text_a: George Malley (John Travolta) is a mild-mannered mechanic who is genuinely in love with an attractive woman from the town he lives in, named Lace (Kyra Sedgwick) who has just arrived in town and who wants nothing to do with a relationship. On the day of his 37th birthday, George notices a light in the sky as he leaves a bar, and immediately suffers a fainting spell and falls to the ground. At first, he may not give it any importance, but little by little he will discover that something has happened. After this extraordinary moment, the next day he finds out how his intelligence has developed abnormally, and he uses it to benefit his friends and the people in his community, helping them in the most unexpected ways. Everyone is interested to know what has happened to him, even the government wants to know to use his knowledge in other fields and take advantage of his enormous intelligence and powers. In the film there are scenes with a notable presence of amateur radio teams and you can see the cards exchanging the aforementioned (called qsl) from different countries on the wall of the room, although without undermining other less inveterate hobbies such as buggies and radio control crawlers.
text_b: Wealthy businessman and despot Max Holt is killed in a plane crash. Previously rescued in critical condition by physician and brain researcher Dr. Peter Corrie, the patient dies in his research laboratory. Against all moral concerns, Corrie secretly removes the uninjured brain of the deceased for research purposes and deposits it in a nutrient solution to keep it alive. After some time, the attached EEG actually shows brain activity and not just that: the organ seems to have taken possession of the physician's mind. Time and time again, Corrie gets into an absent-minded state that causes him to do things that were typical of Max Holt. For example, he compiles a list of names of people who were very close to Holt. Over time, it crystallizes that Holt sees himself as the victim of a conspiracy by his business partners. That's what caused the plane crash. Instead, it's up to Corrie to find the killer and bring him to justice.

anchor_text: In 1902, John McCabe, a mysterious poker player, arrives in the small town of Presbyterian Church, located in the northwestern United States in the still wild state of Washington. He formed a partnership with Constance Miller, a prostitute with whom he opened a brothel. The business is so successful that a powerful company wants to buy it. McCabe is too greedy, the negotiations are broken. The company sends killers.
text_a: Ireland, in the year 1932. Bull McCabe farms the land of his ancestors. This one is rented to him by an English widow. One day the widow decided to sell the land at the local auction. For Bull, this land is his by right. However, he is opposed by a wealthy and ambitious American. Looks like he's interested in this land, too.
text_b: In 1902, John McCabe, a tailor, arrives at Presbyterian Church, a mining town in the northwestern United States. He opens a brothel, which comes to run well thanks to the help of one of the prostitutes, Constance Miller. As the town becomes richer, a pair of agents from the Shaughnessy Harrison Mining Company arrive to buy McCabe's company as well as the surrounding zinc mines. But this one refuses...

anchor_text: Both father and son vampires are forced to flee their family castle, requisitioned by the communist regime. Seeking refuge on the other side of the Iron Curtain, they are accidentally separated along the way: while the father disembarks by accident on the coasts of Britain, where he becomes a star of horror films, the son is stranded on the French coast where he suffers multiple setbacks as an immigrant worker. Each thought the other lost forever, but fate brought them together on the occasion of a film shoot in France. But the reunion quickly turns sour as a love rivalry between father and son comes to poison their relationship.
text_a: The third episode picks up where the second left off, with Father Uffizi being shot, but actually recovering and hunting down and killing two vampires. Together with a boy who survived in the second film he will hunt Dracula, this time without the approval of the Church, with the various dangers that this entails. A new Van Helsing in the role of a priest.
text_b: Forced to leave their native castle in Romania due to the Communist regime, a vampire father and son decide to escape across the Iron Curtain but end up getting lost along the way. While the father luckily reaches England and becomes a popular horror film actor, the son lands instead in France where he suffers all the mistreatment inflicted on immigrant workers. They're both convinced they'll never see each other again, but they happen to find themselves on the set of their father's new film, which is being shot in France. The joy of finding each other turns to hatred as they both find themselves in love with the same woman.

anchor_text: The film depicts the life of pop star Justin Bieber with footage from his 2010 My World Tour to his appearance at Madison Square Garden. Some scenes include backstage footage and interviews with the My World Tour crew. The film also includes scenes from his childhood, recorded by a camcorder.
text_a: It is the follow-up to Never Say Never, continuing to focus instead on Bieber's rise to international fame as he embarks on his Believe Tour. In interviews with Bieber, the film reveals expected answers to questions about his passion for music, relationships, his coming of age and his spotlight as well as never-before-seen concert footage and behind-the-scenes access. The film also includes interviews with Justin's mother Pattie Mallette, his mentor Usher Raymond IV, his manager Scooter Braun, Ludacris, as well as others.
text_b: Never Say Never is a documentary film chronicling the life of Justin Bieber, who has become a global phenomenon with young people. It traces his journey from Stratford, Canada, where he played in the streets, to his sold-out concert at Madison Square Garden. Never Say Never is dedicated to his fans.

anchor_text: Billy is the 27-year-old son of a hotel tycoon and he wastes most of his life partying. When his father Brian announces his retirement, he hands the reins to his acting vice president Eric Gordon. Billy's disappointed and wants to know why he's not taking over the company. His father tells Billy that he bribed Billy's teacher to get him into school. So Billy makes a bet that he's smart enough to take over the company. If he goes back to school and masters the syllabus from first to 12th grade in just 24 weeks, he'll be in charge of the Madison hotels. In a final duel, Billy and Eric face off. In an academic decathlon, the leadership of the company is decided. Billy emerges victorious, but hands over management to another of his father's employees because he wants to be a teacher.
text_a: Billy Heywood (Luke Edwards), is a boy who plays in the minor league baseball. He is the son of a widowed single mother, Jenny (Ashley Crow), and his grandfather is Thomas Heywood (Jason Robards), owner of the Minnesota Twins. When Grandpa dies, it's revealed that he wanted Billy to inherit the franchise. Thomas Heywood specified that if Billy is still a minor at the time of his death, his assistants will assist him until Billy is old enough to run the team on his own.
text_b: Billy Madison (Adam Sandler) is a 27-year-old man who has spent his entire life living off the profits of his father, who owns a chain of hotels. He has spent his days wandering with his friends Jack (Mark Beltzman) and Frank (Norm Macdonald). Billy ruins a meeting between his father Brian (Darren McGavin) and his associates. His father loses faith in his son and as a result chooses Eric Gordon (Bradley Whitford) as the future owner of the company, as he thinks that Billy would not be able to take care of himself and reveals that Billy has not even been able to finish school without paying the teachers. Billy asks his father to reconsider that decision. In the end, the two come to an agreement; Billy must strive to pass primary and secondary in as little as 24 weeks (two weeks per grade), to prove his competence. At that time, Billy falls in love with one of his teachers (Bridgette Wilson) and they become boyfriends, also becoming friends with the children of the primary school.

anchor_text: Beethoven's been living with the Newtons for a while now, a year after Dr. Varnick's arrest. Beethoven walks around town looking enviously at all his friends' dogs, because they're already mated and settled, having left Newton's house. One day he meets a female St. Bernard dog who is disputed between the two separate owners: the animal has been entrusted to his wife, Regina, a treacherous and unscrupulous woman, who in reality does not care anything about the dog, but rather uses it to blackmail her ex-husband, who on the contrary is a good man and loves his four-legged friend very much. Beethoven and the bitch named Missy mate. When the puppies are born, Regina first wants to get rid of them, but then realizes that she could sell them since they are purebred. The Newton children, who have followed Beethoven into his "escapades" with Missy, save the puppies by taking them home and hiding them. One night, George Newton discovers the presence of the four puppies in the basement, and although he initially wants nothing to do with them, he soon becomes fond of them. Sometime later, the family leaves for a holiday in the mountains, taking the dog and the puppies with them. Regina and her new companion, who are also on vacation in the same area, meet the Newton children at a fair and steal their puppies. At which point the entire family chases the thieves and manages to retrieve the puppies, while Regina and her new companion, pushed by Beethoven, end up in a river and are swept away by the current. A few months later, the puppies are all grown up, and Missy's divorced, good-natured owner has finally secured custody.
text_a: Beethoven, a Saint Bernard, lives with the Newton family, consisting of Richard, Beth and their children Brennan and Sara. Although loved by the latter, Beethoven is less liked by the parents because of the disasters he combines. Brennan and Sara decide to enroll their dog in a training course taught by Sergeant Rutledge, but once there, Beethoven destroys the obstacle course. Meanwhile, at the home of the wealthy Sedgewick family, lives Michelangelo, another St. Bernard identical to Beethoven in appearance, but with a refined character. The owner is Madison Sedgewick, a child, the daughter of two parents who neglect her. The two dogs meet on a merry-go-round and Beethoven is mistaken for Michelangelo by Simmons, the Sedgewick butler. The Newtons are amazed at the change in character of "their" dog, as are the Sedgewicks: the first is very kind, polite and composed (at the training camp he overcomes all the obstacles, but without being seen by the trainer), the second shows signs of "aggressiveness". Martha, Madison's mother, then seeks help from a therapist who reports that the reason for this change in the dog's character is due to Martha's selfishness, careless of others and especially of her daughter. Meanwhile, Richard, concerned about his dog's excessive calmness, begins to behave like Beethoven and, as a result, Michelangelo imitates him. A man named Nigel plans to kidnap the Sedgewick family's dog, currently Beethoven, and, with Simmons' complicity, demands a ransom of $250,000, but the dog manages to free itself and, at the awarding of the training course, the two dogs exchange owners again. Beethoven is the winner of the competition (because Michelangelo had passed the tests) and both Nigel and Simmons are arrested by the FBI and imprisoned.
text_b: Beethoven continues to live his pleasant life with the Newtons, and one day, on one of his walks, he meets Missy (Mina in Latin America) a beautiful female Saint Bernard very flirtatious, with whom he will have a love story, which will continue over time and who will give birth to four beautiful puppies. But when the evil owner of the dog named Regina takes her and her boyfriend Floyd to the countryside, the two dogs are separated, and so, the three Newton children: Ryce (Nicholle Tom), Ted (Christopher Castile) and Emily (Sarah Rose Karr), will take care of the dogs as much as they can and when George, the father of the family, decides to take a vacation, he takes his family (including Beethoven and the puppies) for four days to a house in the countryside on the eve of the 4th of July. That's where the tangles start when Beethoven meets Missy again. Finally Brillo, Regina's ex-husband gets custody of Missy and they both visit the Newtons, where they see their grown puppies.

anchor_text: During the Second World War, when he learns of Italy's surrender, Italo Bombolini, a drunken peasant from the wine-growing village of Santa Vittoria, climbs on top of the water castle and has an inscription to Mussolini's glory erased. His gesture, interpreted as an act of bravery, earned him the appointment of mayor and led him to cunningly steal from the German soldiers the wines of the best cuvée that they planned to carry on leaving the scene...
text_a: Just after the fall of fascism, during the period of occupation, the Germans arrive in Santa Vittoria, a town renowned for the quality of its wine, to take possession of all the bottles stored in the social cellar. Mayor Bombolini, a penitent admirer of the Duce, has the task of preventing the country's only wealth from ending up in the hands of the Nazis, risking his own life, but in the end stubbornly resisting the military's insistence.
text_b: The film describes the popular revolt that broke out in Naples spontaneously following the shooting of some Italian sailors on 28 September 1943 and that in four days defeated and fled the German troops from the city before the arrival of the Allies. The film is choral and mixes individual episodes and popular characters protagonists of the revolt. From the boys who escaped from the reformatory to join the insurrection to the little Gennarino Capuozzo who dies with a bomb in his hand ready to throw it on the Nazi tanks to many other characters, among whom Adolfo Pansini should be remembered.

anchor_text: Stanislas Marchand, once a successful writer, found political asylum in Spain where, having remarried Manuela, he lives very modestly in Seville with his daughter Eva. The latter was long engaged to Albert, a young bus driver. Eva, attracted to dancing, would like to make it her profession. During the feria of Abril where she performs, she is courted by Matteo Diaz, rich manadier and great seducer, but enjoys it. From then on, Matteo is obsessed with the desire to possess her to the point of enduring the ultimate humiliations. It's to a fallen Matteo, finally stripped of his beauty, that Eva will give herself.
text_a: Stanislas Marchand, a successful writer, has found political asylum in Spain, where he lives modestly in Seville with his daughter Eva. This one's been engaged to a young truck driver for a long time. Her passion is dancing and she wants to be a dancer. During the fair festivities, Eva is courted by Matteo Diaz, a great seducer. He becomes obsessed with the girl and for her sake suffers all kinds of humiliation, while she enjoys tormenting him. Eventually, though, Eva abandons her haughty attitudes and succumbs to love for Matthew.
text_b: Ten years after her husband's murder, the Queen decides to go to Krantz's castle and hold a grand ball. In her room comes Stanislas, an anarchist intent on killing her. The man, however, falls at her feet and the Queen decides to hide him, seeing the resemblance to her late husband. Even Stanislas realizes that he is facing a woman different from the one described to him, and therefore gives up his deadly project. The couple live three days of passion, but court intrigue takes over. Stanislas realizes that their love is impossible and swallows a poison capsule; the Queen then, unable to give up on him, provokes him by accusing him of being a weakling and he shoots her. Before she dies, the Queen confesses her love, and shortly after, Stanislas takes his last breath.

anchor_text: The story takes place in Hollywood in the late 1930s. The father of unsuccessful actress Faye Greener, Harry, works as a peddler. The simple-minded former accountant Homer Simpson and the artist Tod Hackett, who lives in Faye's neighborhood, fall in love with her. Simpson is being treated badly by the woman. Hackett, a recent college graduate, is hired by a movie studio. Death Hackett keeps having visions that end up mixing with reality. The grasshopper day ends with the premiere of a film at Grauman's Chinese Theatre. Homer Simpson unleashes a wave of violence and mass panic through a short-circuit reaction.
text_a: The film, a grim psychological study, tells in chronologically disordered flashbacks the story of a man who comes to great wealth, but in his personal life suffers a devastating loss of illusion. The film begins with the funeral of Tom Garner, the widely hated president of a railroad company, who is remembered with sympathy only by his secretary Henry Garner, who is also the narrator of the film. Suffering from Garner's selfishness was particularly his wife Sally, a former teacher who taught the illiterates to read and write. She's going to work for him so he can go to college and learn all about railroads. Garner, who works for a railroad company, progresses in his career and eventually becomes president of the company. Although he has a son with Sally, he begins a relationship with the daughter of his business partner. When he wants to divorce Sally, she kills herself. Garner is marrying Eve, his mistress. His son enters into a relationship with the stepmother; when he discovers that the child supposedly born of this relationship is not his, he too goes into voluntary euthanasia.
text_b: This is Hollywood, 1938. Some residents of San Bernardino: Faye Greener, an aspiring untalented actress, her father Harry, a former vaudeville actor who works as a door-to-door salesman, Homer Simpson, a sexually repressed accountant who loves Faye to death, and Tod Hackett, an aspiring artist employed in the department of a Major, who in turn fantasises about Faye. As the play unfolds, disturbing images are seen, such as a cockfight in which one of the opponents will find death, or the androgynous son performing in a grotesque imitation of Mae West.

anchor_text: World War II, in a country occupied by the Nazis. In a village, an elementary school teacher, Albert Lory, lives with his mother, despite his advanced age. He is a man of respect and submission to school and military authorities. He is secretly in love with his colleague, Louise Martin, who is engaged to a collaborator, Georges Lambert, a railway engineer, and the sister of Paul, a partisan. An attempt is made against the German Major Von Keller, commander of the military post. Albert is being held hostage. His mother suspects that Paul is the author of the bombing and, to save her son, she confides in Georges, who denounces him to the German police. Paul is killed by soldiers. Albert is released, and Louise thinks he's the one who betrayed her brother. Albert is informed by his mother about what happened and seeks an explanation from Georges but he committed suicide to not give in to the pressure of Von Keller who wants to force him to reveal the names of other young members of the Resistance. Albert is being blamed for Georges' death. There's a trial. Albert abandons his fearful attitude: he apologizes, denounces the collaborators, exalts the resistance, publicly reveals his love for Louise. The jury acquits him. He returns to his classroom and reads to his pupils the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen. The Gestapo arrests him to shoot him. She greets Louise with a passionate kiss and continues her reading with the students.
text_a: The First World War is almost over. Elizabeth lives in Baltimore and works for the Hamilton family's chemical plant. The managing director of the company is Lawrence Hamilton. With the end of the war, Elizabeth, who is pregnant, learns of her husband's death. She marries Lawrence Hamilton and settles with her son Drew shortly after the marriage. Drew will never know who his real father was. In Austria, an American soldier is in a hospital with serious injuries. He's being treated by Dr. Ludwig. The soldier is John MacDonald, who suffers from amnesia. Recovered, he assumes a new identity and calls himself Erich Kessler. The year is 1939. World War II is breaking out in Europe. Drew Hamilton just graduated from college. He and his friends want to join many young Americans in the war and fight the Nazis. Her plan is to go to Canada and join the Royal Canadian Air Force. Elizabeth is strictly against it. Just now, the family receives a visit from the Austrian chemist Erich Kessler, who fled Austria with his daughter Margaret from the Nazis. In reality, Margaret is the daughter of Dr. Ludwig, who, along with his wife, was murdered by the Nazis. Confronted with places from his own past, Kessler gets small flashes of memory. Elizabeth also recognizes similarities to her fallen first husband. Kessler's narratives provide Drew with further arguments for his will to fight the Nazis. Against his parents' wishes, Drew leaves for Canada. Kessler, who is still suffering from the effects of his injury and is a sick man, follows him and brings him back home. Kessler wants to put the past behind him. He dies of a heart attack. Elizabeth can now devote herself to the future.
text_b: In a town in occupied France, Albert Lory (Charles Laughton), an unmarried schoolteacher living with his mother, has acquired a reputation, even among his pupils, as a person of little aplomb, as a result of his attitude in the bombings. He conceals his love for another teacher, classmate and neighbor of his, Louise Martin (Maureen O'Hara), settling for her friendship and that of his brother Paul. In addition, she maintains romantic relationships with George Lambert (George Sanders), so he sees her unreachable. Paul is a member of the French resistance and very active in sabotage work, therefore, he is betrayed by George who sells him to the Nazis, since he understands that collaboration is the only way to achieve peace. Albert, in a fit of indignation, goes to meet George just as the repentant man commits suicide. Albert, you are wrongfully accused of murder. During the trial, Albert, invested with a serene dignity and a courage unknown to all, leads a memorable plea of freedom that ignites his fellow citizens. Warning of the danger of insurrection, the Nazi commander, Major Erich von Keller, proposes a deal: if he ceases in his plea and testifies at trial, he guarantees a verdict of innocence. Back in court, Albert continues his attitude and harangues his fellow citizens.

anchor_text: Harold Soyinka (David Oyelowo) is a newly married businessman who works for Cannabix Technologies Inc., a pharmaceutical company that has turned medical marijuana into pills. His bosses, Elaine Markinson (Charlize Theron) and Richard Rusk (Joel Edgerton), send him to Mexico to manage the manufacture of this new drug. But Harold is soon kidnapped by the Mexican cartel, who refuse to compete. Stuck and wanted on the wrong side of the border, Harold must survive while his boss Rusk hires a professional mercenary, Mitch (Sharlto Copley), who is none other than his brother, to rescue him...
text_a: An employee of a large U.S. company travels to Mexico to conclude business deals and sign contracts involving pills. While he's in the country, he'll be kidnapped because he's mistaken for the owner of the pharmaceutical company. In the beginning it was what he aspired to because he wanted to take revenge on his boss who meanwhile weaved a relationship with his wife plus he came to know about the expulsion of the work project.
text_b: Robert Bob Mazur is an experienced special agent with the United States Customs Service. With a well-stocked hunting board, his superiors decided to give him a big challenge: to expose 85 barons of Pablo Escobar's clan and an international bank involved in trafficking, the Bank of Credit and Commerce International. Bob Mazur must then invent a new identity, Bob Musella, as well as a family life and past that are convincing enough to infiltrate the Medellín cartel.

anchor_text: All the birds of the world gather to decide who will be their king. The oppa, who is the wisest among them, convinces them to embark on the quest for the legendary Simurgh, a bird of Persian mythology that roughly corresponds to the Phoenix of Western mythology. The birds must cross seven valleys before reaching Simurgh: When the only thirty remaining birds finally reach the place where the Simurgh lives, all they see is a mirror in which they see their own reflected image (This ending conceals a play on words in Persian between "Simurgh", the name of the mythical bird, and "si murgh", which in Persian means "thirty birds"). This represents the Sufi doctrine that God is not external or separate from the universe, but constitutes the totality of what exists; the thirty birds finally comprise the mystical identity between "Simurgh" and their very essence (the Simurgh depicting God, but also the soul capable of looking like the Divine Being). The bitterness of the journey had purified them, turning themselves into Simurghs: "There were two Simurghs, and yet there was only one; and yet there were two". The anagogical meaning is that, with the union, one arrives at the All (3x10) and thus at God, sum of all beings. In short, the work is an allegory in which the search for the Simurgh represents the search for God, the uppu represents a Sufi master, each of the other birds represents a human vice that hinders the attainment of spiritual enlightenment, and the valleys symbolize the stages that a Sufi must cross to draw upon the true nature of God. Within this narrative framework, constituted by the story of the journey of the birds, ʻAṭṭār inserts numerous short stories, a kind of "parable" with a didactic function.
text_a: It is a mystical epic that relates how thirty thousand birds, under the spiritual guidance of the abubilla, come together and decide to go in search of the king-bird or Simurg - sometimes equated with other mythological birds such as the Phoenix Bird.[2] The Simurg is the analogy of divinity, origin and destiny of every traveling bird. This work is considered to be one of the most remarkable works within the religious and cultural tradition of Sufism. Serving as an initiation guide for anyone following this Muslim mystical stream in order to get closer to Allah or the divinity. In addition to the account of the journey, the book includes various tales, semi-historical legends, quotes from the Qur'an or accounts drawn from the Persian cultural tradition that serve to illustrate the difficulties of the initiation journey and how to overcome them. During the journey one by one the birds are abandoning each other with an excuse, unable to continue the journey; each of these birds symbolizes misconduct, of the large number of birds that began the journey only thirty reach their end. The birds must cross seven valleys in their search for Simurg:[3] These are the stages by which the Sufi can come to the true nature of God. The Sufis always feared the condemnation of the Sunni thinkers who equated Sufi mysticism with any idea of mystical fusion between man and God. # Illustrated It is in the collection of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, New York. Slides from an illustrated manuscript dated c.1600. The paintings were done by Habiballah of Sava (active ca.1590-1610), in ink, opaque watercolor, gold, and silver on paper, dimensions 25.4 x 11.4 cm.[4]
text_b: After a long absence Sadko returns to his native Novgorod: he is taken by the beauty of Liubava, to which he is committed, while the misery of the people saddens him. He lashes out at the rich merchants, who leave their riches unproductive, instead of using them to give the people work. Chased by the merchants, Sadko goes to the sea and here Ilmen, daughter of the king of the oceans, appears to him, who promises him for the next day the fishing of the goldfish. With this, in fact, he returns to the city and persuades the merchants to entrust him with their merchandise and to arm three ships, with which he sets off in search of the Phoenix, the mythical bird of happiness, captured in a distant country. After passing terrible trials, Sadko manages to capture the fabulous bird. On the return journey the God of the sea, whom Sadko has neglected to propitiate, unleashes upon him a terrible storm. Relying on a raft, Sadko ends up at the bottom of the sea and is welcomed by the king and queen of the oceans, who would like to force him to marry one of their daughters. But the beautiful Princess Ilmen, though she loves her, helps her escape. So Sadko can return to Novgorod, where he is greeted triumphantly by the people, the merchants and Liubava, where he will find happiness, that happiness that he has vainly sought in the wide world.

anchor_text: The film's main character, Homer Hickam, lives with his family in the small mining town of Coalwood, West Virginia. His future as a miner in the mine seems preordained. When the Soviet Union launched the first artificial satellite, Sputnik, in 1957, Homer discovered his interest in rockets and decided to build one himself. After the first attempt with his mother's destroyed garden fence ends, he enlists the help of three other boys, Quentin Wilson, Roy Lee Cooke and Sherman O'Dell. He finds further support from his teacher Frieda Riley, who suggests him to participate in a science fair with the project, which could bring him a scholarship and thus a path to his dreams. The experiments are progressing with varying degrees of success, and the dream seems to be over when one of their rockets is blamed for the outbreak of a forest fire. When his father is killed in a mining accident, Homer takes his place in the mine to help support the family. After his father has recovered, Homer tries to find out if a rocket of theirs was actually responsible for the forest fire. After he is able to prove to the school that the rocket was not responsible, the boys start building rockets again and work towards the competition. All is at stake once again when Homer's model of a rocket nozzle is stolen at the deciding federal competition. With the help of his father, who had previously been more of an opponent of the project, a colleague at home manages to make a new nozzle and get Homer there in time.
text_a: In 1957 in a small town called Coalwood, the destiny of the inhabitants was marked from their birth. The nearby coal mine inevitably provided work for almost all the inhabitants. Homer Hickam Jr. (Jake Gyllenhaal) was no less; although his ambitions were high, his financial possibilities would not allow him to continue his studies, and, given his not very athletic abilities, he would not even be able to take a scholarship for sports merits, as his older brother Jim had done. One day, as the Soviet Sputnik passed in the October sky, he decided that his fate would not be what his father John (Chris Cooper), the mine superintendent, wanted his son to follow in his footsteps. He and a group of friends decided to prepare a rocket and launch it into space. After several failed attempts, the final launch succeeds and the rocket flies high into the sky. The boy's hopes are raised, and his passion for astronomy and the encouragement of his chemistry and physics teacher Miss Riley (Laura Dern) lead him to win a scholarship. The film shows how a boy's dreams can come true, despite all the evidence to the contrary. Homer is a boy like many, with ambitions that are not accepted by his father, who considers himself, unlike his son, a man with his feet on the ground. But the boy's perseverance and passion for astronautics will lead him to achieve the success he's longed for.
text_b: A seemingly orphaned boy is picked up alone on a road in a wooded area and handed over to the juvenile authorities. With no parents to be identified, the boy is placed in the care of a foster family, Andy and Joyce Richardson. He is lovingly cared for and develops into a model boy, always friendly, helpful and without reproach, yet highly intelligent and extraordinarily attentive. After some time, his alleged parents contact him, who have all the necessary papers to prove their status. It turns out that the boy is an artificial life form that was freed from the research center by some of the scientists involved to live a normal life. This is where the film begins in the wooded region. The Pentagon-funded research was aimed at creating a super-intelligent and ice-cold perfect soldier. When the military finds out that their research project, Data Analysing Robot Youth Lifeform, develops emotions like love, fear and a taste for chocolate pudding, they're about to be liquidated. In the course of the following adventurous escape, Daryl, as the Cyborg is called, steals a Lockheed SR-71 Blackbird supersonic spy plane, in the crash of which he loses his "life" (he drowns). A medical doctor involved in the development of D.A.R.Y.L. comes to the hospital and reactivates the seemingly lifeless boy, because a cyborg cannot drown. He'd shut himself off to maintain the illusion.

anchor_text: Two killers arrive at a restaurant in a quiet New Jersey town one night, looking for the Swede, the gas station attendant from the nearby gas station. Warned of the danger by a client, Nick Adams, the man does not attempt to flee and is murdered. An investigator from the company that insured the gas station attendant is going to piece together the chain of events leading up to the murder, interviewing people who knew him.
text_a: Police cadet Pete Smith is assigned to Scarface Marca's gang leader. Smith and Marcas were friends before Smith was a cop. He had taken care of the gangster who had been shot, but he escaped with his coat and money. When Pete's friend, Sergeant Coughlin, is killed in a shootout with Marcas, he redoubles his efforts. In plain clothes, he can approach Marcas, who doesn't recognize him, and shoot him. It also allows Pete to free Mary Monks, who is being held captive by Marcas.
text_b: Two hired killers arrive in a small town and kill a gas station employee, Ole Andreson "the Swede" (Burt Lancaster), who was waiting for them.[Note 1] Detective Riordan (Edmond O'Brien) investigates the case for the insurance company for which he is employed, despite his boss telling him not to do so, as it is a matter of little economic importance.[5] Riordan begins to tie the knot and discovers that "the Swede"'s life was full of deceit and crime, and that in all of this had something to do with the mysterious Kitty Collins (Ava Gardner).

anchor_text: The story centers around the figure of reporter Richard Dees engaged in the search for a killer who kills his victims like a vampire and who also distinguishes himself for roaming in search of his victims aboard a black Skymaster. Once Dees reaches the Night Flyer, he finds himself confronting the killer and the nightmares it awakens in his mind. Dees is joined, against his will, by a young journalist recently hired by Inside View, the magazine he works for. The two, unable to work as a team, try to each get an exclusive on the story, but it will be the young Katherine, unexpectedly, who will have the upper hand and write the final account.
text_a: Dr. David Krane, a brilliant forensic pathologist, has been relieved, for lack of evidence, of the investigation into his wife's murder. Haunted by the memory of this tragedy, he embarks on a new investigation of his own. When he meets Dr. Martha Briggs, a neurobiologist who works on memory transfer, Krane sees this as his last chance to clear his name. Martha is experimenting with a memory serum that could reveal memories buried in the shadowy areas of the brain.
text_b: Richard Dees (Miguel Ferrer) is a veteran journalist for Inside View, a magazine that publishes articles focused on the supernatural and demonic. One day, Dees hears about a night flyer who flies over dark airports in the middle of the night and brutally murders his victims, so he decides to investigate the character. As he investigates, he discovers important clues that will lead him to know the true face of the night flyer. During the film he will be confronted with a journalist who also wants to take the case. He also meets villagers who help him with clues to find the strange being.

anchor_text: Joe Bell is the epitome of masculinity, and as a husband and a father, he's used to yelling to get what he wants. Jadin, his handsome teenage son, is gay and is being mercilessly bullied in high school. Instead of losing himself in his grief after his son's suicide, Joe travels across the U.S. to speak to students and anyone else who will listen as he talks about the consequences of bullying.
text_a: Fourteen-year-old Joe Brinson witnesses the wreckage of his parents' marriage, Jeanette and Jerry, a housewife and a gardener at a golf club in a small Montana town in the 1960s. On the nearby Canadian border, an uncontrolled wildfire rages and Jerry decides to join the volunteers to fight the fire, leaving his wife and son alone. Joe is suddenly forced to become an adult to help his mother, who in the meantime has found love in the arms of another man.
text_b: In April 2013, Joe Bell, a father of two, crosses the long roads of the United States, with his transport wagon, to pay tribute to his eldest son Jaiden, a victim of bullying at school for his homosexuality, who is devastated by the heavy responsibility that drove him to suicide.

anchor_text: For a long time, the hotel owner Maria Alsbacher was tied to a much older and sickly husband. His death is something of a release for her, and Mary is open to a new happiness. This is what she believes she found in Monsieur Gidou, the conductor and singer in her establishment. But this relationship alienates Maria from her two children, Paul and Dorothy. Her son in particular can't accept that his mother wants to marry this man anytime soon. Gidou is considered a greyhound who is only after Maria's money. Paul's childhood friend Lydia Bauer, who has been seriously ill for some time, proves her love and unbreakable loyalty to him by risking her good reputation and engaging with the windy bandleader with all her might to open Maria's eyes. Maria is to catch her and her future husband in flagrant disorder on a joint, intimate rendezvous. In fact, this plan succeeds, and Gidou is suddenly history. Paul now knows what he has in the loyal, self-sacrificing girlfriend of his youth. Maria, on the other hand, realized late, but not too late, with whom she had almost exchanged the rings and thus found her way back to her children. To make an illusion poorer and alone again, Mary finally resigns, while son Paul sees his life's mission in making Lydia's last weeks in her life as pleasant as possible.
text_a: For a long time, the owner of the hotel, Maria Alsbacher, was married to a much older and sick husband. His death is a kind of liberation for her and Mary is opened to a new happiness. That's what she thinks she found in Mr. Gidou, the conductor and singer in her establishment. But this relationship alienates Maria from her two children, Paul and Dorothy. Especially her son can't accept that his mother wants to marry this man in the near future. Gidou is considered a jackass, who only wants Maria's money. Paul's childhood friend Lydia Bauer, seriously ill for some time, shows him her unwavering love and loyalty, in which she puts her reputation on the line and engages with the conductor with all physical strength to open Maria's eyes. Maria wants to get a clear idea by taking him on an intimate date. In fact, this plan succeeds and Gidou is taken. Paul now knows what his childhood friend says. Maria, however, realizes it later, but not too late, and returns to her children. Maria finally gives up on an increasingly poor and lonely illusion, while son Paul makes Lydia's last weeks of life as pleasant as possible.
text_b: Paul is a seasoned business traveler who faces long journeys every day to earn an honest living. One morning, during the inspection of the ticket counter on the train, he takes the defense of a young girl who had lost her ticket, mocking the controller. In retaliation, the latter took advantage of the fact that Paul had forgotten his membership card at home to force him to get out of the car. Once at the station, the man is forced to take a courier to get near Tarascon. Paul does everything he can to catch the shuttle that would take him to his destination in time, but the driver's delays and a breakdown in the dilapidated bus derail his plans. Finding himself stranded again, Paul discovers that the girl on the train, re-encountered on the rails, is named Maria and has been abandoned by her companion, a man with whom she ran away from home and who has disappeared after discovering that he got her pregnant. So the young woman was returning to her family, to whom she had lied, and asked Paul to pretend to be her husband, to carry on the lie especially with her father, a loving but very strict and traditional parent. After several resistances, Paul lets himself be convinced by Maria, having pity on her, but the meeting with the girl's family will lead to further misunderstandings, until the discovery of the deception by Maria's family.

anchor_text: Initiated by a Hagenbeck hunting expedition, held for months in a cramped cage on a steamer, the monkey seeks a way out and soon realizes that in addition to living in a cage in the zoo, a second way is open to him: that of the Varietés, to become a "man" there after several years of "absolute self-denial and adaptation". He imitates humans because he wants to be as "untroubled" as they obviously are. He seems to learn meaningful gestures and speech with ease. His biggest problem is drinking liquor. A ship's passenger gives him theoretical and practical lessons at various times. That's how he learns that too, with great difficulty. On several occasions, he emphasizes that he only imitates people because he is looking for a way out, but not because he hopes for freedom. Faced with the alternatives of zoological garden or variety show, he aspires to work in the variety show and has "hardly any further successes". His life flows successfully between banquets, scientific societies and social gatherings. He has achieved what he wanted to achieve, and he attests to himself the education of an average European. He's obviously a master of the human-animal borderline. Not so two other beings in his environment. His first trainer, with whom he learns like a "ruthless" one, himself becomes almost epic and has to temporarily go to a sanatorium. The little half-trained chimpanzee, with whom he makes himself comfortable at night "like an ape", has the "madness of the confused trained animal in his eyes" that he can't stand during the day.
text_a: An expedition travels to Africa to capture wild beasts on behalf of an eccentric American businessman who is preparing to launch an extravagant nightclub. Chief Max O'Hara and his sidekick Jack Johnson meet at a farmhouse the young orphan Jane and a domesticated giant-like gorilla named Joe, who have been together since childhood. O'Hara sniffs the deal and writes "girl and gorilla" to make it an attraction. At first things go very well: the monkey's performances, culminating in the number in which he lifts Jane on a platform playing Beautiful Dreamer on the piano, bring in a large crowd of enthusiastic spectators, but one night some customers, completely drunk, turn Joe on to the point that he explodes in a devastating fury. While the police are ordered to kill the animal, Jack, now in love with the girl, organizes the escape to bring the gorilla back to Africa.
text_b: The narrator, speaking to a large audience for the opening of a scientific conference, begins to describe what his previous life as a monkey was. Her story begins in the jungles of Africa, where an expedition of hunters chased her and caught her with two shots. One to his cheek, which will give him a scar that will earn him the nickname of Little Red Riding Hood, and one to his groin, which will cause him to limp. The animal is then loaded onto a ship and shipped off to Europe; during the ocean voyage the monkey finds itself for the first time without the freedom to move as it pleases. Needing a distraction from this painful situation, the monkey begins to study the habits of the human crew and soon imitates them with surprising ease; the only difficulty the animal encounters is in learning to drink alcohol. Throughout the story, the narrator emphasizes and reiterates that he wanted to learn human behavior only to be able to more easily get out of the cage in which he was locked. As soon as he arrives in Europe, the monkey realizes that he is faced with an existential choice, either to end up in the zoo or to go to the "Music Hall": so he devotes himself more and more to becoming human enough to be able to succeed in this second career. He will succeed in realizing his dream, thanks also to the constant and valuable help of many teachers. He finally decided to make this report to the Academy, after his transformation had reached such a level of completeness that he could correctly describe his previous emotions and experiences as a monkey. The monkey concludes his speech by expressing a high degree of satisfaction with his future fate.

text_a: In the middle of England, two students discover a cave inhabited by an ape-like creature. The monster kills one of the students and is subsequently captured and handed over to the custody of Dr. Brockton, an anthropologist and the world's leading expert in monkey research. She examines the creature from a bygone era and names it Trog. Soon, Dr. Brockton discovers that Trog is the missing link, the crucial link in the evolution of man from the ape. Friendly feelings quickly develop between man and ape, and soon Trog is able to use sign language to express his innermost thoughts. Everything could go smoothly and Trog could become a valued member of society, but one day he kills a dog. The event is exaggerated by the media and leads to a court case in which Dr. Brockton is convicted of killing her protégé because he would pose a latent danger to the people. Before that happens, Trog is freed by conservationists, spreads fear and terror in a rampage, kidnaps a child, and ends up being blown up by the army with dynamite.
text_b: Captured by a Hagenbeck Company hunting expedition, kept for months in an oppressively cramped cage on a steamboat, the monkey searches for a way out and soon realizes that, in addition to life in a cage at the zoo, there is a second possibility for him: vaudeville. For after several years of absolute self-denial and accommodation[2] becoming a "human being" he imitates humans because he wants to be "unmolested", as they seem to be. He apparently learns meaningful gestures and speech with ease. His biggest problem is drinking liquor. A passenger on a ship gives him theoretical and practical lessons on various occasions. He learns that too with great effort. He repeatedly emphasizes that he only imitates people because he is looking for a way out, not because he longs for freedom. In vaudeville he has "successes that can hardly be increased". His life passes successfully between banquets, scientific societies and get-togethers. He has achieved what he set out to do and considers himself an average European. He obviously dominates with virtuosity the border area between humans and animals, two other beings around him fail to do so. His first trainer, with whom he learns in a "ruthless" way, turns himself almost into a monkey and has to go to a sanatorium for a while. The semi-trained little chimpanzee, with whom he "relaxes monkey-style" at night, has "the madness of the confused and tamed animal in his eyes" that he cannot bear during the day.

anchor_text: The farce, which depicts married life, is retrospectively narrated by Antonia, the wife, and is introduced by the scene in which Antonia repeatedly tries to kill herself because of her husband's infidelity. At the beginning of the play, the man persuades Antonia into an open two-way relationship, which is initially only open to his side. The woman first tries to accept this type of relationship and remains in the role of loving mother and wife. But unable to cope with it, suffering from it, and jealous of it, she changes and sets new goals. This is contrary to the man's idea of an open relationship, after which he takes his own life.
text_a: At the end of the war, Antonia returns to her native country, the Netherlands, with her daughter Danielle: the two women will found a large matriarchal extended family. In the 40 years narrated in the film, everything happens: love, death, work, religion, sex, hatred and revenge, but also philosophy, courage and poetry. One morning Antonia wakes up knowing that this will be the last day of her life: she warns everyone and dies surrounded by her affections.
text_b: Antonia's been married for a long time to a man who doesn't love her anymore. She is forced to continually accept extra-marital relations from her husband, a proponent of the "open couple". At first, the woman feels sad, even contemplating suicide; then she realizes that she is still young and therefore ready to start a new life. Just when she has a free pass, her husband becomes jealous and stops her from continuing an affair with a Nobel Prize-nominated physicist.

text_a: At the end of the war, the very independent Antonia returned with her daughter Danielle to the village where she was born in the Netherlands. The two women will form a large matriarchal extended family. Over the course of the film's 40 years, everything comes together: love, death, work, religion, sex, hatred and revenge, but also philosophy, courage and poetry. One morning, Antonia wakes up knowing that this will be the last day of her life. She gathered her entire "clan" and died surrounded by her loved ones.
text_b: It tells the life of a "progressive" couple who think they are very liberal and open, but the only one who enjoys that freedom is the man. It shows the machismo that sometimes locks in even the most liberal and progressive couples. It is a play full of comic gags and delusional situations that help to soften the play, without it ceasing to show a reality.[1]

anchor_text: Paris 1921: Emma Eckhert, a hatmaker with bisexual tendencies, is married to the older but wealthy businessman Moïse Nathanson. The marriage serves Emma as an instrument for her planned ascent into the better society of Paris. After a while, she starts an affair with the rich Camille. With their support, Emma becomes bank president of a savings bank. Their bank's success is based on exorbitant guaranteed interest payments to savers. Horace Vannister, owner of a rival bank, suspects that Emma's rise isn't going well. Horace has influential political friends and is getting a review of the bank. After a court-ordered search of the bank, Emma is charged with stock manipulation and sentenced to three years in prison. In prison, Emma continues to fight for her success and to regain her reputation. However, she does not succeed again until the government changes and Emma is subsequently rehabilitated. When she returns to her bank and promises her angry creditors full repayment of the debt, she is killed in an assassination attempt.
text_a: Lisa and Simon have been in love since they were young. The attractive young couple live in Paris, where Lisa is attending hotel school. Simon makes his living in criminal enterprises. Both enjoy life to the full. But an accident, caused by Simon's criminal machinations, destroys the idyll and puts him in danger. He's leaving France at the drop of a hat. Left behind, Lisa waits in vain for news of her beloved. Three years later, Lisa is married to the elderly and wealthy Léo Redler. A trip with her husband to the Indian Ocean brings her by chance together with her former lover, who works in a hotel there. The fateful encounter has unforeseen consequences for all involved. Lisa enters into an affair with the guilt-ridden Simon, whose memories of the events in Paris have prevented him from leading a normal life. In the end, only Lisa is allowed to free herself from the clutches of this ominous ménage à trois.
text_b: Coming from a modest background, young Emma Eckhert began her career as an employee in the family chapelry. In 1921, she had her first trouble with the law at the same time that she scandalized those around her with her homosexuality. Her marriage to Moses Nathanson, an older family friend, did not prevent her from continuing her affair with Camille Sowcroft, the daughter of a jeweler, who helped her to enrich herself by advancing money that Emma quickly made profitable through shrewd stock market operations. By 1929, Emma had become one of the most popular bankers in Paris. But her meteoric success attracted the antipathy of the powerful banker Horace Vannister; he intervened with the President of the Council Prefailles to ban the activities of Emma Eckhert and prosecute her in court. She was eventually murdered during a meeting where she was explaining to the savers who had trusted her how she was going to repay them.

anchor_text: Christine Lucas has been waking up with amnesia every day for the past 14 years following a serious accident. Every morning she no longer recognizes her house, her husband, and no longer remembers anything. She then preserves, through videos recorded the previous day on her camera, a testimony of her previous days to help her reconstruct her past. Dr. Ed Nash is helping her deal with this amnesia in her husband's back. Gradually, as she assembles the pieces of her life, she discovers that she has to be wary of certain people.
text_a: In order to spy on her lover Ludo, the 50-year-old, very successful Parisian literature professor Claire Millaud creates a fake profile on social networks, according to which she is 24 years old and beautiful. Alex, Ludo's friend, is instantly in love with Claire's avatar, the 24-year-old Clara Antunès. Even though it's all virtual, the feelings are very real.
text_b: Christine Lucas (Nicole Kidman) is a forty-seven-year-old writer who, due to an accident suffered at the age of 25, is unable to remember the past and retain memories after that accident. Every day, when she wakes up, she thinks she is single and still has to make big decisions, but, like every day, she discovers that she lives with her husband (Colin Firth) and that the big decisions of her life are already made.

anchor_text: Rupert Turner was always a big fan of John F. Donovan. A decade has now passed since the TV star died alone and unexpectedly after a series of scandals. Rupert, now a successful actor himself, recalls the written correspondence he maintained with Donovan for five years after the then 11-year-old became his secret pen pal. The letters had an impact on both of their lives, and Rupert gained a unique insight into the TV star's soul. Rupert summarizes the correspondence with Donovan and reveals to reporter Audrey Newhouse in an interview everything he knows about Donovan's turbulent life, including the compromises he made for fame and how the man he adored inspired him as a young boy.
text_a: Rupert Turner is an English boy who has always been a big fan of John Francis Donovan, American star of film and television, who dies at not even thirty years old in 2006 in New York completely alone, for a long time insomniac and fallen into disgrace after a series of scandals that involved him. After a decade, the young actor Rupert tells, in an interview with the journalist Audrey Newhouse, granted in Prague in 2017 where he is shooting a film, his epistolary friendship with the actor lasted 5 years and started when he was 11 years old. Through their letters Rupert recounts the tormented existence, between the compromises with fame and prejudices that hindered the short but intense life full of lies of his idol.
text_b: Henry is a highly successful lawyer with few scruples. While maintaining an appearance of a happy family, both he and his wife are unfaithful, and his relationship with his daughter Rachel is virtually nonexistent. One day, by chance, he goes down to buy tobacco and finds himself in a robbery at the supermarket. During the incident he is shot and, although he recovers from the wounds, suffers severe brain damage that manifests in retrograde amnesia. She remembers nothing of her previous life, seemingly successful and happy. Like a small child, he has to learn everything: to speak, to read, to behave in public... As he thrives, he realizes that before the accident he was a cold person at home and at work. His wife (Annette Bening) and daughter (Mikki Allen) continue to love him and help him rebuild his life. He is determined to change and become a different man, but as he recovers from his past he will have to choose between returning to his former life of fraud and apparent success or embarking on a path of honesty and respect for others.

anchor_text: In the autumn of 1654, the young and spirited Eloise, daughter of the heroic Gascon captain of Artagnan, worthy of her father in the handling of the sword, witnesses in her boarding school-convent the murder of the Mother Superior by the odious Duke of Crassac and the woman in red, Eglantine of Rochefort, who are engaged in the trade of blacks from Africa to the Americas and the coffee trade. She suspects a plot against the young King Louis XIV and runs to Paris to ask her father for help and warn the young king and his prime minister, Cardinal Mazarin. She causes a hunt where his father and his faithful friends the famous musketeers Aramis, Athos, Porthos, although all retired from the service of the king's musketeers, return to service to help him in this paladin's mission. At the same time, she falls in love with a young man.
text_a: In the year 1625 in Venice, Athos, Aramis and Porthos, are three Musketeers of King Louis XIII specialized in the martial arts. With the help of Milady de Winter, they steal a secret document. Everything seems to be going well until they are betrayed by Milady, a spy in the pay of Cardinal de Richelieu. She steals the document from them and runs off with the Duke of Buckingham. A year later, a young Gascon named d'Artagnan arrived in Paris to become a Musketeer. This is where, for unintentionally and successively disturbing the Three Musketeers, without knowing who they are, he must challenge them to a duel for reparation. But since the duels were forbidden by the cardinal, the four men were surprised by his guards, joined forces to fight them and routed them. It was during this victory that he met the young Constance Bonacieux, a vassal of Queen Anne of Austria. King Louis XIII rules the kingdom, but his extreme youth (he is barely twenty years old) makes him mainly interested in fashion, while trying to make the beautiful young queen understand that he is deeply in love with her. In the company of his new companions, d'Artagnan confronts the Cardinal de Richelieu and his two dangerous minions, Milady de Winter and the Count of Rochefort, who, allied with the Duke of Buckingham, want to prevent Louis XIII from reigning, and plunge Europe into war.
text_b: In the autumn of 1654 the young and fiery Eloise, daughter of the heroic Basque captain D'Artagnan, worthy of her father as a swordswoman, is a witness at the convent of the murder of her mother Badessa. The culprits are the Duke of Crassac and a woman in red, Eglantine de Rochefort, engaged in the slave trade and the coffee trade. Eloise suspects a plot against the king and flees to Paris and the court to ask her father for help in order to warn the young king and Cardinal Mazarin. Eloise's intervention brings the three retired musketeers, Athos, Porthos and Aramis, back into action for this last mission.

anchor_text: Three workers and friends, Zeke, Jerry and Smokey, work at a car plant in Detroit. One night, they came up with the idea of robbing the local union office. First, they think it's a failure, because they only steal $600, but Zeke realizes they've also gotten some compromising documents. They then decide to blackmail the union, provoked by the fact that the latter claims to have lost $10,000 as a result of their theft.
text_a: The two brothers, Chuck and Wes have a plan on how to rob Caesar's Palace in Las Vegas and they join a biker gang and while the others are drinking and partying out of town, they change their clothes and go to the casino to do the robbery. Of course, the police aren't looking for two well-dressed criminals among the Hell's Angels.
text_b: Zeke, Jerry and Smokey are three friends who work as blue-collar workers in an automobile plant in Detroit. Tired of coping with the little money they earn and the conditions in which they are forced to work, one night they make a plan to rob the union office. The heist looks like a failure when only $600 is found in the safe, but among the stolen material are documents proving the union's ties to organized crime. So they decide to blackmail the union, but the situation gets out of hand for the three friends who find themselves pitted against each other.

anchor_text: It's 1969, and Dalton Joiner is a young, capable soldier in Vietnam. His girlfriend, Jane, decides to leave him, so after a week's leave Dalton vows to sneak back home to win her back. His best friend, Mickey Wright, decides to go with him. They have to get back to the States, change Jane's mind, and go back to the war undetected. Dalton tracks down Jane and discovers that she now goes by the name Juniper, and is a member of an anti-war activist group that Candace is a part of. The two soldiers pretend to be unjustified absentees and for this they are admired by June's group until it is discovered that they lied. The two boys will discover the true meaning of love, honor and commitment.
text_a: The film portrays a combat unit during the Vietnam War led by a black commander, Lieutenant Joseph Anderson. Anderson, a graduate of the United States Military Academy at West Point, is ordered to capture a high-altitude trench 150 miles (250 km) north of Saigon. The unit, which is characterized by great camaraderie and harmony, will be brought to the region by helicopter. Under enemy fire, the soldiers fight their way through fields, forests, and streams. During a break in the fighting, one of the men will be allowed to go on leave to Saigon. But the man quickly spends his money and returns to the train. When Anderson and his men reach a small village, they are ordered to move on. The wounded and dead are being flown out by helicopter, while the rest of the train continues the fight for the high ground.
text_b: In 1969, at the time of the Apollo 11 mission, U.S. soldier Dalton Joiner, fighting in the Vietnam War, used his leave time (which was supposed to be spent in Hong Kong), to fly back to the U.S. and attempt to retrieve his girlfriend Jane's heart. His partner-in-arms, Private Mickey Wright is accompanying him. Jane now goes by the name Juniper, and is part of a group of anti-war activists. Joiner and Wright make the group believe they have defected, earning them the admiration of activists, until it is revealed that they plan to return to Vietnam shortly. Jane breaks up (again) with Joiner, which causes her to decide to flee to Canada. Wright falls in love with Candace, but returns to Vietnam.

anchor_text: The film is set against the backdrop of the Vietnam War and the moon landing. Two young soldiers, Mickey Wright and Dalton Joiner, serve in the U.S. Army during the Vietnam War. In an attack on the unit's position, a comrade is mortally wounded, while the two survive by chance. Later, Wright and Joiner get a week's vacation in Hong Kong. Joiner decides to fly for a week to his girlfriend Jane waiting in America. Wright can't abandon his comrade and accompanies him. At home, they discover that Jane, now called Juniper, has joined a peace movement. The soldiers present themselves as deserters, although they intended to return at the end of the week. For both of them, the question is whether they should return to their duties.
text_a: It's 1969, and Dalton Joiner is a young, capable soldier in Vietnam. His girlfriend, Jane, decides to leave him, so after a week's leave Dalton vows to sneak back home to win her back.
