anchor_text: Vienna, the owner of a casino-saloon that she has cleverly built in a desert area soon to be served by a railroad, knows her fortune is made if she resists the greed for real estate of the wealthy ranchers of the nearby city, and their anxiety about the trains that will arrive with the farmers who will fence their meadows. The attack of a stagecoach crystallizes the hatred of the nobles for Vienna, carried away by Emma, a wealthy Puritan village owner jealous of Vienna's independence and success. She accuses Dancing Kid of killing her brother in the attack and Vienna of being complicit in protecting him. Johnny Guitar, a musician who has just been hired by Vienna to play at his establishment, tries to interfere...
text_a: Johnny performs musical numbers on a "showboat" that travels down the Mississippi River. He also has a gambling addiction, and often ends up squandering his wages inside the casino on the boat. One day, along with his friend Cully, he meets a gypsy who predicts great luck at gambling if he has a red-haired girl at his side. He'll find it in Nellie Bly, but that will draw the wrath of Johnny's girlfriend, Frankie, and Nellie's boyfriend, Clint Braden. An employee of the latter, thus believing to please his boss, replaces with a real bullet the one that is in a stage gun that Frankie will have to use in a musical number with Johnny during the show. Clint tries to prevent the accident, but he gets to the theater too late. So Frankie will shoot Johnny, but he'll have saved his life thanks to a lucky charm he always carries around in his stage costume pocket, which will stop the bullet.
text_b: Johnny Logan (Sterling Hayden), a portentous gunfighter, has traded his revolver for a guitar. With her he heads to the Vienna Playhouse (Joan Crawford), where a job as a musician awaits him, and an old love. Emma Small (Mercedes McCambridge) hates Vienna because the Dancin' Kid (Scott Brady) prefers it to her. So when the stage is robbed and Emma's brother turns up dead, she doesn't hesitate to blame it on the Dancin' Kid and his gang. He will fight to destroy Vienna and its dreams of prosperity with the railroad.

anchor_text: Mother Bernle is a widow living in Bavaria with her four sons: Franz, Johann, Andreas, and Joseph. She's very well-liked in the village, her only wish is for her children's future. One day Joseph receives a job offer from America. Immediately Mother Bernle collects the family savings to allow her son to leave for New York. Here, in a short time, Joseph opens a gourmet shop, and marries an American woman with whom he has a son. Just at that time World War I begins, Franz and Johann are called to arms and leave for the eastern front. At first the only son who remains at home is Andreas, who however is forced to enlist after the news of the death of the two brothers. Mother Bernle finds herself so alone, and when the United States also enters the war, she suffers the disgrace of hearing Joseph called a "traitor" for fighting with an enemy country of Germany. The two brothers meet only briefly in battle on the Western Front, where Andreas is wounded and dies in Joseph's arms. When the war is over, Joseph returns to New York, where he finds his son, his wife, and his thriving store. He writes to Mother Bernle inviting her to join him and reunite the family. Before she can do that, Mother Bernle has to pass an immigrant English test.
text_a: Ten years after the end of the war, young Franz returns home to Vienna after a train trip from Russia, believing his mother dead. That same evening, in Vienna, a mother thinks of her son who left many years ago for the front, and who is still waiting. Arriving in Vienna, on the night of his return, the young man relieves a misfortune by rescuing a girl who had fallen into the river, while a photographer captures the scene. The next morning the picture appears in the newspapers and the mother thinks she recognizes him as her son. When she manages to track Franz down, she brings him home where she surrounds him with attentions. Planning to reunite him with his ex-girlfriend, Anni, he takes the alleged son to her wealthy parents' home, where a reception takes place, but no one recognizes him as the woman's real son. The two return home and in the modest apartment find Anni waiting for them, to console them of the disappointment. Anni and Franz become friends, to the great satisfaction of her mother, who, to facilitate the marriage, falsifies documents to buy a taxi for her son and get him a job. Franz, who knows nothing, goes to pick up the taxi and goes to Anni, whose parents are against the relationship. Years so he doesn't get it and he's upset about not causing an accident. At the police station the mother's deception is discovered, but the inspector, pitied by the woman's pleas, lets them go free. At the exit, the mother and Franz find Anni waiting for them in the car and so they all leave together.
text_b: In a small Bavarian town resides the widow Frau Bernle (Margaret Mann), who lives for her four children. The idyllic rural atmosphere will be shattered by the outbreak of the Great War, which will cause the dissolution of the family. The eldest son, Joseph (James Hall), will be able to emigrate to the still neutral United States thanks to his mother's savings. The next two, Franz (Ralph Bushman) and Johann (Charles Morton), will enlist as volunteers in the Reich army. The death of the two brothers on the Eastern Front will not prevent the ruthless Major Von Stomm (Earle Foxe) from mobilizing little Andreas (George Meeker) as well. Alone at home, Frau Bernle will also receive the news of the death of young Andreas, who has died in the arms of his own brother Joseph, enlisted in the American army. When Joseph returns safely to New York, where his wife and son are waiting for him, he decides to call his mother to live with his American family. After overcoming the last hurdles of Ellis Island's immigration bureaucracy, the mother is reunited with the rest of her family.[1]

anchor_text: Condemned by the courts, a group of experienced criminals agree to participate in a government space mission, which is doomed from the start, whose goal is to find alternative energy sources and take part in reproduction experiments...
text_a: The Wheel, an artificial satellite, is in orbit about 1,000 miles [1,600 km] above the Earth. Six brave volunteers observe on board a diet consisting only of various capsules. Within this small community, Imoto, Fodor, and Siegle are eventually assigned to sail aboard the Épervier, a spacecraft under construction. Once the spacecraft is completed, the three astronauts set off on a mission of exploration and land on Mars.
text_b: A group of convicts and death row inmates are headed, in a spaceship, away from the solar system because they're part of a scientific experiment to investigate black holes.

anchor_text: Humanity has been served throughout its history by various computers that gradually develop artificial intelligence: Multivac is followed by Microvac, then the Galactic AC, the Universal AC, the Cosmic AC and finally AC (Automatic Computer). The "self-correcting and self-regulating" Multivac was originally built in the 21st century to calculate the orbits of distant planets, but on May 21, 2061, a technician asks it the title-giving final question: "Can entropy ever be reversed?" Multivac and its more advanced forms always give the same answer: There is not enough data to give a meaningful answer. As time progresses, the Milky Way and eventually even thousands of galaxies will be populated by humans who evolve more and more transhuman to the point of disembodiance. Concerned about their own existence and future energy supply - one sun after another burning out and collapsing - people ask this last unanswered question over and over again. Eventually, in the distant future, one galaxy after another dies out, the universe dies the heat death. Humans who have long existed disembodied are merging with the superintelligence AC that exists in hyperspace. Just before the very last moment, when all the data is captured and correlated and the universe dies around it, AC can finally answer the final question: "Let there be light!" and there was light.
text_a: During a hot summer in a 1970s Leningrad high-rise, astrophysicist Malyanov is on the verge of a breakthrough with his work on The Interaction of Stars and Diffuse Matter. However, he is prevented from completing it by a series of unusual events: he receives an unordered delivery of already paid delicacies; an attractive stranger, claiming to be a school friend of his wife, stands at the door and spends the night in his apartment; his neighbor, who works for a secret government project, commits suicide or is murdered; Malyanov is questioned and suspected by the police. Malyanov learns from some well-known scientists from other disciplines that they too are being deterred from their work, sometimes by even more drastic and absurd measures. Is there an extraterrestrial super-civilization behind these events, or is it the age-old secret League of Nine, both of whom want to stop the progress of mankind? His neighbor, the mathematician Wetscherwski, hypothesizes that humanity's increasing knowledge in the distant future will threaten the homeostatic world building, the balance between increasing reason and non-decreasing entropy, and that current events are the world building's first reaction to the danger that humanity could transform itself into a supercivilization. The World Building is defending itself. Under increasing pressure, scientists stop their research for various reasons, but Wetscherovsky does not give up, believing that this is a law of nature to be studied and then used. With the works of others, he wants to fight the universe alone in the solitude of the Pamir.
text_b: The story tells the development of the computer called Multivac through several scenes spaced out in time. Each one follows the same pattern: after a discussion about the future of humanity, two people ask Multivac if we'll ever be able to reverse entropy. Invariably Multivac responds that it does not have enough data to give a meaningful answer, and that it will gather more information. Over time, Multivac evolves and becomes more efficient. As it feeds on matter and energy, it gradually changes its name, to mark its dimensional changes: Microvac, Galactic AC, Universal AC, Cosmic AC, and finally AC. An infinite time after the disappearance of man and space-time, A.C. finds a way to reverse entropy. With no one left to answer to, and all the matter and energy of the universe absorbed, a demonstration of the answer seemed appropriate. The story ends with the words "Let there be light", and the light was...

His neighbor, the mathematician Wetscherwski, hypothesizes that humanity's increasing knowledge in the distant future will threaten the homeostatic world building, the balance between increasing reason and non-decreasing entropy, and that current events are the world building's first reaction to the danger that humanity could transform itself into a supercivilization. The World Building is defending itself. With the works of others, he wants to fight the universe alone in the solitude of the Pamir.
text_b: The story tells of the evolution of a computer called Multivac or AC and its relationship with humanity, explained through seven different historical-temporal settings. The first takes place in the year 2061. In each of the first six settings, one of the characters asks the computer a question, namely, how to deal with the threat to human survival from the thermal death of the universe. As the characters in the story themselves acknowledge, the question is equivalent to saying, "Can the second law of thermodynamics be reversed?" Each time the computer finds itself unable to answer, claiming to have "insufficient data for a meaningful answer", until the final episode, when the computer, the only sentient entity in a now-dead universe, after collecting all the possible data, finally finds the answer to the last question. In the end, AC manages to recreate a new universe:

anchor_text: Venice 1763: Lorenzo Da Ponte converts to Christianity, becomes a priest, is a Freemason and is known to Casanova. While gambling in the casino, Da Ponte meets a wealthy Venetian who wants to give him his daughter (Annetta) as a wife. For writing free-thinking writings, blasphemy and concubinage, Lorenzo Da Ponte is banished from Venice for 15 years. Armed with a letter of recommendation from Casanova to Antonio Salieri, Da Ponte set off for Vienna, where he arrived in 1781. Here he is introduced to Salieri, to be able to work for him as a librettist. Joseph II arranges for Da Ponte to compose the libretto for an Italian opera (Le Nozze di Figaro) by Mozart, which becomes a success. The Emperor orders another opera from Mozart, for which Lorenzo Da Ponte writes the libretto and lets his own private life flow into the story: Don Giovanni. Annetta is also living in Vienna with her aunt after the death of her father. She takes music lessons with Mozart, through whom she meets Da Ponte again. The opera singer Catarina Cavalieri, jealous of this, receives from Casanova a list of all Da Ponte's past loves, which she sends to Annetta. As a result, Annetta becomes estranged from Lorenzo, but through the opera Don Giovanni recognizes parallels to Da Ponte and herself, so that the two find each other again.
text_a: Venice, 1763: Lorenzo Da Ponte, a young priest and man of letters of Jewish origin, is sentenced by the Venetian Inquisition to a fifteen-year exile because of his unprejudiced libertine conduct. Arriving in the more liberal Vienna, Lorenzo is recommended by his friend Giacomo Casanova to the composer Antonio Salieri, for his literary talent. In the Austrian capital, Emperor Joseph, his great admirer, proposes to him to deal with the libretto of Le nozze di Figaro by the famous Mozart. The opera is a success. Thus began a long collaboration with the master, for whom he was subsequently commissioned to write the libretto of a new opera, Don Giovanni. The relationship between the two artists becomes increasingly close, becoming one another's shoulder. In the course of the new draft, Lorenzo meets again Annetta, his former Italian lover. For her, he decides to leave his wife (the singer Adriana Ferrarese), despite the fact that Annetta is already engaged. From this moment the image of Don Giovanni changes: from a libertine similar to Casanova, he becomes more similar to Da Ponte himself, a man who after a life of vices finds true love.
text_b: After years of wandering, Giacomo Casanova approaches his hometown of Venice, from which he was once banished after his legendary escape from the Bleach Chambers. Now, in the autumn of his life, impoverished and without prospects, he decides to put himself in the service of his hometown to make it possible for him to return home. But first, he wants to win Marcolina's heart, which, the more he desires her, the more she refuses him. On the estate of Olivio, an old acquaintance whom he had once helped out of a financial predicament and whose wife he had seduced, Casanova bids one last time for a woman he is hopelessly in love with. But she rejects him. Casanova uses a ruse to get to his goal. At a poker night, Lorenzo, Marcolina's lover, is playing for head and neck. He owes the Marquis de Cesi 2,000 lire, the exact amount that Casanova just won. Casanova proposes a pact to the lieutenant: he will settle his debts, in exchange he will take over the love night with Marcolina in his place. The lieutenant agrees. Casanova immediately rushes to Marcolina's bed, who in the darkness mistakes him for Lorenzo. It is only in the early morning that she realizes with whom she has spent this hot night of love. Embarrassed, she hides her face in her hands. Lorenzo, plagued by jealousy, awaits Casanova in the courtyard and challenges him to a duel. Casanova has agreed. A fight ensues in a secluded olive grove, and the exasperated and angry Lorenzo runs into the serene and sovereign Casanova's blade. For Casanova, this marks the end of another chapter in his life. In the final scene, he is seen with his servant Camille riding a gondola through the canals of Venice, facing his destiny as a spy of the Serenissima.

Thus began a long collaboration with the master, for whom he was subsequently commissioned to write the libretto of a new opera, Don Giovanni. The relationship between the two artists becomes increasingly close, becoming one another's shoulder. In the course of the new draft, Lorenzo meets again Annetta, his former Italian lover. For her, he decides to leave his wife (the singer Adriana Ferrarese), despite the fact that Annetta is already engaged. From this moment the image of Don Giovanni changes: from a libertine similar to Casanova, he becomes more similar to Da Ponte himself, a man who after a life of vices finds true love.
text_a: After years of wandering, Giacomo Casanova approaches his hometown of Venice, from which he was once banished after his legendary escape from the Bleach Chambers. Now, in the autumn of his life, impoverished and without prospects, he decides to put himself in the service of his hometown to make it possible for him to return home. But first, he wants to win Marcolina's heart, which, the more he desires her, the more she refuses him. On the estate of Olivio, an old acquaintance whom he had once helped out of a financial predicament and whose wife he had seduced, Casanova bids one last time for a woman he is hopelessly in love with. But she rejects him. Casanova uses a ruse to get to his goal. At a poker night, Lorenzo, Marcolina's lover, is playing for head and neck. He owes the Marquis de Cesi 2,000 lire, the exact amount that Casanova just won. Casanova proposes a pact to the lieutenant: he will settle his debts, in exchange he will take over the love night with Marcolina in his place. The lieutenant agrees. Casanova immediately rushes to Marcolina's bed, who in the darkness mistakes him for Lorenzo. It is only in the early morning that she realizes with whom she has spent this hot night of love. Embarrassed, she hides her face in her hands. Lorenzo, plagued by jealousy, awaits Casanova in the courtyard and challenges him to a duel. Casanova has agreed. A fight ensues in a secluded olive grove, and the exasperated and angry Lorenzo runs into the serene and sovereign Casanova's blade. For Casanova, this marks the end of another chapter in his life. In the final scene, he is seen with his servant Camille riding a gondola through the canals of Venice, facing his destiny as a spy of the Serenissima.
text_b: It tells the story of Lorenzo da Ponte (Lorenzo Balducci), who was a friend of Giacomo Casanova (Tobias Moretti) in Venice and a lyricist for Wolfgang Amadeus Mozart (Lino Guanciale). This man participated in such popular works as The Marriage of Figaro, Così fan tutte or Don Giovanni. He worked in the Austrian capital with Mozart after being banished from Italy by the Inquisition "because of his licentious life and full of mischief". Thanks to the help of fellow composer Antonio Salieri (Ennio Fantastichini), of whom he was also librettist, he will move to Vienna where although he is obliged to write works in Italian, he will do so for several composers. After the death of Emperor Joseph II (Robert Accornero) he will be forced to leave Vienna, and after a stay in London, he will finally move to New York City. It's a dramatic story with a lot of passion.

anchor_text: Ivan Velikopolsky is the son of a coachman and studies at the Priestly Academy. Ivan spends Easter at home in the village with his parents. First of all, he's going on a snowplow on Good Friday. On his way home in the evening, Ivan passes by the widow Vasilissa's garden. The widow and her daughter, Lukerja, also widowed, warm themselves outside by an open, crackling wood fire. Ivan says hello and walks up. You're warming up and agreeing that winter wants to come back for Easter. Looking at the fire, Ivan recalls a passage from the Passion of Christ, the scene of the servants warming themselves by a wood fire in the courtyard of the high priest's palace. Ivan, a student of theology, preaches to the two women the well-known story of Peter's three denials of his Lord Jesus. The widow Vasilissa is crying. Ivan is convinced that the widow sheds tears, not because he is able to preach so movingly, but because she has internalized what happened in Peter's day. Peter appears to Ivan as the personification of the guilt that man loads on himself at some point in life in the sense: Everything repeats itself in the course of the world and if it is that part of the passion story mentioned above from two thousand years ago in front of the palace of the high priest: Vasilissa with her fire in the vegetable garden has closed the circle.
text_a: Children find in a ravine a grain of wheat as big as a hen's egg; a man buys it from them for a loaf of bread and sells it to the Tsar as a rare commodity. The ruler wanted to know where grain of this size came from and why no one had ever seen anything like it; his wise men did not know and recommended that he ask the farmers. The Tsar calls for a very old toothless, almost blind peasant, who arrives at the palace on two crutches; he says that he has never seen anything like it and advises the Tsar to ask his father. The latter arrives on a single crutch, sees and hears better than his son, but cannot identify the grain and suggests asking his father the question. This one, in good health, with good legs and shining eyes, looks at the grain and tells the Tsar that, in his time, such a grain grew everywhere and that all the peasants fed only on this kind of grain.
text_b: On a cold and windy evening of Good Friday, Ivan Velikopolsky, a student of the Ecclesiastical Academy and the son of a deacon, returns home from hunting. Ivan stops in front of the home of two widows, mother Vasilisa and daughter Lukerâ; while warming himself by the fire, the young man reminds the two women of the events that took place on Good Friday nineteen centuries ago, when Peter denied Jesus three times. During the narration, the two women are moved and Vasilisa suddenly begins to sob. Later, as he walked home, the student thought of Vasilisa: if she had started to cry, it meant that what had happened to Peter that horrible night had something to do with her. Ivan becomes convinced that the past is linked to the present by an unbroken chain of events arising from one another, and life seemed to him wonderful, magnificent and full of high meaning.

Ivan, a student of theology, preaches to the two women the well-known story of Peter's three denials of his Lord Jesus. The widow Vasilissa is crying. text_a: Children find in a ravine a grain of wheat as big as a hen's egg; a man buys it from them for a loaf of bread and sells it to the Tsar as a rare commodity. The ruler wanted to know where grain of this size came from and why no one had ever seen anything like it; his wise men did not know and recommended that he ask the farmers. The Tsar calls for a very old toothless, almost blind peasant, who arrives at the palace on two crutches; he says that he has never seen anything like it and advises the Tsar to ask his father. The latter arrives on a single crutch, sees and hears better than his son, but cannot identify the grain and suggests asking his father the question. This one, in good health, with good legs and shining eyes, looks at the grain and tells the Tsar that, in his time, such a grain grew everywhere and that all the peasants fed only on this kind of grain.
text_b: On a Good Friday night, young Ivan Velikopolsky, son of a sacristan and student of a clergyman, walks home and imagines that the wind, cold and darkness he feels was also experienced throughout history by characters like Rurik, Ivan the Terrible and Peter the Great. Along the way, he meets Vasilisa and Lukerya, a mother and her daughter, both widows. As he sits with them around their fire, Ivan tells them that the warmth they feel must also have been experienced by Peter, further asking them if they had ever read the gospels. Vasilisa confirms that they have indeed done so. Ivan begins to tell the story of Peter's denial, in which Jesus foretells to the apostle that he will deny him three times before the next cock crows. Peter follows Jesus to his arrest and meets with some workers in the courtyard, standing around a fire, as he is interrogated. Three times the workers ask Peter if he knows Jesus, and three times the apostle denies it. A cock crows and Peter, realizing what he has done, bursts into tears. Ivan finishes his story while Vasilisa begins to cry and Lukerya watches him with deep sorrow. The young man bids the widows farewell and continues home, thinking that his story moved Lukerya and made Vasilisa cry, not because of the way he told it, but because the story was relevant to them nineteen centuries later. As he travels by ferry to his village, Ivan comes to believe that truth and beauty serve as the connectors of all human history and that he is filled with joy and fear of life itself.[1]

anchor_text: Khédidja works for a wealthy Parisian family who offers to take care of the children during a summer in Corsica. The opportunity for her to return with her daughters, Jessica and Farah, to the island they left fifteen years earlier in tragic circumstances. While Khédidja struggles with her memories, the two teenagers succumb to all the summer temptations: unexpected encounters, 400 punches, first love experiences. This trip will be an opportunity for them to discover a hidden part. ] of their history.
text_a: Keiko is a young, delusional single mother who lives alone with her four children - Akira, Kyoko, Shigeru, and Yuki. Between the ages of five and twelve, they come from four different fathers. In order to move into a larger apartment than the previous one, Keiko hid the existence of the three younger ones from the landlord. None of the children are in school. Akira, the eldest, handles the household chores and keeps the accounts with the help of his sister Kyoko while their mother works. And Keiko keeps coming home late and late, until one day she doesn't come home at all. Akira discovers that she has made a new life in Osaka with her new lover. The children are left to fend for themselves.
text_b: Kheìdidja is in her 40's and the mother of two teenage daughters, Jessica and Farah. He works for a wealthy family in Paris. One day, his bosses offer him the opportunity to spend the summer in Corsica and take care of his daughters. Although Kheìdidja and her daughters left the island 15 years ago under tragic circumstances, she accepts. She's got Jessica and Farah with her. While the girls make the most of the summer and live their first love experiences, Khédidja struggles with her memories. However, the journey offers you the opportunity to discover a part of its history. Mother and daughters also strengthen their bonds.[2]

anchor_text: Kheididja is in her 40's and the mother of two teenage daughters, Jessica and Farah. She works for a wealthy family in Paris. Her employers offer to take her to Corsica one summer to look after their children. Although Kheididja and her daughters left the island 15 years ago under tragic circumstances, she agrees. She's accompanied by Jessica and Farah. While the girls enjoy the summer to the fullest and experience their first love, Khédidja struggles with her memories. Nevertheless, the journey offers an opportunity to discover a part of their history. Mothers and daughters also become closer.
text_a: In Tokyo, 12-year-old Akira Fukushima moves in with his mother Keiko, an irresponsible young woman, in a small apartment in the capital. The boy's three younger brothers - Kyoko, Shigeru, and Yuki - also sneaked in. One day the mother of the four boys leaves to pursue the man she has fallen in love with leaving the eldest son a small amount of money that soon runs out. As time passes, their mother disappears, the bills are not paid, and there is no money to buy food. But Akira will not give up and will look for any way to help his family.
text_b: Khédidja works for a wealthy Parisian family who offers to take care of the children during a summer in Corsica. The opportunity for her to return with her daughters, Jessica and Farah, to the island they left fifteen years earlier in tragic circumstances. While Khédidja struggles with her memories, the two teenagers succumb to all the summer temptations: unexpected encounters, 400 punches, first love experiences. This trip will be an opportunity for them to discover a hidden part. ] of their history.

anchor_text: Thomas Vlassenroot, a young man from Luxembourg, fought in Kabylie in the French Foreign Legion during the Algerian War. In 1961, after the putsch of the generals and while the OAS was trying to keep Algeria to France, Thomas deserted and took refuge in Algiers with his friend Maria. His lieutenant, now in the OAS, offers him to participate in a commando operation: it is to abduct a lawyer, Dominique Servet, who came to defend two Algerian revolutionaries. In return, Thomas will receive enough money to return to Luxembourg. The kidnapping is carried out, with the help of a blackfoot, Amério. Dominique is imprisoned with another prisoner, Félicien, under the care of Amério and Thomas. This one, pitied by the young woman, helps her escape after shooting Amério...
text_a: Thomas Vlassenroot is a young man who after deserting the French Foreign Legion joins the OAS, the paramilitary group formed by the rebel generals to De Gaulle after the abandonment of Algeria. Convinced by Lieutenant Fraser, he agrees to kidnap Dominique Servet, a lawyer fighting for the Algerian revolutionaries, in exchange for a large sum of money that will allow him to return home to Luxembourg. The group succeeds in their attempt, Dominique is imprisoned along with another prisoner, Félicien, under the guard of Thomas and Amério, a pieds-noirs. Thomas, however, ends up falling in love with his victim, not hesitating to turn against his comrades.
text_b: This is Marseille, 1924. Alain Lefèvre was a famous boxer. He accepts a proposal from Lucien Galgani, the local mafia boss, who asks him to lie down during his next fight. Filled with remorse and challenged by his opponent, Alain finally decides to ignore the deal and win. In an attempt to escape, he is forced to kill the mobster's younger brother. Alain's brother is killed and his ex-girlfriend, Katrina, is kidnapped. Persecuted, Alain finds refuge in a barracks of the Foreign Legion and decides to enroll there, to escape his assailants. He will therefore have to face the most ferocious and difficult training, in the middle of the Sahara desert in Morocco. He was then sent to a fort not far from the Rif besieged by Riffian rebels led by Abdekrim El-Khattabi. Meanwhile, in Marseille, the Mafia manages to track him down and sends men to find and kill him. Alain will therefore have to face many dangers in a country in the midst of a colonial war.

anchor_text: Louis Simo, a fictional detective, investigates the historically real death of actor George Reeves, who achieved success in the 1950s with the television series Adventures of Superman, in which he played the title role. The scenes of the investigation are alternated with flashbacks that trace Reeves' life. Toni Mannix, the wife of studio head Eddie Mannix, was having an affair with Reeves. She gave him expensive gifts like a watch with the words "Mad About the Boy" engraved on it. Reeves ended the relationship with Toni and began a relationship with Leonore Lemmon. Shortly before his wedding to Leonore, George Reeves is found dead in his bedroom. It is suspected that he committed suicide. Detective Simo discovers inconsistencies and publicly questions the official version of his death. He suspects, among others, his fiancée Leonore, studio head Eddie Mannix and George's ex-girlfriend Toni of having committed or arranged George's murder. In the end, Simo realizes that Reeves killed himself due to his lack of success. However, this will not be resolved.
text_a: After the suicide of his wife Rosa, 45-year-old Paul, an American transplanted in Paris, seems to have lost all reason to live. Wandering aimlessly through the city, he meets the twenty-year-old Jeanne in a rented apartment, which the two casually find themselves visiting together: a passionate attraction is triggered, and the two strangers have an apartment complex in the empty apartment, which they take as pied-à-terre. A fleeting relationship develops between them, during which, ignoring everything about the other partner, even the name, they explore each other's sexualities in depth. Jeanne, the daughter of a colonel and romantically involved with a young film director, with whom she is making an artisanal film, falls in love with Paul who does not correspond with her, but leaves her. Paul, who with his deceased wife ran a low-level hotel, receives in the meantime the visit of his mother-in-law, who came to bury her daughter, and deepens his knowledge of Marcel, a kind lover of his wife and pensioner of the hotel, who has a dressing gown identical to his own. He then finds himself in love with Jeanne, and some time later intercepts her and chases her to a ballroom where a tango competition is taking place. Here the two drink until they are drunk, while Paul tells Jeanne the details of his life, omitting his name, proposing to live together, but for Jeanne it is over. When she flees to his house, Paul, drunk, chases after her and she, terrified, at his request reveals her name to him, then kills him with her father's service pistol.
text_b: On June 16, 1959, one of Hollywood's most famous news events occurred: Actor George Reeves, star of the popular television series Adventures of Superman, committed suicide. Everything seems all too clear, so much so that many begin to doubt that it could really have been this way: the private investigator Louis Simo, without money and work, is therefore interested in the case and convinces Reeves' mother to hire him to find out the truth. After suspecting, respectively, Leonore Lemmon (the actor's new and unstable young flame), Toni Mannix (the former betrayed and abandoned) and her husband Eddie (head of MGM, who discovers that his wife has betrayed him with the actor), the investigator bitterly concludes that it was a "simple" suicide. Not only that: by opening himself up to a world where everything is false, cynical and opportunistic (even Reeves' mother, in fact, is more interested in money and the fame of her deceased son than in the truth) Simo understands that his life is also in danger of taking a bad turn. The film ends with Simo going to his ex-wife's house for the first time sober, clean, and determined to reconnect with his son.

anchor_text: In this play, Brecht discusses the struggle of two men in the metropolis of Chicago. Brecht had a great interest in the phenomenon of boxing during its formative years. For no apparent reason, the timber merchant Shlink, originally from what is now Malaysia, provokes George Garga, an employee of the lending library, and gets into a heated argument with him. In the process, the shop where Garga works is damaged and Garga is fired. However, Garga takes revenge by destroying Shlink's lumber business, which he leaves to him. Afterwards, he wants to go to Tahiti to be free; but now that Shlink also drags Garga's family into the fight, Garga has to abandon this plan. Garga's friend Jane and his sister Marie are now forced into prostitution by Shlink and his friends from the underworld. However, Garga is not fooled and marries Jane despite her lost honor. However, Shlink now claims to the authorities that Garga is a procrastinator, so he has to go to prison, which also destroys his family's cohesion. Now Garga begins to defend himself not only by his own actions, but also shows Shlink just before his release from three years in prison for the rape of his sister Marie, so that Shlink has to hide in a thicket, fearing that he will be lynched as a stranger and accused. Now, however, the fight turns into a strange relationship of mutual obedience, for both of them run away together. Shlink wants to hand over his rebuilt timber trade to Garga and confesses his love to him. However, when Garga is unwilling to make peace, Shlink takes poison. Garga burns down the lumber business and goes to New York.
text_a: Jim Bennett is a highly indebted and gambling professor of literature. He borrows money from several loan sharks and gambles it away. His mother Roberta gives him $260,000 to pay off his debts and save his life. He gambles away that money, too. One of the criminal moneylenders finds out that basketball player Lamar is in Bennett's class and forces Bennett to get Lamar to win the next game by no more than 8 points. Bennett borrows money again and bets anonymously on the basketball game. But even the large profit is not enough to pay off his two main creditors, who are now threatening his life. He meets the two of them at a gambling establishment controlled by an even more powerful underworld figure, so that neither of them can forcibly take his money. At the roulette table, he puts all his money from the basketball bet on black, wins, and for the first time doesn't just play on. He leaves the winnings on the table for his creditors and leaves the gambling hell with not a penny in his pocket. Outside, he throws his jacket into a garbage can and rejects the offer of one of the two moneylenders to pay him his six-figure surplus from the roulette game. At dawn, he runs free across the city and ends up knocking on the door of his student Amy, who has fallen in love with him over the course of the film.
text_b: The protagonists of Im Dickicht are two, George Garga and Shlink. Garga came from the savannah to the city in the company of his family and at the beginning of the play he is introduced to us as a simple clerk of a library. It is here that the whole affair will begin. In fact, Shlink is going to the above-mentioned library not to buy, as we would expect, a book but Garga's opinions on it. The latter, however, has no intention of selling his ideas, and Shlink realizes that he is facing a fighter, a worthy opponent for a challenge. From this moment begins between the two a ruthless struggle for reasons totally unknown, if there were any reasons. Both characters have ambiguous and difficult to define personalities, the only thing that seems to be evident is the great difference between the characters of the two. Cold-blooded, insensitive even to his own family and with power mania taken to the extreme. Shlink gentle, willing to submit and capable of human feelings such as love. Although throughout the course of the drama it is he who appears as the victim of the ruthless Garga, he is in reality the moral winner of the struggle. Garga did not understand what the struggle really was, he had always wanted the end of Shlink, the latter, on the other hand, only wanted to continue the struggle for himself: "it was not about the body, but the spirit". A metaphysical struggle, then, like that of the protagonists of Jensen's novel. Both characters are united by a deep inner loneliness that leads them to become totally involved in the fight and become addicted to it, particularly Garga. Brecht in this play uses numerous quotations from Rimbaud through which he criticizes contemporary society and praises primitivism and wildlife.

anchor_text: The famous Jean Claude Van Damme returns to his native country, Belgium, facing simultaneous difficulties in his acting career, tax problems and a lawsuit for the custody of his daughter (who suffers from quolibets caused by his father's scandals and his tarnished reputation in the media). It was then that he happened to be the victim of a hostage situation at a post office in Schaerbeek (Rue Van Schoor No. 61a). Seen from the outside by a police officer, the actor is mistaken for the perpetrator of the hostage-taking, leading to a media frenzy. At first upset by this unexpected situation, threatening to jeopardize their modest fortune, the three thugs decide to take advantage of it, demanding through him a much larger ransom. One of them is an admirer of Jean-Claude Van Damme, and tries to establish with the actor a relationship of exalted familiarity, both authoritarian and puerile. He then experiences loneliness, loss of control, a much more real anguish (in the context of the film's abysm) and yet much less spectacular than what he is accustomed to in his often very violent action roles. As TV footage of him being ridiculed is played over and over again, crowds of terrified fans chant his name, his own parents are summoned to beg him to surrender, and his lawyer, exasperated by the bad publicity, declares that he is divesting himself of all his assets. He is then, more than ever, faced with himself and his media image, faced with the truth and facticity of his character.
text_a: The famous Belgian actor Jean-Claude Van Damme, who became famous all over the world thanks to the Hollywood martial arts films that featured him as the protagonist, returns to his native village to seek some peace: his career, in fact, has come to a standstill, while his personal life is shaken by the process of obtaining custody of his daughter after the divorce. The actor goes to the post office to make a withdrawal with which to pay his lawyer, who threatens to abandon his advocacy in the case, but just at that moment a robbery is underway: the three bad guys, seeing his celebrity, decide to hold him hostage along with the others and make him mediate with the police. The actor, for his part, decides to take advantage of the situation to get the money with which to pay the lawyer by selling it for the ransom of the prisoners. The hours of imprisonment will serve Jean-Claude to make an honest and open-hearted assessment of his entire existence, regretting that he has given nothing back to that life that made him so much luckier than so many others. He will eventually be sentenced to a year in prison for attempted extortion, but in prison he will have a chance to reconcile with his daughter.
text_b: The local Wehrmacht commander in Nantes is shot dead in the street by three youths belonging to the communist part of the Résistance (Resistance in France). The three assassins escaped unidentified. Hitler immediately orders the execution of 150 French/Communist hostages in retaliation. In the German headquarters in Paris, General Stülpnagel tries to negotiate the number of hostages with Berlin. On behalf of the military commander, Jünger writes an internal protocol on the "hostage shooting" (the paper will be published after 2000). The hostage list is being reviewed by Monsieur Lecornu. He notices that young people are also interested in it. He gets permission from Commander Kristucat to change the list. However, there are complications with the execution in Paris and the list has to be restored to its original state. The film also tells the story of the fictional soldier Otto, who is on military duty in France. He becomes unstable during the execution of the 27 condemned because he can't stand the execution process. In the prison camp in Choisel, you hear the story of Guy Môquet, who lives there. We find out he has a girlfriend, Odette Nilés. He's also friends with the student Claude Lalet. He is to be released on the day of the execution, but is mistakenly added to the list of those to be executed. On October 22, 1941, Claude, Guy and 25 other Frenchmen are executed. The suicide note of one of the two youngest victims, Guy Môquet, will be revealed later. All those involved in the film on the German side finally succeed in portraying this massacre as a purely administrative act. The film does not address the question of the legality and appropriateness of the shooting of the hostages from today's point of view.

anchor_text: Off the coast of Panama, there is an island with two penal colonies, one for men and one for women. The superintendent of the women's correctional facility is Thelma Diaz. The harsh guardian is characterized by a marked sadism. The other penitentiary is run by a lecherous superintendent who, for his own amusement, occasionally lets himself be seduced by female inmates from the women's prison. When several female prisoners mysteriously die, including the severely abused Natalie, Thelma is finally relieved and replaced by the liberal criminal justice Leonie. However, she retains her predecessor as her assistant. Soon Leonie's gentleness and forbearance are used against her, and she is accused of having developed a lesbian relationship with Marie, a delicate inmate with whom she has befriended. Eventually, the pressure becomes so great that Marie, along with her fellow prisoners Helga and Rosalie, makes an escape attempt. He succeeds, and the three escapees go into hiding in the nearby jungle. One of the escaped women soon dies from the late effects of the injuries she suffered as a result of being raped by several male prisoners from the other penal colony. The other two escapees are soon recaptured and flogged for their escape. When a prison riot breaks out, Leonie is removed from office and Thelma is reinstated. The governor of the island hopes that their much tougher gait will teach the women more. But he is mistaken. But in the end, the recent horrifying events on the prison island seem to have an impact, as Leonie learns that an extensive investigation is imminent and criminal justice reforms in favor of the violated prison inmates are in the offing.
text_a: On a remote island off the coast, a women's prison is set up, run by a ruthless executioner, Thelma Diaz, as inhuman with the prisoners as she is willing - to please the dishonest governor and gain his complicity - to force younger and more beautiful women to indulge him. Things seem to take a turn for the worse for the two when, following the deaths of some inmates due to mistreatment and lack of medical care, an inspector, Miss Carol, is sent to the penitentiary. The woman manages to improve the living conditions of the prisoners but without obtaining their gratitude while, to get rid of her, Telma and the governor accuse her to the superiors of excessive (and equivocal) interest in a prisoner. One night, taking advantage of Carol's lack of outside guards, the three girls escape, but after a grueling escape into the jungle, they are attacked by a group of prisoners, escaped from another penitentiary, and forced to turn back. One of them dies and the other two are captured. While Telma and the governor - to whom the escape offered an excellent pretext to make Carol leave - restore the prison to the conditions before her arrival, the defeated inspector leaves the island forever, under the contemptuous gaze of the inmates.
text_b: Mia Baran, who suffered from heart disease, was a nun before she founded a boarding school in Pennsylvania and married Guy, a teacher who worked there. Guy is having an affair with Nicole Horner, a teacher employed at the boarding school. Nicole and Mia become friends anyway and decide to kill the unfaithful man. Mia sedates him and together with Nicole she throws him into a nearby pool. She is startled when the pool is emptied a little later, but there is no body in it. At the end of the story, Mia sees her husband alive in the bathtub and, apparently favoured by her weak heart, collapses. Now it turns out that Guy and Nicole were plotting to get rid of Mia. However, Nicole feels guilty about Mia's helplessness and turns against Guy. It becomes a life-and-death struggle and ultimately both women together drown Guy in a swimming pool. The final scene shows how the detective assigned to Guy's disappearance is helping to make the death case look like self-defense.

text_a: Several young women are incarcerated at El Castillo de la Muerte, a women's prison in the middle of the ocean. This prison is quite unusual because it is run by the director Thelma Diaz, a sadistic director who, together with the governor of the region, the so-called Santos, sexually abuse prisoners, especially one of them, number 99, Marie. But an unexpected event shocks the prison: the state has just appointed a new minister of justice who has opened an investigation into suspicious deaths in prison. He decides to send a young inspector, Leonie, to report to him; she upsets the life of the prison by her kindness and humanity towards the prisoners, including Mary. The latter was thrown into prison when she tried to warn the guards of the death of one of them, Nathalie, who died of peritonitis as a result of her ill-treatment. Diaz and the governor then decide to set a trap for Leonie... Meanwhile, Mary, freed by Leonie, escapes with another captive, Rosalie, and her lover. But they are soon hunted into the hostile jungle...
text_b: Mia Baran, who suffered from heart disease, was a nun before she founded a boarding school in Pennsylvania and married Guy, a teacher who worked there. Guy is having an affair with Nicole Horner, a teacher employed at the boarding school. Nicole and Mia become friends anyway and decide to kill the unfaithful man. Mia sedates him and together with Nicole she throws him into a nearby pool. She is startled when the pool is emptied a little later, but there is no body in it. At the end of the story, Mia sees her husband alive in the bathtub and, apparently favoured by her weak heart, collapses. Now it turns out that Guy and Nicole were plotting to get rid of Mia. However, Nicole feels guilty about Mia's helplessness and turns against Guy. It becomes a life-and-death struggle and ultimately both women together drown Guy in a swimming pool. The final scene shows how the detective assigned to Guy's disappearance is helping to make the death case look like self-defense.

anchor_text: Johnny (Jane Birkin), a waitress at a truck bar in the middle of nowhere, leads a lonely and bored life. She falls in love with a dump truck driver, Krassky (Joe Dallesandro), despite warnings from her boss Boris (Reinhard Kolldehoff), who tells her that he is gay. Due to the young woman's androgynous appearance, Krassky is nevertheless also attracted to her. However, their romance is thwarted by the jealousy of Krassky's boyfriend, Padovan (Hugues Quester).
text_a: Krassky is a gay truck driver who has a relationship with Padovan (now Patrice in the Italian version), his co-worker. In a remote club in the desert, run by Boris, works Johnny, a girl with an androgynous appearance. Krassky begins a relationship with her, but without any particular erotic transport towards the girl, and for this he has only anal relations with her. Patrice's jealousy does not serve to drive the two apart, however it comes to the point that Patrice tries to kill Johnny. Krassky intervenes and saves her, but Johnny's request to punish his friend drives him away from her.
text_b: Oscar Novak (Matthew Perry), a young architect, and his gay business partner, Peter Steinberg (Oliver Platt), are given a great opportunity to turn their careers around by Chicago tycoon Charles Newman (Dylan McDermott), who has chosen them to compete for the artwork of a multimillion-dollar cultural center. In a publicity stunt, Newman put Oscar and Peter in a head-to-head competition with two very famous and ruthless colleagues, Decker and Strauss. However, Oscar is mistaken for being gay during his meeting with Charles Newman. Falling into error, Newman asks Oscar to keep an eye on his lover Amy (Neve Campbell), a sympathetic and impulsive artist. Oscar falls in love with the girl, but has to pretend to be gay so as not to miss the opportunity to make a career.

anchor_text: Sand Rock, a small town in the Arizona desert: Writer John Putnam and his girlfriend Ellen watch a fireball fall near an old mine. John investigates the crash site and discovers a UFO, but a sudden rock slide buries it beneath him. In a town where John, an outsider, is considered an outsider, no one believes his observation. The temporary standoff has drawn the displeasure of Sheriff Warren, who has also made no secret of his dislike for John and Ellen's relationship. Shortly thereafter, the town's first inhabitants disappear, only to reappear later in strange shapes, cold and mechanical. Ellen's being kidnapped too. John descends into the mine, where he learns from an alien the background of the event: the crew of the spaceship had to make an emergency landing on Earth. In order not to attract attention, she has kidnapped people and takes their form herself, so that with a human appearance she can obtain the necessary materials for repair. Meanwhile, a group led by Sheriff Warren is approaching the mine. The peaceful aliens warn John that they are prepared to use force in self-defense. John tries in vain to stop the sheriff from attacking. Before there's an escalation, the aliens can launch their spaceship. The abducted humans return to the Earth's surface unharmed.
text_a: A fiery meteorite lights up the sky and falls to Earth near the desert town of Sand Rock, Arizona, interrupting a kiss between two lovers. Amateur astronomer John Putman and his girlfriend, Ellen Fields, rush to the site of the impact, an old mine, and discover that the fallen object is actually a spacecraft, hexagonal in shape and resembling a large luminous crystal. A landslide hides the ship, and when Putnam tells the town what he saw, no one believes him. Over the next few days, several locals disappear. They reappear in small numbers and exhibit bizarre and mechanical behavior, seem distracted and do not appear normal. The sheriff is convinced that something is wrong and organizes a hunt against the aliens. Only Putnam is looking for a peaceful solution, and he's just going to the mine to talk to the aliens. The aliens explain to him that they had a malfunction and accidentally crashed to Earth, so they have replicated some Earth individuals to repair the shuttle without arousing suspicion. Putnam manages in extremis to avoid a conflict, in which the earthlings would certainly have lost.
text_b: Arizona, 1867: A group of outlaws robs a bank in a small town. The outlaws are led by Stretch and Dude. Fleeing the cavalry, they found themselves in a salt desert. The first differences arise after the small spoils have been divided. At the end of the salt desert, they discover, as a last resort from thirst, a dead gold-mining city formerly known as "Yellow Sky". All that's left is an old man and his granddaughter Constance Mae, named Mike. The old man owns a considerable amount of gold that he's buried somewhere, which the gang finds out pretty quickly. The old prospector and his granddaughter will be challenged for their hard-earned treasure. Stretch, who has fallen in love with the girl, takes the "fair" view of sharing. The leader of the opposition, Dude, wants it all. Meanwhile, Apaches occupy the abandoned town, but the old man manages to convince the Indians to leave. The bandits split into two camps after they can't come to an agreement and eventually go at each other. Because Dude wants the gold for himself, there's a final gunfight at the saloon that Stretch can decide for himself. Stretch eventually returns the stolen money to the bank and rides off with Mike, the old man, and the two surviving gang members.

anchor_text: The camera exemplarily accompanies the lives of the polar bear girl Nanu and the walrus girl Seela. There are also polar foxes, seals, seagulls, narwhals, and beaked whales. It depicts the growth of two young animals, Nanu and Seela, as well as the changing habitat of the animals in the context of global warming. Among other things, it shows the difficult living conditions due to insufficient or months-long freezing of sea ice and increasingly violent storms.
text_a: The film shows the world's oceans and their inhabitants in all their facets: from the coast, where you can see crabs and orcas hunting on seals, to the open sea, where, for example, marlin and a sei whale play with a swarm of fish. From the polar regions with polar bears, penguins and belugas to the tropics with coral reefs and their inhabitants. From seabirds plunging into the sea to catch fish, to the lightless expanse of the deep sea with its wondrous creatures. From the tiny beach crab to the giant blue whale.
text_b: The documentary chronicles the early years of two cubs: one a polar bear, nicknamed Nanu, and one a walrus, named Seela. The cubs are taught by their mothers how to find food and how to avoid danger. It also shows how the effects of global warming are making it difficult for these animals to survive in the Arctic ice.

anchor_text: It's from 1897. Covington, a small, isolated, self-sufficient community in Pennsylvania, United States, lives in the terrifying certainty that dangerous creatures inhabit the woods surrounding its village: Those we don't speak of. This evil force is so menacing that no one dares venture beyond the last houses, much less enter the woods that mark the boundary between the villagers' territory and that of the creatures, bound by a tacit agreement not to violate the boundaries. In order to avoid any contact with the creatures, the color red is banned from the village as it is supposed to attract them, while the yellow that protects the villagers from the creatures is omnipresent. Despite the decision by the village elders never to return to the big cities, young Lucius Hunt, a reserved but determined boy, plans to go there to bring back medicine. But when her friend Noah, a young man with autism, tries to murder her, it's up to Ivy Walker, a young blind woman madly in love with Lucius, the man she was supposed to marry, to go on a dangerous quest to find the cure through the forbidden woods. But she has no idea what awaits her outside these woods.
text_a: It's about a village in a valley of Philadelphia surrounded by forests. The villagers are simple people, living modestly even by late nineteenth-century standards, but nevertheless happy. They must always be on their guard, for the forests around them are inhabited by enigmatic creatures capable of annihilating the community. Fortunately, there has always been a truce between the creatures and the villagers, with each side respecting the opposing territory. But when a villager receives a serious wound, it will be necessary for someone to cross the forest, in search of medicines to heal the wounded... and that could destroy the fragile truce between the village and the inhabitants of the forest.[1]
text_b: One morning, before going to school, Carolyn, an African-American girl, picks flowers in a meadow but falls into a pit dug in the ground and covered with vegetation. When she's reported missing, Sheriff Ben Kellogg's men begin their search. Evidence goes back to a man, a Claude Packard, relative of a local builder, who gave flowers to the girl and helped her cross the street. The man is stopped and the joint presses for release. The black community thinks you're covering up for a suspected child murderer. Soon violence erupts within the town, until a child, with the help of a dog, locates the well with the little girl inside, and can alert the authorities and stop the violence. Racial strife aside, the entire community is working to get her out of harm's way.

anchor_text: Reporter Malcolm Anderson works for a newspaper in Miami. He gets a call from a serial killer who says he likes Anderson's writing style. The serial killer offers Anderson some background information, which the journalist uses. The killer shows a growing interest in the reporter's private life and kidnaps his friend, teacher Christine Connelly.
text_a: After getting their hands on a shipment of drugs, Lenny "Pluto" Franklyn, Ray Malcolm and his girlfriend Fantasia head from Los Angeles to Star City, Arkansas, leaving behind a trail of violence. Detectives Dud Cole and John McFeely are on their trail while Sheriff Dale "Hurricane" Dixon awaits them. Fascinated by the possibility of participating in a manhunt, "Hurricane" will discover that the girl is actually his old flame Lila Walker.
text_b: Malcolm Anderson, a talented reporter for the Miami Herald, tired of dealing with the facts, is about to leave town to follow his girlfriend Christine Connelly to Colorado. He's assigned one last event to cover, the murder of a young woman. Shortly after his article was published, a man called him claiming to be the author of the murder and announcing that there would be more murders. The phone calls continue and the murders...

anchor_text: Gail Macaulay is a teenager about to graduate from high school. During an argument with her younger sister Joan, Joan tells her that she was adopted, meaning that Fred and Lois Macaulay are not her parents. Gail is shocked, although her boyfriend Chuck and her stepmother assure her that it is meaningless. She decides to look for her birth mother. The adoptive mother makes contact through her attorney, but Gail can't wait and drives from Los Angeles to Long Beach, where Mrs. Lynch, her birth mother, lives. The reunion does not go as Gail had imagined: Although the mother is friendly, she does not want to tell her second husband anything about her daughter. She's going back to her family. At her high school graduation, Gail is giving a speech about the true meaning of family.
text_a: In London, Hortense, a 27-year-old black woman whose adoptive mother has recently died, sets out to find her birth mother. This one turns out to be Cynthia, a white factory worker living in a working-class neighborhood and a single mother of another younger daughter, Roxanne, a road worker with whom she has serious relationship problems. The arrival of this hidden child, whom Cynthia had at the age of 15 and whom she did not even want to look at (she did not know that Hortense was black), will bring to light old secrets and lies that undermine the family's understanding secrets vis-à-vis her daughter, but also her younger brother, to whom she served as a second mother and whom she favored, as well as his wife.
text_b: Eighteen-year-old Gale lives happily with her parents and younger sisters Joan, sixteen, and Penny, nine. She's engaged to Chuck and is preparing a speech for the upcoming bachelor's graduation party. While searching for her birth certificate in her parents' bedroom, Joan discovers papers that prove her sister Gale was adopted when she was a baby. At Gale's eighteen-year-old birthday party, Joan teaches him the truth about his origins. It's a shock to Gale. She asks to see her birth mother, and after a brief period of rebellion against her adoptive family, she comes to her senses and is reunited with her family.

anchor_text: An American family is on vacation in the Alps when, while having lunch on a restaurant terrace, an avalanche threatens to engulf them. The mother instinctively tries to protect her children while the father equally instinctively runs away, thinking only of himself. After the danger has passed, the family dynamic will never be the same.
text_a: After believing they are about to be killed by an avalanche during a family ski vacation in the Alps, a married couple is thrown into disarray when they are forced to reassess their lives and how they feel about each other.
text_b: After years of marriage, Pete is the only man in the household, surrounded by his wife, Debbie, and their daughters, Charlotte and Sadie, aged 8 and 13. As his indie label flies, he and Debbie must find ways to let go and enjoy the rest of their lives... before they kill each other.

anchor_text: Billie and Pete want to spend a relaxing winter holiday in the Alps with their children, enjoying the snowy winter landscape. As the family sits on the terrace of a ski lodge for lunch, an artificial avalanche is set off, seemingly out of control. In the face of the looming avalanche, Pete leaves his family behind and instead brings only himself and his smartphone to safety, putting the relationship between him and his wife to the test.
text_a: After years of marriage, Pete is the only man in the household, surrounded by his wife, Debbie, and their daughters, Charlotte and Sadie, aged 8 and 13. As his indie label flies, he and Debbie must find ways to let go and enjoy the rest of their lives... before they kill each other.
text_b: An American family is on vacation in the Alps when, while having lunch on a restaurant terrace, an avalanche threatens to engulf them. The mother instinctively tries to protect her children while the father equally instinctively runs away, thinking only of himself. After the danger has passed, the family dynamic will never be the same.

anchor_text: To earn some money, the penniless John Smith attempts to enter an Elvis impersonation contest. A short time later, he is hunted by several killers who demand a stolen Indian mask back from him. The mask has a high ideological value to the natives, and a million-dollar finder's fee is on the line. In a showdown in an abandoned house, John Smith declares the mask destroyed, but collects the finder's fee. Secretly, however, he gives the mask to an Indian bus driver.
text_a: "John Smith" arrives at a casino run by the Chief. To earn some money, he participates in an Elvis lookalike contest. After a night of poker with the other Sosis, he is accused of stealing the tribal mask of the prosperity-bearing Chief. Chased by the Chief and his gang, the Ranger and his gang, and two crooked cops, he tries to track down the other Elvises, convinced that one of them has the mask. He gets help from his neighbor and has to deal with a blonde who shoots anything that moves. In the end, the survivors find themselves in an old farmhouse where a couple had been massacred decades ago. And that explains it.
text_b: Jim's the best shot in the whole West. On the dead body of his last victim, he vows never to touch a weapon again and to live a peaceful life. But when a band of bandits invades Sanderson, holding the city breath and terrorizing peaceful citizens, he is forced to renege on his oath. While no one else dares to resist, a masked man keeps appearing, making life difficult for the lawless. In a robbery of a gold shipment and a brawl with the sheriff guarding it, the masked man intervenes again. Behind him is Jim, the man who can end the bandits' reign of terror.

anchor_text: To earn some money, the penniless John Smith attempts to enter an Elvis impersonation contest. A short time later, he is hunted by several killers who demand a stolen Indian mask back from him. The mask has a high ideological value to the natives, and a million-dollar finder's fee is on the line. In a showdown in an abandoned house, John Smith declares the mask destroyed, but collects the finder's fee. Secretly, however, he gives the mask to an Indian bus driver.

In a robbery of a gold shipment and a brawl with the sheriff guarding it, the masked man intervenes again. Behind him is Jim, the man who can end the bandits' reign of terror.
text_b: An Apache mask disappears from where it was stored. It blames the seemingly innocent John Smith, who, the night before the disappearance, had participated in an Elvis impersonator contest. Thus begins John's long and farcical search for the real culprit that will force him to constantly defend himself from attacks by numerous local villains.

anchor_text: The son of a good family, young Butler is sent to camp in the mountains by his father, who thinks this will harden him. But the luxurious conditions of the camp were hardly conducive to this. Butler falls in love with a young mountain girl, whose family does not appreciate the young man's fragile and idle pace. His butler, having discovered from a newspaper article that Butler had the same name as a famous boxer, proposed to his master to pretend to be the champion, in order to be accepted by the rustic mountaineers. The ruse works, and he can marry her. Trapped, he must be confused, go to the next championship venue and pretend to train. After a duel with his namesake, the boxing champion, he is forced to go all the way and face a formidable opponent in the ring. Playing all for all, he decides to take the gamble. Lucky for him, Butler the true champion will enter the ring at the last moment. A final reckoning with the latter will allow him to show that his training has not been in vain! He can thus come out of this adventure with his head held high, thus regaining all credit with his young bride.
text_a: The film is based on the historical records of the priest Asser, who was Alfred's teacher, friend and companion. The young Prince Alfred is to be ordained a priest, but this does not happen as his elder brother Æthelred, King of Wessex, asks him for help against the Danish invaders. Alfred leads a successful battle and is once again a prince at court, but still wants to become a priest. When his brother arranges a meeting with the King of Mercia, Alfred meets his daughter Aelhswith, an old childhood sweetheart, and falls in love with her again. Driven by lust, he renounces chastity and marries Aelhswith. Shortly afterwards, the king dies and Alfred becomes his successor, but the Viking raids shake his kingship, personal cruelty against subordinate princes and also his wife lead to alienation, so that he loses the trust of his people. After heavy military defeats, he goes underground. There he becomes human again and tries to drive the Danes from England, whose king has also fallen in love with Aelhswith.
text_b: The film is based on a stage play called Battling Buttler and performed by Charlie Ruggles at the Selwyn Theatre and then the Times Square Theatre for the Broadway theatre from 1923 reaching 313 performances. Alfred, the son of a millionaire, is sent by his father to the mountains, on a "forced" vacation with his butler, to gain strength and break away from an extremely comfortable life: he falls in love with a girl and wants to marry her, but her parents want her to have an athletic and strong man by her side. The butler thus decides to pass off master Alfred as the boxer Battling Butler, his namesake. Alfred is involved in a series of misunderstandings and paradoxes, but the ruse holds, until the real boxer comes to town and the two must challenge each other.

anchor_text: Spoiled millionaire Alfred Butler's son is sent by his father to camp so he can finally become a real man. But Alfred has everything he needs to make life as pleasant as possible in the wild, and his butler treats him with the utmost care. On an unsuccessful hunting trip, the clumsy Alfred meets a pretty girl, who shortly afterwards rescues him from a fishing accident. He falls head over heels in love with her, and the very next day, he sends a proposal of marriage to the girl's family. But the father refuses to give his daughter in marriage to a softened man. A newspaper article about a boxer named "Battling Butler" saves Alfred's plans: he claims to be that boxer. They're getting married the next day. But now Alfred's troubles really begin, for he must leave as soon as possible, as the battle between the "Battling Butler" and the "Alabama Murderer" is imminent. Alfred and his butler are watching the fight on location. They hope that "Battling Butler" loses the fight and ends his career, but in the end he wins by knockout. Shortly thereafter, Alfred meets the boxer. When he learns of Alfred's deception, he urges him to fight back against the "Alabama Murderer". Thus the frail Alfred actually becomes a boxer against his will and is trained for the fight. In the end, it turns out he was set up. Battling Butler wasn't going to let him go up against the Alabama Murderer. Instead, it's a duel in the dressing room. Alfred eventually emerges victorious through his ability to overcome himself.

After a duel with his namesake, the boxing champion, he is forced to go all the way and face a formidable opponent in the ring. Playing all for all, he decides to take the gamble. Lucky for him, Butler the true champion will enter the ring at the last moment. A final reckoning with the latter will allow him to show that his training has not been in vain! He can thus come out of this adventure with his head held high, thus regaining all credit with his young bride.

anchor_text: Julius Kelp is a shy chemistry professor, physically handicapped but with brilliant intellectual abilities. After an argument with one of his students, he finds himself stuck on a shelf in his classroom closet. Rescued by Stella, a student he is secretly in love with, he attempts to acquire a more advantageous physique by taking bodybuilding classes. Unfortunately, success is not at the rendezvous. So he decides to use his knowledge of chemistry to invent an elixir that will transform him into a handsome man. He succeeds beyond all hope and morphs into Mister Love, a seductive but self-centered, cynical and macho playboy.
text_a: Julius Kelp (Jerry Lewis) is a professor of chemistry at the university. In love with a student and fed up with being abused by his more muscular students, he decides to enroll in a gym to improve his poor physical appearance. Seeing that he does not get what he wants he decides to use his knowledge of chemistry to create a concoction that will allow him to be more muscular. The concoction works and every time he drinks it he becomes "Buddy Love", a handsome young man in love with his own appearance.
text_b: Sherman Klump is a severely overweight but popular professor. One day, he meets Professor Carla Purty and decides to lose weight. At the same time, he is tasked with developing a tool to support weight loss. His Dean Richmond wants to see results to get a grant from entrepreneur Harlan Hartley. Klump is testing the drug on himself. The drug turns Klump into Buddy Love, a slender but arrogant man. Carla meets him in this form, unaware that he is the transformed Lump. At first, she's fascinated by him. When Buddy Love ends up in bed with three women, Carla gets mad. Klump publicly reveals himself to be Buddy Love and returns to his former form. Hartley's enthusiastic and promises to fund it. Carla comforts Sherman in the final scene, saying Buddy Love is too skinny for her.

anchor_text: The eccentric, clumsy and rather unattractive chemistry professor Julius Kelp is not taken seriously either by his students or by his colleagues. Since he's secretly in love with his student Stella Purdy, he's doing everything he can to impress her. Since most efforts in the athletic field are unsuccessful, he tries to achieve the desired effect with the help of chemistry, and accidentally discovers a means by which he can transform himself into the attractive but also cocky, arrogant macho Buddy Love. Buddy Love becomes the beloved and revered centerpiece of the campus, creating a sensation with his vocal performances at the nightclub. However, the transforming agent has only limited effect, so Kelp is forced into a double life, which is complicated by the fact that Stella has fallen in love with Buddy Love. At the same time, however, she is disgusted by Buddy's grandiose appearance and finds confidence in the more sensitive Professor Kelp. As the drug's duration of action gets shorter and shorter, it's inevitable that Buddy will have to reveal his true colors. Closing scene: Kelp and Stella become a couple. For special occasions, however, Stella has winked her eye and secured a few bottles of the transforming agent.
text_a: Sherman Klump is a severely overweight but popular professor. One day, he meets Professor Carla Purty and decides to lose weight. At the same time, he is tasked with developing a tool to support weight loss. His Dean Richmond wants to see results to get a grant from entrepreneur Harlan Hartley. Klump is testing the drug on himself. The drug turns Klump into Buddy Love, a slender but arrogant man. Carla meets him in this form, unaware that he is the transformed Lump.
